meta.method: rubric + AI-assisted (Claude), grounded in deterministic metrics; includes field missing-topics and emerging-trends analysis

b-a-in-biology-330.name: B.A. in Biology

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 8-term plan is well-ordered: a biology spine (BIOL 200 Concepts -> BIOL 201/202 Cell & Molecular and BIOL 205/206 Ecology & Evolution -> upper electives such as BIOL 222 Evolution and BIOL 352 Genetics), a chemistry track (CHEM 125/126 -> 243/244 Organic -> CHEM 473 Biochemistry), and algebra-based physics (PHYS 102/103) all run in parallel and support each other. Progression is smooth (deepestPrereqChain=5). The main softness is the number of generic 'Biology Elective'/'Cluster A/C Elective'/'Technical Elective' placeholders in years 3-4, which leave the upper-level path loosely specified.
dimensions.suggestion: Convert the repeated 'Biology Elective' and cluster placeholders into named example pathways (e.g., organismal, cell/molecular, ecology tracks) so students can see coherent upper-level routes.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy for a B.A.: deepestPrereqChain=5 is appropriate, and the 8 dangling references (missingCoursesSample: BIOL 202, BIOL 206, CHEM 125A, CHEM 126A, GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 102A, PHYS 103A) are all co-requisite lab components and general-education placeholders rather than genuine prerequisite breaks in the biology sequence.
dimensions.suggestion: Add catalog stubs mapping the co-requisite labs (BIOL 202/206, CHEM 125A/126A, PHYS 102A/103A) to their lecture courses so no referenced code dangles.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the data; only the term plan and per-course descriptions are present. For a B.A. degree page this absence is a real finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit program learning outcomes describing what a biology B.A. graduate can analyze, experiment with, and communicate.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: statedTotalCredits=120, summedTermCredits=120, creditReconcileDelta=0. The distribution is a defensible liberal-arts split, with the science core front-loaded and generous free/technical electives and humanities GERs later, consistent with a B.A. rather than a B.S.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a brief credit-summary table (core vs. GER vs. elective totals) so the balance that already reconciles is visible at a glance.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled by year and semester and credits total to 120. Readability is only mildly undercut by null-code alternative rows ('or CHEM 121 or Fundamentals of Chemical Principles I') and several generic elective placeholders that read as blanks.
dimensions.suggestion: Render course alternatives as a single 'CHEM 125 or CHEM 121' line and give each elective placeholder an example course so the plan has no visually empty rows.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The core is a sound classical biology education (evolution, genetics, physiology, ecology) with CHEM 473 Biochemistry and BNFO 135 Programming for Bioinformatics providing molecular and computational entry points. Currency gaps are that genetics (BIOL 352) and molecular content sit in the elective pool rather than the required core, quantitative training is only MATH 105 Elementary Probability and Statistics, and there is no required microbiology or genomics.
dimensions.suggestion: Require a molecular-biology/genetics course and strengthen quantitative training beyond MATH 105 so graduates leave with the data and genomics literacy expected in modern biology.

descriptionQuality.justification: Course descriptions are present and substantive across the spine (BIOL 200, BIOL 201, BIOL 205, CHEM 243/244, CHEM 473 Biochemistry), with only isolated gaps such as BIOL 352 Genetics carrying an empty excerpt.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with explicit grade conditions ('with a grade of C or better'), but clarity is reduced by pervasive legacy dual-numbering (e.g., 'BIOL 201 and BIOL 202 or R120 201 and R120 202'), which forces readers to reconcile two course-code systems, and by the dangling co-requisite labs.

missingTopics.rationale: Genetics (BIOL 352) and biochemistry (CHEM 473) exist but genetics sits among electives; a modern biology B.A. should require molecular/genomic content in the core.
Quantitative preparation is limited to MATH 105 Elementary Probability and Statistics, insufficient for reading and analyzing contemporary biological data.
No microbiology course appears in the required plan even though it is foundational to health, ecology, and biotechnology tracks students may pursue.
BNFO 135 Programming for Bioinformatics is a single intro; there is no follow-on course applying computation to sequence or 'omics data.

emergingTrends.rationale: Genetics is taught (BIOL 352) but the plan predates the routine use of genome editing and sequencing; a genomics elective would modernize the molecular track.
Building on BNFO 135, a biological-data-analysis course would give B.A. students the computational fluency now expected across the life sciences.
The ecology/evolution foundation (BIOL 205/222) positions the program to add a disease-ecology offering connecting environmental change to human and animal health.
With biochemistry (CHEM 473) and programming (BNFO 135) already present, a systems/synthetic-biology elective would connect the molecular and computational strands.

topSuggestions: Publish program-level learning outcomes; none currently appear on the page.
Require a molecular-biology/genetics course and strengthen quantitative training beyond MATH 105 to close the clearest currency gaps.
Convert the generic 'Biology Elective'/cluster placeholders into named example pathways so the upper-level route is legible.
Add a genomics/bioinformatics follow-on to BNFO 135 to give students computational-biology depth.
Add catalog stubs for the co-requisite labs (BIOL 202/206, CHEM 125A/126A, PHYS 102A/103A) so referenced codes resolve.

b-a-in-biology-physician-assistant-348.name: B.A. in Biology/Physician Assistant

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 6-term plan is purpose-built and coherent for pre-Physician-Assistant preparation: the biology foundations (BIOL 200 -> BIOL 201/202 and BIOL 205/206) feed directly into the clinical-prep sequence PA programs require, with BIOL 340 Mammalian Physiology, BIOL 421 Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy, R120 335 General Microbiology, CHEM 473 Biochemistry, and PSY 210 Introduction to Psychology all placed deliberately, and the chemistry/physics support courses ordered underneath. The compression into three years is aggressive but the sequencing itself is logical.
dimensions.suggestion: Flag the front-loaded depth (e.g., BIOL 340 Mammalian Physiology in the second year) with an advising note so students understand the accelerated pacing before they commit.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sound: deepestPrereqChain=7 reflects genuine build-up (foundations -> physiology/anatomy -> advanced courses), which is expected in a compressed accelerated track, and the 8 dangling references (BIOL 202, BIOL 206, CHEM 125A, CHEM 126A, GER 300, HUM 099, PHYS 102A, PHYS 103A) are co-requisite labs, a developmental writing placeholder, and GER placeholders rather than real breaks.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the HUM 099 and co-requisite-lab references so the accelerated chain has no dangling nodes that could confuse tight scheduling.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives appear in the data, which is a notable omission for a selective accelerated professional pathway where the competencies leading into PA study should be explicit.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish learning outcomes tied to PA-school readiness (clinical science foundations, quantitative literacy, and professional competencies).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (statedTotalCredits=102, summedTermCredits=102, creditReconcileDelta=0). The 102-credit total is a deliberate feature of the accelerated combined structure (undergraduate portion feeding the PA program) rather than a shortfall, and the distribution is appropriately science-dense with limited free electives.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a one-line note that 102 credits reflect the undergraduate portion of the combined B.A./PA pathway so the below-120 total is not misread as incomplete.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are labeled by year and semester and credits total to 102. Clarity is only lightly reduced by course rows that reference legacy codes without a resolvable code ('R120 335 General Microbiology', 'R120 360 Biochemistry') and by null-code 'or' alternative rows.
dimensions.suggestion: Give the R120-referenced requirements (General Microbiology, Molecular Biology/Biochemistry) resolvable current course codes so every plan row links to a real catalog entry.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum is well aligned with current clinical-science preparation: BIOL 340 Mammalian Physiology, BIOL 421 Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy, R120 335 General Microbiology, BIOL 350 Immunology (available), CHEM 473 Biochemistry, and PSY 210 Introduction to Psychology cover the physiology/anatomy/behavioral base PA programs expect. Gaps relative to modern clinical practice are limited quantitative training (only MATH 105) and no explicit epidemiology, pharmacology, or healthcare-ethics content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an epidemiology/biostatistics course and a healthcare-ethics or professionalism component to match competencies emphasized in current PA education.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are substantive across the clinical-prep spine (BIOL 340 Mammalian Physiology, BIOL 421 Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy, BIOL 350 Immunology, CHEM 473 Biochemistry), with only isolated gaps such as the empty excerpt for BIOL 352 Genetics.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites carry explicit grade conditions, but clarity is reduced by legacy dual-numbering ('BIOL 201 and BIOL 202 or R120 201 and R120 202') and by plan rows that reference R120 courses (R120 335, R120 360) without a resolvable current code, which is risky in a tightly sequenced accelerated program.

missingTopics.rationale: Quantitative preparation is limited to MATH 105 Elementary Probability and Statistics; PA study and evidence-based practice require stronger epidemiology/statistics.
The plan builds strong normal-physiology coverage (BIOL 340) but no pharmacology or systematic pathophysiology course prepares students for clinical reasoning.
A selective clinical pathway should include explicit ethics/professionalism content; none appears alongside the science core.
With immunology (BIOL 350) and microbiology (R120 335) present, a population-health or global-health course would round out the clinical foundation.

emergingTrends.trend: Health informatics and electronic health records
emergingTrends.rationale: BNFO 135 programming is already required; extending it toward clinical data and EHR literacy matches how modern PAs practice.
Clinical delivery is shifting to remote and digital modalities, which the current physiology/anatomy-focused plan does not address.
PA competencies increasingly emphasize team-based practice, a theme absent from the individual-course clinical foundation.
Strengthening statistics/epidemiology beyond MATH 105 would prepare students for the evidence-based, data-heavy clinical environment they are entering.

topSuggestions: Publish learning outcomes tied to PA-school readiness; none currently appear.
Add epidemiology/biostatistics beyond MATH 105 and introduce pharmacology/pathophysiology to align with current PA competencies.
Give the R120-referenced plan rows (General Microbiology, Biochemistry/Molecular Biology) resolvable current course codes.
Add a note clarifying that the 102-credit total reflects the undergraduate portion of the combined accelerated pathway.
Add a healthcare-ethics/professionalism component to complement the strong clinical-science core.

b-a-in-computer-science-226.name: B.A. in Computer Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 8-term plan follows a clean CS spine (CS 100 -> CS 113 -> CS 114 -> CS 241/CS 280 -> CS 288/CS 331/CS 332 -> CS 350/CS 356 -> CS 435/CS 490 -> CS 491 Senior Project) with prerequisite depths rising smoothly from 0 to 6 and no abrupt difficulty jumps. As a B.A. it deliberately trades depth for breadth, so the upper-level path leans on repeated generic placeholders ('CS Elective 300 or above', 'CS/IS/IT Elective 200 or above') and omits the theory-II and data-science courses that the B.S. requires.
dimensions.suggestion: Replace the repeated generic upper-level elective placeholders with 2-3 named recommended tracks (e.g., applied AI, security, systems) so B.A. students see coherent pathways instead of open slots.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is well-shaped with a deepest chain of 6 (CS 100 -> 113 -> 114 -> 241 -> CS 435 -> CS 490 -> CS 491), reasonable for a four-year CS degree, and the core shows no over- or under-constraining. All 5 dangling references (missingCourseCount 5: GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) are GER placeholders, a lab, and one-credit seminars rather than genuine CS prerequisite breaks.
dimensions.suggestion: Add catalog entries or explicit code mappings for YWCC 207, YWCC 307 and PHYS 111A, and resolve the GER 200/GER 300 placeholders so no referenced code dangles.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only the term-by-term plan of study and per-course descriptions are present. For a catalog page this absence is a real finding, and it is especially costly for a B.A. whose value proposition (breadth plus a second field) needs an explicit statement.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit set of program learning outcomes describing what a B.A. graduate can design, analyze, and communicate, and how the breadth electives are meant to be used.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: summedTermCredits 120 equals statedTotalCredits 120 with creditReconcileDelta 0.0, and terms are evenly loaded at 14-16 credits. The mix transparently spans a CS core, a lighter math/science block (MATH 111/112/333, no required linear algebra), GER, and multiple free electives appropriate to a B.A.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short credit-summary breakdown (major core vs. GER vs. free/CS electives) so the B.A.'s heavier elective share is stated explicitly rather than only implied by the term plan.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled by year and semester and required courses are legible, but the plan carries parsing artifacts that hurt clarity: a stray cross-program line ('228 B.S. in Computer Science') appears mid-plan in Year 4, a null-code FYS seminar row shows 0 credits, and the COM 312 requirement is split across an 'or COM 313 or Technical Writing' fragment row.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the plan-of-study rendering so alternative courses ('COM 312 or COM 313') are single legible entries and remove the cross-program artifact line ('228 B.S. in Computer Science').

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The required core covers durable, employable fundamentals (databases CS 331, operating systems CS 332, networks CS 356, systems CS 350, Linux tooling CS 288, advanced algorithms CS 435, software engineering CS 490, team Senior Project CS 491), but unlike the B.S. this B.A. requires no data science (CS 301), no cybersecurity (CS 351), and no AI/ML, leaving every modern high-demand area to unspecified electives; CS 288 also still foregrounds a dated PHP/MySQL stack.
dimensions.suggestion: Require at least one modern applied course (intro AI/ML, data science, or security) in the B.A. core and refresh CS 288's stack emphasis away from PHP toward current cloud/data tooling.

descriptionQuality.justification: Every sampled course has a present, specific description with real technical substance (e.g., CS 241 on algorithm design/proof and complexity, CS 356 on layered architectures and routing, CS 435 on graphs and hash tables); only the MATH 111/112 entries are terse topic lists rather than full descriptions.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with explicit grade conditions and sensible OR-alternatives (e.g., CS 280 'CS 114 or CS 116 or IT 114 or equivalent with a grade C or better'), and the core chain is internally consistent. Clarity is dinged only by inconsistent phrasing ('grade C or better' vs 'grade of C or higher') and prerequisites that point at dangling GER placeholders.

missingTopics.rationale: A 2026 CS degree should give every graduate baseline ML exposure, but this B.A. requires none (CS 301 Introduction to Data Science is required only in the B.S.); add a required or clearly recommended intro-AI/ML course.

The B.A. omits CS 351 Introduction to Cybersecurity that the B.S. requires, leaving graduates without any guaranteed secure-coding or threat-modeling grounding; make CS 351 a required or default elective.

CS 332 (OS) and CS 356 (networks) cover single-machine and network fundamentals but nothing on cloud deployment, containers, or distributed computing that dominate current practice; add a cloud/distributed-systems elective.

The B.A. requires only MATH 333, omitting the MATH 337 Linear Algebra the B.S. requires, which is foundational for graphics, ML, and data work; recommend or require linear algebra for students eyeing those tracks.

CS 490 is the sole SWE course and CS 288 still centers PHP/MySQL, so version control, testing/CI-CD, and API design are underweighted; modernize CS 288 or add a practical software-engineering elective.

emergingTrends.rationale: Employers now expect graduates to build with and reason about LLM tooling; CS 490 and the CS 491 Senior Project are natural places to require an AI-assisted development component.
Data and model pipelines are among the fastest-growing CS roles, yet this B.A. has no required data course; a data-engineering elective would let breadth-oriented B.A. students target this market.
Secure defaults and privacy-by-design are becoming baseline professional expectations, reinforcing the case for embedding CS 351 content rather than leaving security optional.
The existing IS 350 (Computers, Society and Ethics) is a strong hook to extend into algorithmic accountability and AI governance, differentiating the B.A.'s breadth angle.
Industry has shifted to containerized, serverless deployment; positioning an elective here would keep the B.A. current without adding heavy theory load.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes for the B.A., which are entirely absent from the catalog data.
Add at least one required modern applied course (intro AI/ML, data science, or cybersecurity) so the B.A. does not leave every high-demand area to unspecified electives.
Replace the repeated generic elective placeholders with named recommended tracks (applied AI, security, systems) to give the upper-level path coherence.
Modernize CS 288's dated PHP/MySQL emphasis toward current cloud and data tooling.
Clean plan-of-study parsing artifacts (the stray '228 B.S. in Computer Science' line and split 'or COM 313' rows) and resolve the 5 dangling references (YWCC 207/307, PHYS 111A, GER 200/300).

b-a-in-digital-design-199.name: B.A. in Digital Design

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The plan progresses logically from Art & Design foundations (AD 150 Color and Composition, AD 111/112 traditional and digital media, AD 161->162 history) into the core Digital Design studio ladder DD 263 -> 264 -> 363 -> 364 and on to the AD 463 Collaborative Design Studio capstone (deepestPrereqChain 5). Specialization courses (DD 275 History of Games, DD 301 Acting for Animators, DD 303/403 Sound and Music, DD 334 Simulated Environments, DD 443/444 Character Design, IT 265 Game Architecture) slot in at sensible points. The main gap is that only 7 requirement groups parsed, so the Fourth-Year second semester is missing from the visible progression.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the missing Fourth-Year second-semester term so the studio sequence resolves cleanly to graduation and the capstone's placement is unambiguous.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is well developed and mostly clean (deepestPrereqChain 5), with the DD studio chain properly gated (DD 264 requires AD 111, AD 112, AD 150 and DD 263; DD 364 requires ARCH 382, DD 275, DD 363, IT 201). missingCourseCount is low at 6, and the danglers are cross-listed or placeholder items: ARCH 251/282/334, FA 264, GER 300, and the PHYS 102A lab. Some prerequisite strings are, however, convoluted (DD 275's 'AD 111, AD 112 and AD 162 or ARCH 163, ARCH 263 and ARCH 251').
dimensions.suggestion: Simplify and parenthesize the tangled prerequisite strings (notably DD 275) and register PHYS 102A and the cross-listed ARCH/FA course codes so the 6 dangling references resolve.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the data; only the plan of study and course list are present. For a design B.A. spanning games, animation, motion, and interactive media, an explicit outcomes statement is needed to define the shared competency across those tracks.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 program learning outcomes covering visual design fundamentals, digital 2D/3D production, interactive/motion media, and collaborative studio practice.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: creditReconcileDelta is null because statedTotalCredits is null, so the plan cannot be reconciled to a target; this is a data-consistency caveat to note rather than a defect. The summed 106 credits across the 7 parsed terms sit in a reasonable 14-17 credits-per-term band, but with the total unstated and one term missing, the gen-ed/major/elective balance cannot be fully verified.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the program's total required credits (e.g., 120-124) and the missing term so the plan reconciles and the general-education vs. studio-core vs. elective split is transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The seven parsed terms are labeled with per-term credits and the studio spine is legible, but the page carries extraction artifacts: multiple 'or ...' alternatives appear as separate credit-less rows (MATH 107 'or MATH 113', CS 100 'or CS 115', PSY 210 'or R830 101', IT 265 'or IT 266', DD 443 'or DD 444'), the Fourth-Year second semester is absent, and no program description or total-credit figure is shown.
dimensions.suggestion: Fold each 'or' alternative into its parent course row, add the missing term and a program overview, and state the total credits so the page reads as a complete plan.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The course set is strong for games, animation, and motion media (DD 263-364 studios, DD 275 History of Games, DD 443/444 character design, DD 303/403 sound, IT 265 Game Architecture, AD 201 Human Factors). Currency gaps stand out, though: DD 334 Simulated Environments is described as 'desktop, non-immersive virtual reality,' which is dated; there is no named real-time game engine (Unity/Unreal), no dedicated UX/UI or interaction-design course, and no coverage of generative-AI tooling now central to digital media.
dimensions.suggestion: Modernize DD 334 toward real-time engines and immersive XR, and add explicit UX/UI/interaction-design and generative-AI-tooling courses.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are generally rich and specific, naming concrete media and methods (DD 264 covers motion graphics and time-based media; DD 443 covers character sheets and creature design for 2D games; DD 334 describes non-immersive VR for representing architecture). Many carry useful 'Restrictions: For Digital Design majors only' registration notes. Only excerpts are truncated; few read as thin placeholders.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Most prerequisites are explicit and sensible (DD 264 <- AD 111/112/150 and DD 263; AD 463 <- (DD 364 or ID 364 or INT 364 or ARCH 364) and PHYS 102). Clarity suffers from a few genuinely tangled, unparenthesized strings such as DD 275's 'AD 111, AD 112 and AD 162 or ARCH 163, ARCH 263 and ARCH 251', where the intended logic is ambiguous.

missingTopics.rationale: The program teaches game architecture (IT 265) and 3D studios but never names a real-time engine; contemporary games/interactive work is engine-centric and this is the largest practical gap.
AD 201 Human Factors and IT 201 Information Design touch usability, but no course covers interface/interaction design, a core employment path for digital-design graduates.
Character work (DD 443/444) and 3D studios exist, yet there is no technical-art course on shaders, lighting, or real-time materials that bridges art and engine pipelines.
IT 201 Information Design is the only web-adjacent course; interactive media careers expect front-end/creative-coding skills beyond it.

missingTopics.topic: Motion capture and character animation pipeline
missingTopics.rationale: DD 301 Acting for Animators and DD 444 3D character development imply performance-driven animation but no course covers the mocap-to-rig production pipeline.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI for image, video, and 3D
emergingTrends.rationale: Generative tools are rapidly reshaping concept art, texturing, and animation; the studio and character courses (DD 363/364, DD 443/444) are natural places to integrate them.
Engine-based virtual production is now standard in film and games; modernizing DD 334 toward real-time pipelines would align the program with industry.
DD 334's 'non-immersive, desktop VR' framing is dated; immersive XR is a major growth area the simulated-environments course should be updated to cover.
Procedural and data-driven asset creation is expanding in games and motion media, a skill the current 3D studios could incorporate.
Interactive installations and web experiences are a leading destination for digital designers, arguing for a dedicated creative-coding/interaction course.

topSuggestions: Add a real-time game-engine course (Unity/Unreal) and modernize DD 334 from desktop non-immersive VR toward immersive XR and real-time pipelines.
Add a dedicated UX/UI and interaction-design course to serve the interactive-media career path the current plan underserves.
Publish explicit program learning outcomes and the program's total required credits so the plan reconciles (statedTotalCredits is currently null).
Integrate generative-AI tooling into the studio and character courses (DD 363/364, DD 443/444) to keep production skills current.
Clean the plan-of-study parse: fold the 'or' alternatives (CS 100/CS 115, MATH 107/113, PSY 210/R830 101, DD 443/444) into their parent rows and add the missing Fourth-Year second semester.

b-a-in-history-385.name: B.A. in History

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: A capstone spine exists and is well-ordered (ENGL 101/102 -> distribution across American and Global/Comparative History -> R510 315/316 Perspectives in History -> HSS 404 Senior Seminar -> HIST 490 Research Seminar), and the plan sensibly reflects NJIT's identity by including CS 100/103 and MATH 105. However, the middle two years are dominated by generic 'Free Elective' and un-coded 'American/Global History Elective' rows (only 7 courses are actually referenced), so the major's substantive progression is thin between the intro and capstone bookends.
dimensions.suggestion: Name the American, Global/Comparative, and upper-level history electives (or provide representative menus) and reduce the very large free-elective block so the middle years show real disciplinary progression.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: missingCourseCount=0 (no dangling references) and deepestPrereqChain=5 is appropriate for a humanities B.A. The capstone chain is notably well-articulated: HIST 490 requires ENGL 102, a 200-level GER, an upper-level history course, Perspectives (R510 315/316), and HSS 404 -- a genuine scaffold rather than a nominal prerequisite.
dimensions.suggestion: Because so much of the major is expressed as placeholders, ensure the named 'American'/'Global' electives inherit clear, consistent prerequisites when they are added so the clean graph is preserved.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data; only the term plan and a handful of course descriptions are present. For a B.A. whose value proposition is research, analysis, and writing, stated outcomes are an expected catalog element.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit history-major outcomes (historical argumentation, primary-source analysis, historiographical reasoning, research writing) on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles (statedTotalCredits=120, summedTermCredits=120, creditReconcileDelta=0.0) and terms are transparent, but the distribution is heavily skewed toward free electives -- roughly half the degree is generic 'Free Elective' rows -- leaving the required history core comparatively light for a major.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebalance toward a larger named history core (period/region distribution plus methods) so the major's identity is not carried mostly by free electives.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are labeled and the totals reconcile, but the page leans on generic placeholders and Rutgers-legacy 'R510' rows with null codes (e.g., 'R510 201 Hist Of West Civ', 'or CS 103 or...'), which read as raw catalog fragments rather than finished entries.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the 'R510' legacy codes into current course numbers and render 'or' alternatives as clean choice groups so the plan is fully legible.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The plan makes a smart nod to NJIT's technical setting (CS 100/103, MATH 105 statistics) and provides real research training (HSS 404, HIST 490), which supports analytical careers. But it shows no digital-humanities, public-history, or applied-methods course that would let history majors leverage that quantitative/computing exposure, so currency is latent rather than realized.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a digital-humanities or public-history course that connects the required CS 100 and MATH 105 skills to historical practice, giving graduates a demonstrable applied edge.

descriptionQuality.justification: Among the courses actually coded, descriptions are substantive and specific (HIST 490 Research Seminar and HSS 404 Senior Seminar describe their scaffolded research work in detail); coverage is thin only because most of the major is expressed as placeholders.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are unambiguous and well-specified -- HIST 490's multi-course chain is a model of explicit sequencing -- and missingCourseCount=0 means no dangling references.

missingTopics.topic: Digital humanities and computational text analysis
missingTopics.rationale: The plan requires CS 100 Roadmap to Computing but never connects it to history via a digital-history/text-mining course, wasting a natural NJIT advantage.
R510 315/316 Perspectives in History is the lone methods offering; a stronger historiography/theory requirement would deepen disciplinary training.

missingTopics.topic: Public history, archives, and museum studies
missingTopics.rationale: There is no applied-history course preparing students for archives, museums, or public-history careers, a common destination for the major.
MATH 105 statistics is required but not applied to history; spatial/quantitative history methods (mapping, datasets) are absent.
The plan uses generic 'Global or Comparative History Elective' placeholders with no named world-history courses, leaving geographic breadth unspecified.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital humanities and AI-assisted text mining
emergingTrends.rationale: Pairing the required CS 100 with a digital-history course would let majors apply LLM/text-analysis tools to primary sources -- a distinctive, employable skill.

emergingTrends.trend: Public and applied history / cultural-heritage careers
emergingTrends.rationale: Growing demand in archives, museums, and heritage management makes an applied-history offering timely.

emergingTrends.trend: Data storytelling and GIS mapping for historians
emergingTrends.rationale: Spatial and data-visualization methods are increasingly standard in historical scholarship and public engagement.

emergingTrends.trend: History of science and technology as a signature track
emergingTrends.rationale: At a technological university, a formal history-of-science-and-technology pathway would differentiate the major and fit its home institution.
The field's shift toward transnational framing argues for named world-history courses in place of generic global-elective placeholders.

topSuggestions: Name the American/Global/upper-level history electives and enlarge the required history core so the middle years show real progression rather than free electives.
Add a digital-humanities or public-history course that leverages the already-required CS 100 and MATH 105 skills.
Publish explicit program learning outcomes for the history major.
Resolve the Rutgers-legacy 'R510' codes and 'or' rows into clean, current catalog entries.
Introduce named global/non-Western and historiography courses to guarantee breadth and methods depth.

b-a-in-information-systems-249.name: B.A. in Information Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The B.A. keeps the same well-ordered IS spine as its B.S. siblings (CS 100 -> IS 117 -> IS 218 -> IS 331; IS 265 -> IS 344 -> IS 465; IS 247/IS 375 UX; IS 390 requirements analysis; IS 455 IS management) converging on an IS 491/IT 491 capstone, with a lighter quantitative load (MATH 138, MATH 105) befitting a liberal-arts degree. Progression is logical, but the plan is very elective-driven -- 'IS Career Track Electives 1-3', 'New Media or Business Specialization Electives 1-5', and multiple general electives -- so the required technical arc is comparatively thin and coherence depends on students choosing a coherent track.
dimensions.suggestion: Define named, pre-packaged tracks (e.g., New Media vs. Business) with a fixed course sequence rather than a menu of interchangeable 'Specialization Elective' slots, so each path reads as a coherent progression.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph mirrors the department's healthy IS structure: deepestPrereqChain is 7 (IS 465 Advanced IS as terminal), with foundational courses shallow and no over-constraining. missingCourseCount is 3 and all are peripheral -- GER 200 is a general-education placeholder and YWCC 207/YWCC 307 are the one-credit professional-development seminars present in the plan but absent from the catalog.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the three dangling references by cataloguing YWCC 207/YWCC 307 and re-tagging GER 200 as a requirement category.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes, objectives, or overview appear in the data. Because the B.A. differentiates itself from the B.S. mainly through breadth and track flexibility, the absence of an explicit outcomes statement makes it especially hard for a student to understand what distinguishes this degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish learning outcomes that articulate the B.A.'s emphasis (IS analysis/design plus a humanities/new-media or business specialization) and a short overview clarifying how it differs from the B.S.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles cleanly (statedTotalCredits 120, summedTermCredits 120, creditReconcileDelta 0.0), which is a real strength. Transparency, however, is undercut by the sheer number of unspecified slots -- five 'Specialization Electives', three 'IS Career Track Electives', and several general electives -- meaning a large share of the 120 credits is not visibly defined, which is characteristic of a B.A. but reduces at-a-glance clarity.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a compact credit-distribution summary (gen-ed vs. IS core vs. track electives vs. free electives) and cap/label the elective menus so the balance between required and open credit is transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The structure is intact and terms are labeled, but footnote superscripts bleed heavily into titles ('General Elective 1 3,7', 'IS Career Track Elective 1 4', 'New Media or Business Specialization Elective 1 5', 'MATH 138 | General Calculus I 1'), GER rows are split with null credits, and duplicated 'Select one of the following:' lines and 'or COM 313 or Technical Writing' options render as unresolved rows -- collectively noisy even though nothing is truncated.
dimensions.suggestion: Strip the footnote reference numbers out of the title text (render them as footnotes) and collapse the 'Select one of the following' / 'or ...' groupings into single readable choice lines.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The IS core is current -- database design (IS 331), UX (IS 247/IS 375), requirements analysis (IS 390), IS management (IS 455/IS 465), networking (IT 120) -- and the career-track flexibility is a genuine plus. But as a B.A. the technical depth is modest: analytics is limited to what IS 465 touches, there is no security/governance course, no cloud/SaaS exposure, and the only programming is web, so graduates may need to supplement with data or security skills employers now expect.
dimensions.suggestion: Steer at least one required 'IS Career Track Elective' toward a modern data-analytics or information-security course so every B.A. graduate leaves with one concrete, in-demand technical competency beyond web development.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 25 sampled courses carry present, specific descriptions with no missing/placeholder entries (e.g., IS 218 web-based information systems, IS 331 databases for decision-making, IS 465 data analysis/statistics from an IS perspective, IS 350 computing ethics). Descriptions are informative rather than thin, though some registrar titles are abbreviated.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with explicit grade gates and coherent alternatives (IS 390: 'CS 103, CS 113, CS 115, IS 218 or IT 202'; IS 455: '(IS 265 or MIS 245) and IS 390'; COM 312/313 with ENGL 102 + GER conditions), and intro courses correctly show none. Minor issues -- GER-set placeholders embedded in IS 350's prerequisite and slight phrasing variance -- keep it from a 5.

missingTopics.rationale: Analytics is confined to IS 465; even a B.A. IS graduate is expected to query and visualize data, so a hands-on analytics course should anchor at least one career track.
No security or data-governance course exists despite IS 350 raising ethics/privacy conceptually; compliance and infosec literacy are baseline for IS roles.
The technical stack is web + networking (IT 120) with no cloud exposure, yet modern IS work is overwhelmingly delivered on cloud/SaaS ecosystems.
IE 492 is generic management; a dedicated Agile/PM course would connect IS 390 requirements work to how IS projects are actually run.
IS 455 treats processes conceptually; hands-on ERP or workflow-automation experience would make the business specialization concrete.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI and low-code application development
emergingTrends.rationale: IS professionals increasingly assemble solutions with AI copilots and low-code tools; a B.A. track could adopt this quickly to add applied value on top of IS 218/IS 344.
Configuration and integration of SaaS platforms is now core IS work and a natural modernization of IS 344/IS 455.

emergingTrends.trend: Self-service business intelligence and data storytelling
emergingTrends.rationale: Data-driven decision-making with self-service BI fits the B.A.'s communication strengths (COM 312/313) and would differentiate its graduates.
Organizations are automating processes IS 455 studies; RPA/process-mining exposure would make the business track current.
Extending IS 350 ethics into concrete governance/compliance aligns with a fast-growing, communication-heavy IS career path suited to a B.A.

topSuggestions: Publish program-level learning outcomes and an overview that clarifies how the B.A. differs from the B.S. in Information Systems.
Convert the loose 'Specialization Elective' menus into named, fixed tracks (New Media / Business) so each path is a coherent sequence.
Guarantee one modern technical competency per graduate by steering a career-track elective into applied analytics or information security.
Add cloud/SaaS and Agile-project-management options to close the most visible currency gaps.
Clean presentation: remove footnote numbers from titles and collapse split GER and 'or ...' rows into single lines.

b-a-in-interior-design-204.name: B.A. in Interior Design

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The interior-design course catalog is coherent and professionally structured: a studio ladder INT 263 -> 264 -> 363 -> 364 -> 464 (deepestPrereqChain 5) runs alongside Building and Interior Systems I->II (INT 221/222), Structural Principles (INT 223), Methods and Materials (INT 321), Contract Documents (INT 322), and History of Furniture (INT 350). A real coherence problem, however, is that the parsed plan of study populates the studio slots with the Industrial Design studios (ID 263/264/363/364/464) rather than these INT studios, and the First Year is missing entirely, so the plan as shown does not match the interior-design course spine.
dimensions.suggestion: Correct the plan of study to reference the INT studio sequence (INT 263-464) rather than the ID studios, and add the missing First Year so the interior-design progression is accurate and complete.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy: missingCourseCount is only 2 (GER 300 and the PHYS 102A lab), and the INT chains are sensible (INT 264 <- AD 150, INT 263; INT 364 <- INT 221, INT 222, INT 321, INT 363; INT 464 <- ARCH 282, ARCH 337, INT 321, INT 322, INT 364). The deepest chain of 5 is reasonable. One data flaw: INT 263's description carries a copy-paste restriction 'For Industrial Design majors only.'
dimensions.suggestion: Register PHYS 102A and GER 300, and fix the INT 263 course note that erroneously restricts an interior-design studio to Industrial Design majors.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the data. Interior design is a professionally accredited field (CIDA/NCIDQ), so the absence of stated outcomes around code-compliant, human-centered interior environments is a notable catalog gap.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 program learning outcomes aligned to professional interior-design competencies (space planning, codes and life safety, materials and finishes, building systems, professional practice).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: creditReconcileDelta is -31 (summedTermCredits 89 vs. stated 120), consistent with the First Year (~31 credits) being absent from the parsed plan; this is largely a data-completeness caveat rather than a genuine imbalance. Across the six parsed terms (Years 2-4) the load is steady at 14-17 credits, but with the First Year missing and several credit-less GER rows, the full gen-ed/core split cannot be verified.
dimensions.suggestion: Add the First-Year terms so the plan reconciles to 120 credits and assign explicit credits to the null GER rows so the distribution is transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Years 2-4 are labeled with per-term credits, but the page has several clarity problems: the First Year is missing, the studio rows reference ID studios instead of the program's own INT studios, GER rows appear with null credits (Science GER, Social Sciences GER, senior-seminar GER), and 'or R830 101' alternatives appear as separate credit-less rows. No program description is present.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebuild the plan with the correct INT studios and the First Year, fold the 'or' alternatives into their parent rows, credit the null GER items, and add a program overview.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum is strongly aligned with professional interior-design practice: Building and Interior Systems I-II (INT 221/222) cover HVAC/lighting/acoustics, INT 321 addresses code requirements and life safety, INT 322 Contract Documents covers professional documentation, ID 341 adds sustainable materials, and ARCH 337 Building Information Modeling is a prerequisite of the INT 464 capstone. Currency gaps are targeted rather than broad: no dedicated lighting-design course, no explicit universal/inclusive-design or accessibility course, and no wellness-certification (WELL) content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a dedicated lighting-design course and an accessibility/universal-design offering, and introduce WELL/wellness-standard content to match current practice.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are detailed and profession-specific (INT 221/222 explain large- and small-scale interior building systems; INT 321 covers code requirements, life safety, and specification; INT 350 surveys furniture history and its social/design forces; INT 464 describes a comprehensive multi-level institutional studio). None read as thin; the main flaw is a mis-copied restriction line in INT 263, not a lack of content.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Most prerequisites are explicit and well-formed (INT 464 <- ARCH 282, ARCH 337, INT 321, INT 322, INT 364; INT 264 <- AD 150, INT 263). Clarity is reduced by a couple of ambiguous multi-operator strings (INT 321's 'AD 111, AD 112, AD 150 or ARCH 334, AD 161, AD 162 and ARCH 251') and by the plan-of-study/course mismatch where ID studios stand in for the INT studios the prerequisites actually reference.

missingTopics.rationale: Building Systems (INT 221/222) touch systems broadly, but interior design requires a dedicated lighting course (daylighting, layered electric lighting, controls) that the plan does not isolate.
INT 321 covers code requirements in part, but a standalone building-codes and ADA/universal-design course is expected for NCIDQ-track interior programs.
Comfort and acoustic performance are core to interior environments yet are not addressed as a distinct topic beyond the general systems courses.
ARCH 337 Building Information Modeling is a prerequisite of INT 464 but appears only as a gateway; an interiors-focused BIM/documentation course would make digital delivery an explicit competency.

missingTopics.topic: Furniture, fixtures, and equipment (FF&E) specification and estimating
missingTopics.rationale: INT 321 Methods and Materials and INT 350 History of Furniture provide context, but a dedicated FF&E specification/estimating course reflects a central professional deliverable.

emergingTrends.rationale: Health-focused interior standards (WELL) and biophilic design are reshaping the field; the human-factors (AD 201) and systems courses are natural anchors to add this content.
The INT 364 studio already spans healthcare and institutional types, positioning the program to formalize evidence-based design methods.
ID 341 Sustainable Materials is a foothold; interiors practice increasingly requires certification literacy and circular material strategies.
With ARCH 337 BIM already gating the capstone, the program is well placed to make model-based interior documentation a through-line.

emergingTrends.trend: Adaptive reuse and flexible/hybrid workplace design
emergingTrends.rationale: Post-pandemic reprogramming of offices and adaptive reuse are major market drivers the commercial-focused INT 364/464 studios could target explicitly.

topSuggestions: Correct the plan of study to reference the program's own INT studio sequence (INT 263-464) instead of the Industrial Design studios, and add the missing First Year so the plan reconciles to 120 credits.
Publish explicit, accreditation-aligned program learning outcomes (none are currently present).
Add a dedicated lighting-design course and a codes/life-safety/accessibility (ADA/universal design) course to meet professional-track expectations.
Introduce WELL/wellness and sustainability-certification content, building on ID 341 and AD 201, to match current interior-design practice.
Fix the data flaws: the INT 263 note that wrongly restricts the studio to Industrial Design majors, the null-credit GER rows, and the 2 dangling references (GER 300, PHYS 102A).

b-a-in-law-technology-and-culture-387.name: B.A. in Law, Technology and Culture

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No plan-of-study terms are parsed (requirementGroups=0) even though this is a full B.A., so progression cannot be judged from a term sequence. The visible course cluster is, however, thematically tight and well-chosen for the major: a law-and-society history core (HIST 342 Civil Rights, 361 Founding, 362 Sex/Gender/Law, 364 American Law in the World, 369 Law and Society), a legal-reasoning skills course (STS 300 Legal Reasoning, Writing, and Technology), a research-methods course (HIST 395), and a doctrinal anchor (MGMT 290 Business Law I).
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the actual plan of study (the sibling Patent Law concentration has one) so the term-over-term progression from foundations to capstone is visible, not just a thematic course list.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: missingCourseCount=0 (no dangling references) and a shallow deepestPrereqChain=4 is appropriate for a humanities/law major; the law-and-society courses share a clean, uniform prerequisite (ENGL 102 plus a 200-level History/Humanities GER), which is coherent and accessible.
dimensions.suggestion: When the full plan is published, ensure the capstone (HSS 404/HIST 490) sits behind the STS 300 and HIST 395 skills courses so the light chain still scaffolds the research sequence.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data; only a course cluster with descriptions is provided. For an interdisciplinary law/technology/culture degree, explicit outcomes are important to show how the three strands integrate.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes describing what a graduate can do across law, technology, and culture (legal reasoning and writing, analysis of technology's social/legal impact, and research).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits=120 is asserted, but with no parsed terms (summedTermCredits=null, creditReconcileDelta=null) the distribution cannot be verified and no gen-ed/core/elective split is visible. For a degree page this is a genuine gap, not merely a minor-style data caveat.
dimensions.suggestion: Render the full term-by-term plan so the 120 credits reconcile and the balance across gen-ed, legal/STS core, technology, and electives is transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: For a full B.A., the page shows only an eight-course list with no plan of study, no term labels, and no credit accounting, so it reads as incomplete next to peer degree pages (including its own Patent Law concentration).
dimensions.suggestion: Add the complete plan-of-study rendering (terms, credits, required vs. elective) so the degree is presented as thoroughly as the concentration variant.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Conceptually the major is well-aimed at law-and-technology careers, and STS 300 Legal Reasoning, Writing, and Technology plus MGMT 290 Business Law give real professional grounding. But the eight visible courses are history/law heavy and the 'Technology' strand is under-represented -- privacy, cyberlaw, IP, and AI-governance courses (e.g., IT 331 Privacy and IT, IT 400 IT and the Law) that exist in the catalog do not surface here.
dimensions.suggestion: Surface concrete technology-law courses (privacy/IT 331, IT and the Law/IT 400, IP) in the core so the 'Technology' strand is as visible as the 'Law' and 'Culture' strands.

descriptionQuality.justification: The visible course descriptions are substantive and specific (HIST 364 American Law in the World, HIST 369 Law and Society, STS 300 Legal Reasoning), clearly conveying each course's scope.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are unambiguous and uniform (ENGL 102 plus a 200-level GER) with grade conditions, and missingCourseCount=0 means there are no dangling references in the cluster.

missingTopics.rationale: The base major shows MGMT 290 Business Law but no IP survey; HIST 384 Invention and Regulation exists in the catalog and would strengthen the technology-law core.

Catalog courses IT 331 Privacy and Information Technology and IT 400 Information Technology and the Law are not surfaced in this major, leaving data/cyber law -- central to 'law and technology' -- underweighted.

missingTopics.topic: AI and algorithmic law and ethics
missingTopics.rationale: There is no course on AI governance, automated decision-making, or algorithmic accountability, now a defining area of technology law.
STS 300 covers legal reasoning, but a broader technology-policy/regulation course is missing for a degree explicitly about law and technology.
HIST 395 Research Methods provides qualitative grounding, but there is no data/empirical-legal-analysis course connecting the major to legal analytics.

emergingTrends.rationale: A 'Law, Technology and Culture' degree is ideally positioned to own AI-governance content as regulation (e.g., the EU AI Act) matures.
GDPR/CCPA-style regimes and platform governance make privacy law a high-demand strand; integrating IT 331 would capture it.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital IP and generative-AI copyright/patent questions
emergingTrends.rationale: Generative AI is reshaping copyright and patent doctrine, a natural fit for the major's technology-law core.
Rising cyber-incident regulation and e-discovery create demand the major could serve by adding cyberlaw content.
Legal-tech and empirical legal studies are growing; a data-oriented methods option would modernize HIST 395's research training.

topSuggestions: Publish the full plan of study (terms, credits, required vs. elective) so the degree reconciles to its stated 120 credits and reads as completely as the Patent Law concentration.
Strengthen the 'Technology' strand by surfacing concrete technology-law courses (IP/HIST 384, privacy/IT 331, IT and the Law/IT 400) in the core.
Add AI-governance/algorithmic-accountability content to position the degree for the defining area of contemporary technology law.
Publish explicit program learning outcomes showing how law, technology, and culture integrate.
Add an empirical/computational legal-studies option to connect the major to legal analytics.

b-a-in-law-technology-and-culture-patent.name: B.A. in Law, Technology and Culture (Patent Law Concentration)

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: Unlike the base major, this concentration has a full 8-term plan that reconciles, and its design is genuinely purposeful for patent-law preparation: a technical spine (MATH 111 Calculus plus repeated Natural Science and Natural-Science-with-Lab requirements, which map to the USPTO patent-bar technical-competency requirement) is layered with a legal core (MGMT 290 Business Law, Legal Foundations and LTC Core electives), and a professional capstone (HSS 404 Senior Seminar, HIST 310 Co-op or HIST 490 Research, HIST 312 Professional Development in Law). The science-for-patent-eligibility logic is a real coherence strength.
dimensions.suggestion: Reduce the reliance on generic 'Legal Foundations/LTC Core/Law-Related Elective' placeholders by naming representative courses so the distinctive science-plus-law progression is explicit rather than implied.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles and the graph is nearly clean: deepestPrereqChain=5 is reasonable, and missingCourseCount=2 involves only a GER 200 placeholder and HIST 312 Professional Development in Law (a real one-credit course that dangles as a reference). The capstone options (HIST 310/490) carry sensible ENGL 102 + 200-level GER prerequisites.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a catalog entry/code mapping for HIST 312 and resolve the GER 200 placeholder so both dangling references clear.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data; only the term plan and course descriptions are present. A patent-track concentration especially should state the technical-plus-legal competencies it builds toward (e.g., readiness for the USPTO patent bar).
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes tying the science requirements to patent-practice readiness (technical literacy, patent-eligibility analysis, legal writing, professional development).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles cleanly (statedTotalCredits=120, summedTermCredits=120, creditReconcileDelta=0.0) with a transparent and deliberate split: gen-ed and a real science/math block (Calculus plus multiple natural-science requirements), a legal core, and electives. The balance directly serves the concentration's purpose of technical eligibility for patent practice.
dimensions.suggestion: Where natural-science and 'Free Elective 2' rows carry null credits, assign explicit values so every line item -- not just term subtotals -- is accounted for.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The full plan is rendered with labeled terms that reconcile, which is a clear improvement over the base major. Presentation is undercut by rough edges: 'Legal Foundations Elective 1' appears in both second-year terms with the same '1' label, HIST 310/312 titles carry trailing footnote digits ('...History I 4', 'Senior Seminar 3'), and HIST 312 dangles.
dimensions.suggestion: Fix the duplicated elective numbering and strip the trailing footnote digits from course titles so the plan reads cleanly.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The concentration is well-aligned with a real and growing career path: patent agents/examiners and patent attorneys need documented technical coursework, and the plan's Calculus-plus-natural-science spine paired with legal foundations and a co-op (HIST 310) is exactly what that pathway requires. The main currency gap is the absence of a named patent/IP course in the visible plan, so the technical eligibility is built but the doctrinal IP content is implied through generic electives.
dimensions.suggestion: Require an explicit patent/IP course (e.g., HIST 384 Invention and Regulation) and encourage engineering/CS technical electives so the concentration delivers named IP doctrine alongside its science spine.

descriptionQuality.justification: The coded courses have substantive descriptions (HIST 310 Co-op, HIST 490 Research Seminar, HSS 404 Senior Seminar, MGMT 290 Business Law); the one gap is HIST 312 Professional Development in Law, which dangles and shows no description.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly with grade conditions, and the only dangling references (missingCourseCount=2) are a GER 200 placeholder and HIST 312, so the capstone chain (ENGL 102 -> GER 200 -> upper-level -> HSS 404 -> HIST 490) is unambiguous.

missingTopics.rationale: The concentration is defined largely by science requirements; no dedicated patent or IP course is visible even though HIST 384 Invention and Regulation exists and would supply the doctrinal core.

missingTopics.topic: Patent drafting, claims, and USPTO practice
missingTopics.rationale: There is no course teaching patent-agent skills (claim drafting, prosecution, USPTO procedure), which are the concrete deliverables of a patent-law track.
USPTO patent-bar eligibility rewards substantial engineering/science coursework; the plan provides Calculus and generic natural-science electives but no engineering/CS depth pathway.
Modern patent eligibility turns on Alice/Mayo (software) and biotech questions; no course addresses these decisive doctrines.
The plan lacks a technology-transfer/licensing course connecting patents to startups and commercialization careers.

emergingTrends.rationale: Questions like inventorship for AI (the DABUS cases) are reshaping patent law and are a natural signature topic for this concentration.
The frontier of patentable subject matter sits in biotech and software; pairing the science spine with these doctrines would be distinctive.

emergingTrends.trend: Global IP harmonization and standard-essential patents
emergingTrends.rationale: Cross-border patent strategy and SEP/FRAND disputes are growing areas the track could address.

emergingTrends.trend: AI-assisted prior-art search and patent analytics
emergingTrends.rationale: Patent search and drafting are being transformed by AI tooling; exposure would modernize the professional-development component (HIST 312).
University and startup IP commercialization is an expanding career destination the co-op (HIST 310) could be oriented toward.

topSuggestions: Require a named patent/IP course (e.g., HIST 384 Invention and Regulation) so the concentration delivers explicit IP doctrine, not just technical eligibility.
Add a defined engineering/CS technical-elective pathway to strengthen USPTO patent-bar readiness beyond Calculus and generic science.
Resolve the HIST 312 dangling reference and the GER 200 placeholder, and assign credits to the null-credit science/elective rows.
Publish program learning outcomes that tie the science requirements to patent-practice readiness.
Clean the plan's presentation (duplicated 'Elective 1' labels, trailing footnote digits in course titles) and name the generic legal-core electives.

b-s-in-architecture-167.name: B.S. in Architecture

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The first two years mirror the professional B.Arch and are well-ordered (studios ARCH 195 -> 196 -> 295 -> 296, Construction I/II ARCH 223/224, History I/II ARCH 210/211), but coherence weakens sharply in the upper years: Studio V (ARCH 395) is the last required studio and years three and four dissolve into undifferentiated 'ARCH Elective' and 'Free Elective' rows with no required Structures, ECS, or professional-practice core and no culminating studio.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a required upper-level integrative studio and pull core technical courses (Structures, ECS) into the required sequence rather than leaving the entire junior/senior experience elective-driven.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is internally consistent but shallow (deepestPrereqChain 4), ending at Studio V (ARCH 395) because the deeper studio spine is not required here. All 3 dangling references (missingCoursesSample: GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 102A) are gen-ed placeholders and a physics-lab co-requisite, not genuine architecture breaks.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the PHYS 102A lab and GER 200/300 placeholder references, and if deeper studios are intended, restore the prerequisite chain into the upper years.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the provided data; only the plan of study and course descriptions are present. This absence is a real catalog finding, and it matters more here because a pre-professional degree must clearly signal how it differs from the accredited B.Arch.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit program learning outcomes that distinguish this non-licensure pre-professional B.S. from the B.Arch (e.g., breadth, path to an accredited M.Arch).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (summedTermCredits 120 = statedTotalCredits 120, creditReconcileDelta 0.0) and terms are evenly loaded at 12-16 credits. The distribution is transparent, but it is heavily weighted toward open electives in the upper years (multiple 'ARCH Elective' plus 'Free Elective' slots per term), which is flexible but under-structured for a design degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Cap or define the free-elective share and convert some open ARCH-elective slots into declared technology/history-theory requirements so the upper-year balance is intentional.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The eight-term plan is clean, with labeled terms and per-term credits, and the shared ARCH descriptions are mostly substantive. The main weakness is the large number of unresolved placeholder rows ('ARCH Elective', 'Free Elective', GER lines with null credits) that make the upper years read as a template rather than a plan.
dimensions.suggestion: Annotate each elective slot with its category and credit rule (e.g., '300+ ARCH Technology elective, 3 cr') so placeholder rows are self-explanatory.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: As a non-licensure pre-professional degree the flexibility is defensible, but making Structures, ECS, and Professional Practice optional means graduates can complete the major without core technical or practice currency, and the only required computing course is the dated CS 104. Digital tools (BIM ARCH 337, fabrication) and sustainability remain elective.
dimensions.suggestion: Embed a minimum required technology core (one structures, one environmental-systems, one BIM/computational course) so every graduate leaves with current technical fluency.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are specific for the foundational courses (ARCH 210/211 History, ARCH 223/224 Construction give real topic content), but the continuation studios are thin or boilerplate-contaminated (ARCH 196 'A continuation of ARCH 195. ARCH 2**...', ARCH 296, ARCH 395 'This course is a continuation of ARCH 296.'), and the MATH entries are terse topic lists.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are generally explicit, but the retained studio prereqs carry ambiguous compound logic (ARCH 395 'ARCH 224, and (ARCH 296 or ARCH 264), and ARCH 211 or (ARCH 381 and ARCH 382)' mixes precedence), and ARCH 211's 'ARCH 210 or ARCH 251 and ARCH 252' is unparenthesized. Dangling GER/PHYS references are minor.

missingTopics.topic: Required building technology core (structures and environmental systems)
missingTopics.rationale: Structures I/II (ARCH 303/304) and ECS I/II (ARCH 309/314) exist in the catalog but are not required here, so a B.S. graduate can bypass load paths and building energy entirely, a serious gap for any architecture degree.
With only the dated CS 104 required, BIM (ARCH 337) and digital fabrication (ARCH 301/335) remain optional; parametric/Grasshopper modeling that employers expect is absent from the required core.

missingTopics.topic: Sustainability and environmental performance as a required thread
missingTopics.rationale: Sustainability appears only in electives such as ARCH 571 Sustainable City; a degree in architecture should require at least one environmental-performance course given climate-driven practice.
Professional Practice I/II (ARCH 472/475) are not required in this plan, leaving graduates without exposure to project delivery, contracts, or firm operations.
No course addresses code compliance or universal design; even a non-licensure degree should introduce accessibility and life-safety principles.

emergingTrends.rationale: ML-driven form-finding and text-to-image tools are entering studio pedagogy; the Tools and Techniques sequence (ARCH 110/156) should incorporate them and displace the aging CS 104 computing requirement.
Digital electives (ARCH 301/335/337) show the capacity exists; making a parametric/BIM course required would align this flexible degree with contemporary practice.
ARCH 571 Sustainable City demonstrates faculty strength that could anchor a required environmental-performance thread for climate-era practice.
Courses like ARCH 361 Adaptive Paradigms point to responsive/smart-building interest that an elective-heavy degree could formalize as a concentration.
Landscape and Urbanism (ARCH 324) plus urban electives could extend toward GIS/data-driven site analysis, an increasingly expected skill.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program learning outcomes that clearly position this pre-professional B.S. relative to the accredited B.Arch/M.Arch path.

Introduce a required upper-level integrative studio and a required building-technology core (structures, environmental systems, BIM/computational) instead of leaving juniors and seniors on open electives.
Require at least one sustainability/environmental-performance course (e.g., building ECS or ARCH 571) and modernize the dated CS 104 toward computational design.
Convert generic 'ARCH Elective'/'Free Elective' slots into declared categories with credit rules to give the upper-year plan intentional structure.
Resolve the 3 dangling references (GER 200/300, PHYS 102A) and clean the thin/boilerplate continuation-studio descriptions.

b-s-in-architecture-and-m-b-a-in-managem.name: B.S. in Architecture and M.B.A. in Management of Technology

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The undergraduate architecture spine visible in the course list is well ordered: the studio sequence ARCH 195 -> 196 -> 295 -> 296 -> 395 carries a clean depth chain (deepestPrereqChain 4), paired with History of Architecture I->II (ARCH 210 -> 211) and Construction I->II (ARCH 223 -> 224). However, only 2 of the plan's requirement groups parsed (both Fourth Year), so term-over-term progression and, critically, the point where the M.B.A. Management-of-Technology graduate courses interleave with the B.S. are not visible; the combined-degree bridge is asserted rather than shown.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the full five-year plan of study showing exactly which term the graduate MGMT/FIN/ACCT courses begin and how they build on the undergraduate architecture core, so the B.S.-to-M.B.A. transition is explicit.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: With a deepest prerequisite chain of 4 the undergraduate graph is sensibly shallow and clean (e.g., ARCH 224 requires ARCH 223; ARCH 395 requires ARCH 224 plus a studio and history course). The high missingCourseCount of 26 is dominated by graduate M.B.A./M.O.T. courses that live outside the undergraduate catalog: missingCoursesSample lists ACCT 615, FIN 516/600/618/624, MGMT 620/625/630, HRM 601, MATH 661, and ARCH 650/651/652, plus GER 200/300 category placeholders. These are expected dangling references for a combined degree, not broken undergraduate chains.
dimensions.suggestion: Cross-link the graduate course codes (ACCT 615, FIN 600, MGMT 620, etc.) to the graduate catalog or mark them as graduate-level so they no longer register as dangling undergraduate references.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only a partial plan and a course list are present. For a combined B.S./M.B.A. this absence is especially consequential because the whole value proposition is an integrated design-plus-technology-management competency that should be stated explicitly.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 program learning outcomes that name the integrated architecture + technology-management capabilities graduates should demonstrate (design fluency, project/technology management, finance for the built environment).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: creditReconcileDelta is -93 (summedTermCredits 27 vs. statedTotalCredits 120), but this is a data-extraction caveat rather than a real imbalance: only the two Fourth-Year requirement groups were parsed, so 93 credits of earlier terms and the graduate load are simply absent from the summed figure. The visible portion is entirely ARCH/free electives and a credit-less GER placeholder, giving no view of the gen-ed/core/elective split.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore the full plan of study so the summed credits reconcile to the stated 120 (plus the graduate credits) and the general-education, architecture-core, and management split becomes transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: As parsed, the catalog page shows only the Fourth-Year first and second semesters, populated largely by generic 'ARCH Elective', 'ARCH Elective (Technology)', and 'Free Elective' rows with one credit-less 'History and Humanities GER 300+ level' item. There is no program description, no first three years, and no view of the graduate portion, so a prospective student cannot read the shape of the degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a program overview paragraph and the missing First through Third Year terms, and give the placeholder elective and GER rows explicit credit values.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The graduate M.B.A. in Management of Technology is itself a currency-relevant pairing (technology strategy, finance, operations for design professionals). The undergraduate courses shown, however, lean traditional: the computing requirement is the dated CS 104 'Computer Programming and Graphics Problems,' and nothing visible addresses building energy performance, sustainability, or real-estate/development finance that would naturally bridge architecture and the M.O.T. content.
dimensions.suggestion: Introduce a real-estate-development-and-finance elective and a building-performance/sustainability course as the explicit link between the architecture core and the Management-of-Technology graduate work.

descriptionQuality.justification: Undergraduate course descriptions are generally present and specific (ARCH 223 names wood, steel, masonry and concrete assemblies; ARCH 210 defines a Paleolithic-to-industrialization scope). Quality is uneven, though: several read as thin extraction fragments (ARCH 196 'A continuation of ARCH 195. ARCH 2**. Architecture Elective...'; ARCH 395 'This course is a continuation of ARCH 296.'), and none of the graduate M.B.A./M.O.T. courses that define this degree are described at all.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Undergraduate prerequisites are mostly stated clearly with sensible logic (ARCH 224 requires ARCH 223; ARCH 295 uses an explicit alternative-group form). Clarity is reduced by 26 dangling references (the graduate ACCT/FIN/MGMT/HRM courses and GER placeholders) and by a couple of ambiguous operator precedence strings such as ARCH 211's 'ARCH 210 or ARCH 251 and ARCH 252'.

missingTopics.topic: Building environmental systems and energy performance
missingTopics.rationale: Construction I-II (ARCH 223/224) cover materials and enclosure but no visible course addresses HVAC, lighting, or energy modeling, a core competency for any accredited architecture path.
The plan shows construction assemblies but no statics or structures course, leaving a gap between ARCH 223/224 and the technical judgment a design professional needs.

missingTopics.topic: Real estate development and project finance
missingTopics.rationale: This is the natural bridge between the architecture B.S. and the M.O.T. finance courses (FIN 600/618), yet no development-finance course links the two halves of the degree.
The only computing course is the dated CS 104; contemporary practice expects parametric modeling (Grasshopper/Rhino) and BIM well beyond ARCH 156's introductory digital tools.

missingTopics.topic: Professional practice and construction project management
missingTopics.rationale: For a management-of-technology pairing, a course on AEC project delivery, contracts, and construction management would directly operationalize the M.B.A. content in a built-environment context.

emergingTrends.rationale: Net-zero and embodied-carbon mandates are reshaping practice; the technology-management focus is an ideal frame to teach the business case for low-carbon design, which the current construction courses omit.
Generative tools are entering schematic design and optioneering; upgrading the CS 104 requirement toward design computation would keep graduates current.
Technology-driven real estate (smart buildings, digital asset management) sits squarely at the architecture + management intersection this degree targets.

emergingTrends.trend: Building lifecycle BIM and digital twins
emergingTrends.rationale: Owners increasingly demand model-based lifecycle management; pairing BIM with the M.O.T. operations content would differentiate graduates.
The M.B.A. Management-of-Technology track positions graduates to launch AEC-tech ventures, a growing niche the curriculum could name explicitly.

topSuggestions: Publish the complete five-year plan of study (First through Third Year and the graduate terms) so the page reconciles to its stated 120 undergraduate credits and shows where the M.B.A. courses begin.
Add explicit program learning outcomes for the integrated architecture-plus-technology-management competency (none are currently present).
Introduce a real-estate-development-and-finance course as the concrete bridge between the architecture core and the M.O.T. finance sequence (FIN 600/618).
Modernize the technology core: replace the dated CS 104 requirement with parametric/BIM design computation and add a building-energy-performance course.
Cross-link or relabel the 26 graduate-level dangling references (ACCT 615, FIN/MGMT/HRM 6xx, ARCH 650-652) so they resolve to the graduate catalog rather than reading as broken references.

b-s-in-architecture-and-m-s-in-civil-eng.name: B.S. in Architecture and M.S. in Civil Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
