dimensions.justification: Unlike the other combined B.S. degrees, the full eight-term plan parsed, and it is well ordered: the studio ladder ARCH 195 -> 196 -> 295 -> 296 -> 395 runs term over term (deepestPrereqChain 4) with History I->II and Construction I->II sequenced alongside, PHYS 102/102A landing in Second Year, and Years 3-4 opening into architecture electives. The one coherence concern is engineering readiness: for a program feeding an M.S. in Civil Engineering, the visible math is only MATH 107 (University Mathematics A) and MATH 105 (statistics), with no calculus, statics, or structures in the undergraduate spine.
dimensions.suggestion: Insert a calculus sequence and an introductory statics/structures course into the undergraduate terms so students enter the M.S. Civil Engineering with the expected engineering-mechanics foundation.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is clean and appropriately shallow (deepestPrereqChain 4; e.g., ARCH 296 <- ARCH 295, ARCH 395 <- ARCH 224 and a studio/history course). missingCourseCount is only 9, and the danglers are all graduate or placeholder items: ARCH 601/602/636/651/677/684 (graduate civil/architecture courses), GER 200/300 categories, and the PHYS 102A lab co-requisite. No undergraduate chain is broken.
dimensions.suggestion: Register the PHYS 102A lab and the GER 200/300 category codes, and link the graduate ARCH 6xx courses, so missingCourseCount drops toward zero.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the data despite the otherwise-complete plan. A B.S. Architecture + M.S. Civil Engineering especially needs an explicit statement of the integrated design-plus-structural-engineering competency that distinguishes it.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 program learning outcomes covering architectural design, building technology, and the structural/civil-engineering competencies the M.S. adds.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: This program reconciles exactly: creditReconcileDelta is 0.0 with summedTermCredits 120 equal to the stated 120. The visible distribution is reasonable, blending the architecture core (studios, history, construction), a science base (PHYS 102/102A), writing (ENGL 101/102), math, and GER, though Years 3 and 4 lean heavily on generic ARCH and free electives (roughly a dozen elective rows), and the final term drops to 12 credits.
dimensions.suggestion: Annotate the gen-ed/architecture-core/elective credit split and firm up the Year 3-4 elective rows (especially the required 'ARCH Elective (Technology)') so the back half of the plan is as concrete as the front half.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: This is the best-presented of the combined degrees: all eight terms are labeled with per-term credits and the plan reconciles. Readability is nonetheless reduced by credit-less GER placeholder rows (Social Sciences GER, Natural Science GER, History and Humanities GER shown with null credits), a wall of generic 'ARCH Elective' rows across Years 3-4, and the absence of any program description or view of the graduate M.S. component.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short program overview and the graduate-term outline, and assign explicit credits to the null GER rows so every term line carries a value.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The undergraduate architecture content is sound but traditional (studios, history, Construction I-II, the dated CS 104 computing course). For the civil-engineering destination the notable currency gap is engineering fundamentals: there is no statics, mechanics of materials, structural analysis, or calculus in the undergraduate plan, and no visible coverage of building performance/BIM that modern AEC integration expects.
dimensions.suggestion: Add undergraduate statics/mechanics-of-materials and a BIM/structural-modeling course so graduates carry current structural-engineering and digital-delivery skills into the M.S.

descriptionQuality.justification: Most sampled descriptions are present and specific (ARCH 223 names wood/steel/masonry/concrete assemblies; PHYS 102 states its statics-and-dynamics scope; ARCH 210 gives a clear historical range). A few are thin continuation stubs (ARCH 196 'A continuation of ARCH 195...'; ARCH 296 'A continuation ARCH 295...'; ARCH 395 'This course is a continuation of ARCH 296.'), and no graduate civil-engineering course is described.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly and consistently, with sensible grade conditions (ENGL 102 requires ENGL 100/101 with a grade of C or higher; MATH 112 requires MATH 111 with C or better) and correct empty prerequisites on intro courses. The only clarity drag is the operator ambiguity in ARCH 211's 'ARCH 210 or ARCH 251 and ARCH 252' and the handful of graduate/GER danglers.

missingTopics.rationale: A program feeding an M.S. in Civil Engineering shows Construction I-II but no statics or mechanics course, the single most important undergraduate engineering prerequisite missing from the plan.
The undergraduate math is only MATH 107 and MATH 105 (statistics); calculus and differential equations are prerequisites for graduate structural and geotechnical coursework.
No course bridges Construction II (assemblies) to structural behavior of frames and loads, a gap for the civil-engineering track.
HVAC, lighting, and energy performance are absent, yet they are central to integrated architecture-engineering practice.
The only computing course is the dated CS 104; BIM/structural-modeling skills are now standard across the AEC pipeline this degree targets.

emergingTrends.rationale: Modern civil practice is shifting to performance-based methods; the architecture-plus-civil pairing is well placed to teach integrated structural performance once statics/structures are added.
Mass timber and low-carbon concrete are transforming structures; Construction I-II could be extended toward embodied-carbon-aware structural choices.
Model-based structural analysis and digital twins are becoming standard in AEC delivery, arguing for a BIM/structural-modeling course upstream of the M.S.
Flood, seismic, and climate loads increasingly drive civil design, a growth area the combined degree could own.
Parametric and optimization-driven structural design is rising in practice and would modernize the dated CS 104 computing requirement.

topSuggestions: Add the engineering foundation the civil-engineering destination requires: a calculus sequence plus statics/mechanics of materials and an intro structural-analysis course in the undergraduate terms.
Publish explicit program learning outcomes for the integrated architecture-plus-civil-engineering competency (none are currently present).
Modernize the computing/technology requirement from the dated CS 104 toward BIM and structural digital modeling.
Firm up and annotate the Year 3-4 elective load (a dozen generic 'ARCH Elective' rows) and assign credits to the null GER rows so the balanced 120-credit plan reads clearly.
Register the PHYS 102A lab and GER 200/300 categories and link the graduate ARCH 6xx courses so the 9 dangling references resolve.

b-s-in-architecture-and-m-s-in-managemen.name: B.S. in Architecture and M.S. in Management

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The undergraduate B.S. architecture base is coherent through the mid-program (studios ARCH 195 -> 196 -> 295 -> 296 -> 395, Construction I/II, History I/II), but like the standalone B.S. the upper years dissolve into 'ARCH Elective'/'Free Elective' rows, and the graduate M.S. Management block is not shown. The combined-degree progression therefore cannot be read end to end.
dimensions.suggestion: Present the integrated B.S.-plus-M.S. timeline and give the upper-year electives declared categories so the path into the management year is explicit.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The architecture prerequisite pool is sensible (deepestPrereqChain 6), but this program carries the largest dangling set of the group: 30 references (missingCoursesSample includes ACCT 615, ARCH 647/650/651/652/675, CE 200A/501/615/631/642/702/711, ENE 662, FIN 516). These are graduate management and civil-engineering courses outside the undergraduate catalog, so the high count is a combined-degree data caveat, but the CE-heavy sample looks mismatched for an M.S. in Management and needs verification.
dimensions.suggestion: Cross-link the graduate references to the graduate catalog, verify why civil-engineering (CE 5xx-7xx) courses appear in a management combined degree, and resolve GER placeholders.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data; only the plan and course descriptions are present. For a design-plus-management dual credential this is a real gap because the differentiator is the fusion of design and management competencies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish combined learning outcomes tying the B.S. architecture competencies to the M.S. Management competencies (organizational, financial, operational).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The metrics show summedTermCredits 120 against statedTotalCredits 31, a creditReconcileDelta of +89.0. The stated 31 evidently captures only the graduate M.S. Management overlay, so the large delta is a combined-degree data-consistency caveat rather than a defect, but the page neither reconciles nor shows the full combined total.
dimensions.suggestion: State the full combined total and the B.S.-vs-M.S. credit split so the 31-credit overlay is contextualized and the plan reconciles.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The eight-term B.S. plan is legible with labeled terms and per-term credits, but it leans on many placeholder elective rows, the graduate management year is not depicted, and 30 dangling references (including seemingly mismatched CE courses) make the overall page read as unresolved.
dimensions.suggestion: Depict the graduate management terms, label each elective slot with its category/credit rule, and resolve or verify the dangling CE/management references.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Pairing a B.S. in Architecture with an M.S. in Management is relevant to design-business and firm-operations careers, and this program's sampled electives are notably current: DD 320 Robotics for Architects and Designers, DD 321 Interactive and Reactive Environments, ARCH 571 Sustainable City, and CE 200 Surveying show emerging-technology and sustainability exposure. The main gap is that business/economics bridge content is not visible in the undergraduate portion.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a required construction-economics/business-of-design course in the undergraduate years and foreground DD 320/321 as design-technology bridges into the management curriculum.

descriptionQuality.justification: Several sampled descriptions are strong and current (DD 320 Robotics on Arduino/sensors/actuators, DD 321 Interactive and Reactive Environments, ARCH 571 Sustainable City, CE 200 Surveying with GPS/mapping content), but continuation studios (ARCH 196/296) are thin/boilerplate, AD 325 Entrepreneurship has an empty description, and ARCH 583 'ST:' is a stub.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Clarity is materially reduced by 30 dangling references to graduate management and civil-engineering courses outside the undergraduate catalog, compounded by unparenthesized compound architecture prereqs (ARCH 395, ARCH 211) and multi-condition strings in DD 320/321. The dangling count is largely a combined-degree caveat, but the mismatched CE references make the prerequisite picture hard to trust.

missingTopics.topic: Construction economics and business of design
missingTopics.rationale: The undergraduate plan lacks estimating, pro-formas, or design-firm economics, exactly the bridge a management combined degree should own before the graduate business block.
AD 325 Entrepreneurship for Designers exists but has no description and is not clearly required; a design-plus-management degree should make entrepreneurship a documented core.

missingTopics.topic: Required building-technology core (structures and environmental systems)
missingTopics.rationale: As in the standalone B.S., Structures and ECS are not required here, so graduates can bypass core technical competencies before entering management study.

missingTopics.topic: Project and operations management for design practice
missingTopics.rationale: No undergraduate course addresses scheduling, staffing, or operations, the practical management skills that connect the two degrees.

missingTopics.topic: Data analytics for design and operations
missingTopics.rationale: With DD 320/321 signaling technology interest, the plan would benefit from a data/analytics course supporting management decision-making.

emergingTrends.rationale: DD 320 Robotics for Architects and DD 321 Interactive and Reactive Environments already position the program at the design-technology frontier; formalizing them as a track would strengthen the management-of-technology angle.
AD 325 Entrepreneurship plus the management degree can prepare graduates to launch and run design-technology ventures, a growing career path.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI and computational design tooling
emergingTrends.rationale: AI-driven design and estimating are reshaping design-business practice; the studio and DD sequence are natural places to teach responsible AI adoption, replacing the aging CS 104 emphasis.

emergingTrends.trend: Sustainability economics and ESG in the built environment
emergingTrends.rationale: ARCH 571 Sustainable City provides a base to connect sustainability with the management curriculum's financial and ESG decision-making.

emergingTrends.trend: Smart, sensor-driven buildings and IoT operations
emergingTrends.rationale: DD 321 Interactive and Reactive Environments and CE 200 Surveying point toward sensor/data-rich buildings whose operation is increasingly a management discipline.

topSuggestions: State the full combined B.S. + M.S. Management credit total and split so the +89 reconciliation gap (31 stated vs. 120 summed) is contextualized and the plan reconciles.
Verify the dangling references: 30 references include civil-engineering (CE 5xx-7xx) courses that appear mismatched for an M.S. in Management and should be corrected or explained.
Publish combined learning outcomes fusing B.S. architecture and M.S. Management competencies.

Add construction-economics/business-of-design content and make AD 325 Entrepreneurship and the DD 320/321 technology electives explicit bridges into the management block; introduce a required building-technology core.
Depict the graduate management terms, resolve GER placeholders, and clean thin/empty descriptions (AD 325, ARCH 583 'ST:').

b-s-in-architecture-and-m-u-d-187.name: B.S. in Architecture and M.U.D

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The undergraduate architecture core is coherent: the studio ladder ARCH 195 -> 196 -> 295 -> 296 -> 395 (deepestPrereqChain 4) runs alongside History of Architecture I->II and Construction I->II. But as with the other combined B.S. degrees, only the two Fourth-Year requirement groups parsed, so the plan does not show how the Master of Urban Design graduate studios and seminars (ARCH 601/602/636/677/684 appear only as dangling references) build on the undergraduate base, nor is any urban-scale progression visible before the graduate stage.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the full plan showing the transition into the M.U.D. graduate studios (ARCH 601/602) and any upper-level urban/site courses, so the escalation from building scale to urban scale is legible.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The undergraduate prerequisite graph is shallow and clean (deepest chain 4; e.g., ARCH 296 requires ARCH 295, ARCH 395 requires ARCH 224). Of the 26 missing references, most are graduate M.U.D. and cross-college courses outside the undergraduate catalog: missingCoursesSample includes ARCH 601/602/636/677/684 (graduate urban-design studios/seminars), ARCH 650/651/652, ACCT 615, FIN 516/600/618, HRM 601, and GER 200/300 placeholders. These are expected combined-degree danglers rather than broken undergraduate chains.
dimensions.suggestion: Mark the ARCH 6xx and management graduate codes as graduate-catalog references (or link them) so missingCourseCount reflects only genuine undergraduate gaps.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present in the data. For a B.S. Architecture + M.U.D. the missing statement is significant because urban design carries its own competency set (urban form, public space, land use, community process) that should be articulated distinctly from the architecture outcomes.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish program learning outcomes that distinguish the architecture competencies from the urban-design competencies the M.U.D. adds (urban morphology, site and infrastructure systems, community engagement).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: creditReconcileDelta is -93 (summedTermCredits 27 vs. stated 120), which here is a parse caveat, not an imbalance: only the two Fourth-Year terms were captured, so the earlier terms and the graduate load are absent from the sum. The visible rows are all ARCH/free electives plus a credit-less GER placeholder, so the gen-ed/core/elective split cannot be judged.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore the full plan of study so credits reconcile to the stated 120 (plus graduate credits) and the distribution across general education, architecture core, and urban-design work is transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The parsed page shows only Fourth-Year semesters, filled with generic 'ARCH Elective', 'ARCH Elective (History/Theory)', and 'Free Elective' rows and one credit-less GER item; there is no program description and no view of the first three years or the graduate M.U.D. component, so the degree's structure is not readable from the page.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a program overview and the missing terms, and give the placeholder ARCH-elective and GER rows explicit credits.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The M.U.D. pairing is relevant to a field increasingly focused on cities, but the undergraduate courses shown are traditional building-scale offerings (studios, history, Construction I-II) with the dated CS 104 as the computing requirement. Nothing visible addresses GIS, urban analytics, transportation, housing, or sustainability/resilience that a contemporary urban-design education expects.
dimensions.suggestion: Introduce an urban-analytics/GIS elective and a course on sustainable, resilient urbanism as the explicit undergraduate on-ramp to the M.U.D. studios.

descriptionQuality.justification: Undergraduate descriptions are largely present and specific (ARCH 210 defines its historical scope; ARCH 223 lists structural materials and assemblies), but several are thin continuation stubs (ARCH 196 'A continuation of ARCH 195...'; ARCH 395 'This course is a continuation of ARCH 296.'), and none of the M.U.D. graduate courses that define this degree are described.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Undergraduate prerequisites are generally unambiguous and sensibly grade-gated (ARCH 224 <- ARCH 223; ENGL 102 <- ENGL 100/101 with grade conditions). Clarity is weakened by 26 dangling graduate/GER references and by operator-precedence ambiguity in strings like ARCH 211's 'ARCH 210 or ARCH 251 and ARCH 252'.

missingTopics.rationale: The undergraduate courses stop at building-scale studios; a land-use/planning-principles course would give students the zoning and policy grounding the M.U.D. studios assume.
No mapping or spatial-data course appears, yet GIS is foundational to contemporary urban design; it would extend the analytical base beyond the dated CS 104.
Urban design hinges on mobility and infrastructure networks, absent from the current studio/history/construction sequence.
Equitable housing is central to urban design practice but no course addresses housing typologies or affordability in the visible plan.
Site ecology and stormwater/green-infrastructure knowledge underpin resilient urbanism and are missing between Construction II and the graduate studios.

emergingTrends.rationale: Coastal and heat resilience is reshaping city design; the M.U.D. focus is a natural home for resilience methods the undergraduate plan does not yet seed.
Sensor data and urban analytics increasingly inform planning decisions, arguing for a GIS/urban-data course upstream of the graduate studios.
Participatory, justice-oriented practice is now central to urban design and warrants an explicit community-engagement course.
Compact, mixed-use, transit-linked urban models are a leading planning paradigm the curriculum should position graduates to work within.
Green stormwater and biodiversity strategies are becoming standard in urban projects, a growth area the current plan omits.

topSuggestions: Publish the full plan of study through the M.U.D. graduate studios so the page reconciles to its stated 120 undergraduate credits and shows the building-to-urban-scale progression.
Add explicit, urban-design-specific program learning outcomes distinct from the architecture core (none are currently present).
Introduce a GIS/urban-analytics course and an urban-planning/land-use fundamentals course as the undergraduate on-ramp to the graduate urban-design studios.
Add a resilient/sustainable urbanism course to align the degree with climate-adaptation practice.
Relabel or link the 26 graduate/GER dangling references (ARCH 601/602/636/677/684, ACCT/FIN/HRM 6xx, GER 200/300) so they no longer read as broken undergraduate references.

b-s-in-biochemistry-365.name: B.S. in Biochemistry

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 8-term plan interleaves two well-ordered spines that converge exactly where biochemistry demands. The chemistry track (CHEM 121/125 -> 122/126 -> 222 Analytical + 243/244 Organic -> 231/235 Physical Chemistry -> 480 Instrumental Analysis) and the biology track (BIOL 200 -> BIOL 201/202 -> BIOL 205/206 -> BIOL 352 Genetics -> R120 356 Molecular Biology) both feed CHEM 473/474 Biochemistry I/II and CHEM 475 Biochemistry Lab, supported by a full math/physics base (MATH 111/112/211, PHYS 111/121). Progression is smooth with no abrupt jumps (deepestPrereqChain=6).
dimensions.suggestion: Make the two-spine design explicit on the page with a short 'chemistry track / biology track -> biochemistry core' diagram so the intentional convergence is visible to students.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy: deepestPrereqChain=6 is appropriate, Physical Chemistry (CHEM 231) is correctly gated on PHYS 111 and the MATH 211 sequence, and the 10 dangling references (BIOL 202, BIOL 206, CHEM 125A, CHEM 126A, CHEM 210, GER 200, GER 300, MATH 225, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A) are co-requisite labs, the 1-credit CHEM 210 Frontiers seminar, a 1-credit stats course, and GER placeholders rather than genuine breaks.
dimensions.suggestion: Add catalog stubs for the co-requisite labs and the 1-credit CHEM 210 and MATH 225 so the biochemistry prerequisite graph resolves fully.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the data; only the term plan and course descriptions are present. For a professionally oriented B.S. this is a real finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit learning outcomes (e.g., competency in metabolic biochemistry, laboratory/instrumental technique, and interpreting molecular data).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (statedTotalCredits=120, summedTermCredits=120, creditReconcileDelta=0) and balances a heavy integrated chemistry/biology core against math/physics support and gen-ed, with a senior research course (CHEM 491). The tradeoff is that the science density leaves only about three technical electives, so student flexibility is limited.
dimensions.suggestion: Label the technical-elective slots with example biochemistry-relevant options (e.g., molecular modeling, proteomics) so the limited elective space is used deliberately.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are labeled by year and semester and credits total to 120. Readability is only slightly reduced by null-code 'or' alternative rows ('or CHEM 125 or General Chemistry I') and a plan row that references 'R120 356 Molecular Biology' without a resolvable current code.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the R120 356 Molecular Biology reference to a current code and collapse the 'or' alternatives into single lines so every plan row links to a catalog entry.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum reflects current biochemistry practice: CHEM 473/474 Biochemistry I/II with CHEM 475 lab, CHEM 480 Instrumental Analysis (MS, GC, HPLC, spectroscopy), CHEM 222 Analytical Chemistry, BIOL 352 Genetics, R120 356 Molecular Biology, EVSC 385 Environmental Microbiology, and BNFO 135 programming give strong molecular and analytical grounding. Gaps relative to the modern field are the absence of a structural-biology/biophysical-methods course and of proteomics/genomics ('omics) or computational-chemistry content beyond CHEM 336 Quantum.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a structural-biology or 'omics/computational-biochemistry elective so graduates encounter protein-structure and large-scale molecular data methods central to today's biochemistry.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are substantive and specific across the core (CHEM 473/474 Biochemistry, CHEM 480 Instrumental Analysis, CHEM 231/235 Physical Chemistry, CHEM 475 Biochemistry Lab), with only minor issues such as the empty excerpt for BIOL 352 Genetics and a truncated CHEM 336 entry.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: The chemistry prerequisites are stated clearly and consistently with grade conditions and explicit OR-alternatives (e.g., 'CHEM 122 or CHEM 126'); the only clarity drag is legacy dual-numbering on the biology side (BIOL vs. R120) and the R120 356 reference in the plan.

missingTopics.rationale: CHEM 480 Instrumental Analysis covers analytical instrumentation, but no course addresses protein structure determination (X-ray, cryo-EM, NMR), central to modern biochemistry.
BIOL 352 Genetics and R120 356 Molecular Biology are present, but there is no large-scale 'omics or bioinformatics course to analyze the molecular data biochemists now generate.
CHEM 336 Quantum Chemistry provides theory, but no applied molecular-modeling/computational course lets students simulate biomolecules.
The biochemistry sequence (CHEM 473/474) would benefit from an explicit enzymology/medicinal-chemistry elective tying mechanism to therapeutic design.

emergingTrends.rationale: Tools like AlphaFold have transformed structural biochemistry; a module building on CHEM 473/474 and BNFO 135 would keep graduates current.

emergingTrends.trend: Cryo-electron microscopy and advanced structural methods
emergingTrends.rationale: Structure determination has shifted toward cryo-EM; the strong instrumental base (CHEM 480) is a natural place to introduce it.
With BNFO 135 already required, extending toward molecular simulation and 'omics analysis matches how biochemistry research now operates.
Genetics (BIOL 352) and Molecular Biology (R120 356) position the program to add gene-editing and therapeutic-design content.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program learning outcomes; none currently appear.
Add a structural-biology or 'omics/computational-biochemistry elective to close the clearest gap with modern practice.
Resolve the R120 356 Molecular Biology reference to a current code and clean the legacy BIOL/R120 dual-numbering.
Label the limited technical-elective slots with example biochemistry-relevant options so students use them deliberately.
Add catalog stubs for the co-requisite labs, CHEM 210, and MATH 225 so the prerequisite graph resolves fully.

b-s-in-biology-333.name: B.S. in Biology

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The implicit structure defined by prerequisites is strong and deep: a foundations sequence (BIOL 200 -> BIOL 201/202 and BIOL 205/206) gates a large, well-tiered upper catalog reaching depth 7 (e.g., BIOL 448 Neuropathophysiology), so the ordering that exists is logical. However, no plan of study is parsed (requirementGroups=0), so there is no presented term-over-term sequence, no visible core-vs-elective split, and no capstone placement to evaluate; coherence must be inferred entirely from the prerequisite graph.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the four-year plan of study (required core, distribution clusters, and a capstone/research term) so the deep prerequisite structure is expressed as an ordered sequence students can follow.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: Across 77 referenced courses the prerequisite graph is rich and well-formed, with deepestPrereqChain=7 appropriate for a rigorous B.S. and only 8 dangling references (BIOL 202, BIOL 206, CHEM 125A, CHEM 126A, GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A) that are co-requisite labs and GER placeholders. The chain is coherent from BIOL 200 up through advanced courses like BIOL 448.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the co-requisite lab and GER references so the otherwise clean 77-course prerequisite graph has no dangling nodes.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives appear anywhere in the data; only per-course descriptions and the prerequisite graph are present. For a flagship B.S. this is a real catalog finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit program learning outcomes distinguishing the B.S. from the B.A. (e.g., quantitative, laboratory, and research competencies expected of a B.S. graduate).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan does not reconcile because there is nothing to reconcile: statedTotalCredits is null, summedTermCredits is null, creditReconcileDelta is null, and requirementGroups=0. Unlike the sibling B.A. (which reconciles to 120), the B.S. page provides no credit total or term distribution, so a reader cannot verify the program's size or balance.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement and a term-by-term credit distribution so the B.S. reconciles the way the B.A. already does.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page presents a large elective catalog with no four-year plan, no labeled terms, and no stated total, so despite substantive individual descriptions it reads as a course dump rather than a navigable degree. A couple of course records also carry malformed data (BIOL 347's prerequisiteRaw runs into the course description; BIOL 383 lists its prerequisites inside the description with a null prerequisite field).
dimensions.suggestion: Add a legible plan of study and clean the malformed prerequisite/description fields (BIOL 347, BIOL 383) so the page presents as a structured degree.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The elective pool is broad and notably current, spanning modern molecular and computational biology: BIOL 453 Applied Genetics & Genomics explicitly addresses the 'explosion of technological developments' in genomics, BIOL 468 Disease Ecology & Evolution connects ecology to disease dynamics, and neuroscience is deep (BIOL 315/347/441/447/448). Remaining gaps are that genomics/bioinformatics and quantitative methods are elective rather than required, and no biostatistics course is visible in the list at all.
dimensions.suggestion: Require a biostatistics/quantitative-methods course and a genomics/bioinformatics course so the modern content already present (BIOL 453) is guaranteed rather than optional.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present and substantive across the large catalog (BIOL 315 Principles of Neurobiology, BIOL 453 Applied Genetics & Genomics, BIOL 468 Disease Ecology & Evolution, BIOL 340 Mammalian Physiology), with only isolated gaps such as the empty excerpt for BIOL 352 Genetics.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites generally carry explicit grade conditions, but clarity is hurt by pervasive legacy dual-numbering ('BIOL 201 and BIOL 202 or R120 201 and R120 202') and by malformed records where prerequisite text merges into the description (BIOL 347) or the prerequisite is stated in the description while the prerequisite field is null (BIOL 383).

missingTopics.rationale: No statistics or quantitative-methods course appears anywhere in the 77-course list, a serious gap for a B.S. whose graduates must analyze experimental data.
BIOL 453 Applied Genetics & Genomics and R120 356 Molecular Biology exist, but molecular/genomic content is elective; a B.S. core should guarantee it.
General microbiology is absent from the visible list even though it underpins the disease, health, and biotechnology directions the electives point toward.
Programming appears only via BNFO 135 (referenced), with no upper-level computational-biology course to apply it to sequence or 'omics data.

emergingTrends.rationale: BIOL 453 Applied Genetics & Genomics is the natural anchor to make genome-editing and sequencing a required, not optional, part of the B.S.
Deep neuroscience and physiology electives (BIOL 315/347/441) would pair well with an explicit computational-modeling course to match how the field now operates.
Modern biology is data-intensive; adding an 'omics/data-analysis course would connect the molecular electives to current research practice.
BIOL 468 Disease Ecology & Evolution positions the program to expand One Health and infectious-disease content that is highly relevant post-pandemic.

topSuggestions: Publish the four-year plan of study and a stated credit total; the B.S. page currently has no requirement groups and no credits (unlike the B.A., which reconciles to 120).
Require a biostatistics/quantitative-methods course, which is entirely absent from the 77-course list.
Guarantee molecular biology/genomics in the core by requiring a course such as BIOL 453 Applied Genetics & Genomics rather than leaving it elective.
Publish explicit B.S. learning outcomes that distinguish it from the B.A.
Clean the malformed prerequisite/description records (BIOL 347, BIOL 383) and resolve the dangling co-requisite labs.

b-s-in-biomedical-engineering-542.name: B.S. in Biomedical Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The plan of study parses cleanly into eight labeled semesters (requirementGroups 8) with a textbook progression: a first-year math/science/design foundation (MATH 111/112, CHEM 125/126, PHYS 111/121, FED 101, BME 101 seminar), a sophomore BME-fundamentals block that partitions the field by domain (BME 301 Electrical, BME 302 Mechanical, BME 303 Biological/Chemical, BME 304 Materials Fundamentals) atop BME 111 Physiology, then junior integration (BME 382 Engineering Models of Physiological Systems, BME 333 Signals and Systems, BME 386 Biosensor and Data Acquisition Lab) and a senior applications-plus-capstone tier (BME 471 Medical Imaging, BME 372 Electronics of Medical Devices, BME 472 FDA Regulation, BME 495/496 Capstone I/II). Each year builds demonstrably on the prior one with no abrupt jumps.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an explicit lab/skills thread note so students see how BME 386 and BME 383 measurement labs chain into the BME 495/496 capstone, making the design-readiness pathway visible on the page.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is deep and coherent (deepestPrereqChain 7) with clear grade conditions throughout (e.g., BME 301 'Grade of C or higher in PHYS 121 and MATH 112'; BME 333 requires BME 301, MATH 222 and BME 210/310; BME 496 requires BME 495), producing a sensible tapered funnel into the capstone. missingCourseCount is 5, but the missingCoursesSample (CHEM 125A, GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A) is entirely co-scheduled lab sections and general-education placeholders rather than genuine dangling technical prerequisites, so the real graph is healthy.
dimensions.suggestion: Reconcile the co-listed lab codes (CHEM 125A, PHYS 111A/121A) with the undergraduate catalog so those five references resolve and the prerequisite graph shows zero dangling links.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the captured data; the page presents only the plan of study and course descriptions. For an ABET-oriented engineering degree this is a real omission, since accreditation is organized around explicit student outcomes that are not surfaced to the reader here.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 4-6 explicit program outcomes (e.g., ability to apply engineering to biological systems, design medical devices under FDA constraints, analyze physiological signals) aligned to the BME 495/496 capstone deliverables.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: summedTermCredits 120 against statedTotalCredits 120 gives creditReconcileDelta 0. The distribution is well balanced across a math/basic-science foundation, a substantial BME core, humanities/GER and HSS requirements, and roughly four engineering/science elective slots plus IE 492 Engineering Management, giving both depth and breadth within a standard 120-credit envelope.
dimensions.suggestion: Label each elective slot's credit count on the page (several currently render with a null credit value) so the 120-credit total is transparent term by term.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page is well-structured and legible: eight clearly named terms, per-term credit subtotals, and course titles for every required item. Readability is only lightly hurt by placeholder rows carrying a null code and/or null credits (e.g., 'History and Humanities GER 200 level', repeated 'Engineering Elective', 'Science or Engineering Elective'), which make a few requirements read as unfilled blanks.
dimensions.suggestion: Replace null-coded placeholder rows with a short explicit rule (e.g., 'choose one GER 200-level humanities, 3 cr') so no line renders as an empty requirement.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum reflects current biomedical-engineering practice well: BME 471 Principles of Medical Imaging, BME 372 Electronics of Medical Devices, BME 386 Biosensor and Data Acquisition, BME 333 Biomedical Signals and Systems, and notably BME 472 FDA Regulation of Medical Devices give students both technical and regulatory grounding that employers value. The main currency gaps are the near-absence of machine learning / computational analysis of biomedical data and of tissue engineering / regenerative medicine as distinct topics.
dimensions.suggestion: Modernize BME 333 and BME 471 to include machine-learning methods for biosignal and image analysis, and add a tissue-engineering or biomaterials-in-vivo elective to complement the BME 304 materials fundamentals.

descriptionQuality.justification: Every sampled course carries a present, specific description (30 of 30, none thin), and the text is substantive rather than boilerplate (e.g., BME 101 framing biomedical career paths for freshmen, and the fundamentals courses each describing their electrical/mechanical/biological/materials scope); only the excerpt length is truncated in the capture.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated unambiguously with explicit grade thresholds and sensible OR-alternatives (e.g., BME 210 'MATH 112 or MATH 238 with a grade of C or better'; BME 382 'BME 111, BME 301, BME 302 and MATH 222 all with a C or better'). The only deduction is the five dangling co-listed lab/GER references (CHEM 125A, PHYS 111A/121A, GER 200/300) that do not resolve to catalog entries.

missingTopics.topic: Machine learning for biomedical data and imaging
missingTopics.rationale: BME 333 (Signals and Systems), BME 386 (Data Acquisition) and BME 471 (Medical Imaging) build the exact signal/image pipeline that modern diagnostics run ML on, yet no course covers classification, segmentation or model evaluation.

BME 304 Material Fundamentals and BME 303 Biological/Chemical Foundations lay the groundwork, but there is no dedicated course on scaffolds, cell-material interaction or regenerative therapies, a core BME employment area.

missingTopics.topic: Computational modeling and simulation of physiological systems
missingTopics.rationale: BME 382 Engineering Models of Physiological Systems is the natural home for finite-element / lumped-parameter simulation, but the plan offers no explicit computational-methods or biomechanical-simulation course.
BME 302 Mechanical Fundamentals is foundational only; a dedicated biomechanics or human-movement/rehabilitation course would deepen a domain that is otherwise a single second-year touchpoint.
With BME 303 covering biochemical foundations, the program lacks any sequence-analysis or omics-data course, leaving graduates without exposure to the data side of modern molecular biomedicine.

emergingTrends.rationale: BME 471 and BME 333 could be repositioned around deep-learning image reconstruction and computer-aided diagnosis, the direction imaging vendors and clinical AI startups are hiring for.

emergingTrends.trend: Wearable and implantable biosensors / point-of-care devices
emergingTrends.rationale: BME 386 (Biosensor Lab) and BME 372 (Electronics of Medical Devices) already point here; adding low-power wearable-sensor and continuous-monitoring content matches the fastest-growing device segment.
BME 472 FDA Regulation is an ideal anchor to add software-as-a-medical-device, connectivity and device-security topics now central to FDA premarket review.
Building on BME 303/304, microfluidic lab-on-chip platforms are reshaping drug testing and diagnostics and would give the materials/biology thread a modern experimental outlet.
Coupling BME 382 physiological modeling with genomic/patient-specific data prepares students for the individualized-therapy and digital-twin-of-the-patient trend.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes / educational objectives (currently absent), aligned to the BME 495/496 capstone, to make the ABET-style competencies visible on the catalog page.
Introduce machine learning for biomedical signals and images, either as a new elective or woven into BME 333 and BME 471, to close the most significant currency gap.
Add a tissue-engineering / regenerative-medicine (or biomaterials-in-vivo) course to extend BME 304 beyond materials fundamentals into a major employment area.
Resolve the five dangling co-listed lab/GER references (CHEM 125A, PHYS 111A/121A, GER 200/300) so the prerequisite graph is fully connected.
Replace null-coded elective and GER placeholder rows with explicit selection rules and credit values so every term line reads as a concrete requirement.

b-s-in-business-and-information-systems-.name: B.S. in Business and Information Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The eight-term plan interleaves a coherent IS spine (CS 100 -> IS 117 -> IS 218 -> IS 331 database; IS 265 -> IS 344 -> IS 465 Advanced IS; IS 247/IS 375 UX; IS 390 requirements analysis; IS 455 IS management & business processes) with a well-sequenced business core (ECON 201, ACCT 117, FIN 218, FIN 315, MGMT 216 Business Data Analytics, MRKT 330, MGMT 391, HRM 301). Term loads are steady at 12-16 credits and culminate in an IS 491/IT 491 capstone, so difficulty ramps smoothly. The main weakness is that the two disciplines run largely in parallel with only IS 344/IS 455/MGMT 216 explicitly bridging them.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an explicit integrative course or capstone theme that forces the business and IS threads together (e.g., an analytics-for-business-decisions project spanning IS 465 and MGMT 216) so the two spines converge rather than run side by side.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sensible: deepestPrereqChain is 7 (IS 465 Advanced IS, a terminal course gated on a statistical GER plus IS 265/IS 344 and IS 331), which is appropriate depth for a senior course and not over-constraining, while foundational courses sit at shallow depths. missingCourseCount is 4 and every dangling reference is peripheral -- GER 200 and GER 300 are general-education level placeholders, and YWCC 207/YWCC 307 are the one-credit professional-development seminars that appear in the plan but are not catalogued as course entries.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the four dangling references by linking YWCC 207/YWCC 307 to their catalog entries and re-tagging GER 200/GER 300 as requirement categories rather than course codes.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: The program data contains no program-level learning outcomes, educational objectives, or program overview. A prospective student can only infer the intended competencies by reading the course list, with no explicit statement of what a Business & IS graduate will know or be able to do -- a genuine gap for a catalog page.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 program-level learning outcomes spanning IS analysis/design, database and web systems, business analytics, and professional communication, alongside a short program overview.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: Credit accounting reconciles cleanly: statedTotalCredits is 120, summedTermCredits is 120, and creditReconcileDelta is 0.0. The split is transparent and balanced across general education, a substantial business core, and the IS core, with roughly five general-elective slots providing flexibility. The only caveat is that the business load is heavy enough that quantitative/technical IS depth (programming, analytics) competes for limited room.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a one-line credit summary (gen-ed vs. business core vs. IS core vs. electives) so the ~120-credit split is visible at a glance and the business/IS balance is explicit.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled and most rows carry code, title, and credits, but several presentation defects remain: general-education rows are split and carry null credits (e.g., 'Science Literacy GER', 'History and Humanities GER 200 level'), alternative options render as separate null-code lines ('or ACCT 115 or Fundamentals of Financial Accounting', 'or COM 313 or Technical Writing'), and multiple titles are abbreviated ('Principles Of Fin Accountng', 'IS Mgmt & Business Processes', 'Computing & Effective Com').
dimensions.suggestion: Merge each split GER row and each 'or ...' alternative into a single readable line, and restore full course titles in place of the truncated registrar abbreviations.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The program reflects real business-IS practice via database design (IS 331), e-commerce technology (IT 310), business data analytics (MGMT 216), UX (IS 247/IS 375), and IS management (IS 455/IS 465). However, for a contemporary Business & IS degree the analytics footprint is thin (essentially MGMT 216 plus the statistics touched in IS 465), there is no information-security/governance course, no enterprise-systems/ERP exposure, and the only programming is web development -- leaving a graduate short of the Python/SQL analytics fluency employers now expect.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a required data-analytics course using Python/SQL and a BI visualization tool (Tableau/Power BI), and a business information-security & governance elective, to align with current analyst and IS-management hiring.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 33 sampled courses carry present, specific descriptions with no missing or placeholder entries; excerpts convey real topical content (e.g., IS 218 'critical, hands-on introduction to the design of Web-based Information Systems', IS 331 databases for analysis/decision-making, IS 465 data analysis and statistics from an IS perspective). A few registrar titles are terse, but the descriptive text itself is informative rather than thin.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with explicit grade gates ('with a grade of C or better') and sensible boolean alternatives (IS 218: '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'; IS 465 lists an explicit statistical-GER set). Intro courses correctly show no prerequisite. Minor blemishes -- GER-set placeholders inside prerequisite text (IS 350) and slight phrasing variance ('C or better' vs 'C or higher') -- keep it from a 5.

missingTopics.topic: Hands-on business/data analytics (Python, SQL, BI dashboards)
missingTopics.rationale: Analytics is limited to MGMT 216 and the statistics in IS 465; a modern Business & IS graduate needs applied Python/SQL and a dashboarding tool -- add a dedicated analytics course rather than relying on a single survey.

missingTopics.topic: Information security, risk & IT governance (GRC, SOX/GDPR)
missingTopics.rationale: No course addresses security or compliance despite IT 310 e-commerce and IS 455 business processes; business IS professionals are routinely responsible for data-protection and regulatory risk.

missingTopics.topic: Enterprise systems / ERP and process integration
missingTopics.rationale: IS 455 covers business processes conceptually but there is no hands-on ERP (SAP/Oracle) or process-integration experience that business-IS roles rely on.
IE 492 Engineering Management is generic; a dedicated Agile/PM course would match how IS projects (IS 390 requirements, IS 491 capstone) are actually delivered in industry.
Given the FIN/ACCT/MRKT reporting context, students would benefit from dimensional modeling and warehouse/BI pipeline concepts beyond the transactional focus of IS 331.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI for business productivity and analytics
emergingTrends.rationale: LLM copilots are reshaping accounting, marketing, and analyst workflows this program feeds; an 'AI for business' elective would build on MGMT 216 and IS 465.
Business systems increasingly run on Salesforce/ServiceNow/Power Platform; IS 344/IS 455 could be modernized to teach configuration and low-code app building.

emergingTrends.trend: Process mining and robotic process automation (RPA)
emergingTrends.rationale: IS 455 business-process content is a natural home for process mining and RPA, which are now central to operational-efficiency roles.
With FIN 218/FIN 315 and IT 310 e-commerce already present, the program is well positioned to add a FinTech elective covering digital payments and platform finance.

emergingTrends.trend: Data privacy and responsible data governance
emergingTrends.rationale: Building on IS 350 ethics, GDPR/CCPA-style governance is now a core expectation for anyone managing business information systems.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes and a short overview; the data currently contains none.
Add a required applied-analytics course (Python/SQL + BI dashboards) to deepen the thin analytics footprint beyond MGMT 216.
Introduce an information-security/IT-governance course and an enterprise-systems/ERP experience to close the two largest industry gaps.
Add an Agile/project-management course to support IS 390 and the IS 491 capstone with real delivery practice.
Clean the plan-of-study presentation: merge split GER and 'or ...' rows and restore truncated course titles.

b-s-in-chemical-engineering-567.name: B.S. in Chemical Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The eight parsed semesters (requirementGroups 8) follow the canonical chemical-engineering ladder with exemplary ordering: foundation (CHEM 125/126, MATH 111/112, PHYS 111/121, CS 115, CHE 101 intro) into the core unit-operations spine (CHE 201 Material and Energy Balances, CHE 230/342 Thermodynamics I/II, CHE 260 Fluid Flow, CHE 370 Heat and Mass Transfer, CHE 349 Kinetics and Reactor Design, CHE 360 Separation Processes) and culminating in CHE 489 Process Dynamics and Control, CHE 495/496 laboratories and CHE 472 Process and Plant Design. Supporting chemistry (CHEM 236 P-Chem, CHEM 243/244A Organic) and CHE 312 Process Safety are placed exactly where the discipline expects them.
dimensions.suggestion: Surface a one-line 'core spine' annotation (balances to thermo to transport to kinetics to separations to design) so prospective students can see the signature ChemE progression at a glance.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: This is the deepest and most tightly sequenced graph in the college (deepestPrereqChain 10), which is appropriate rather than excessive for ChemE's linear core: CHE 472 Plant Design correctly gates on CHE 312, CHE 349, CHE 360, CHE 365 and IE 492, and prerequisites use clear grade conditions and co-requisites (CHE 365 'CHE 370, CS 115, co-requisite CHE 360'). missingCourseCount is 7, but the missingCoursesSample (CHE 101, CHEM 125A, ENGR 211, GER 200, MATH 225, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A) is dominated by 1-credit intro/lab sections and GER placeholders, not broken technical links.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the seven dangling references, especially the low-credit core touchpoints CHE 101, ENGR 211 and MATH 225, so the deep core chain has no unmatched nodes; and verify the depth-10 path is achievable without scheduling bottlenecks.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives are present in the data; only the plan of study and course descriptions are captured. Given the strongly ABET-aligned structure (a dedicated capstone design course, process safety, and engineering ethics), the absence of published outcomes is a genuine catalog gap rather than a reflection of the curriculum.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit outcomes covering process design under safety/economic constraints, mass/energy/momentum transport analysis, and reactor/separation design, mapped to the CHE 472 capstone.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (summedTermCredits 120, statedTotalCredits 120, creditReconcileDelta 0) and is well balanced between basic science, the ChemE core, laboratory work (CHEM 339, CHE 495/496) and required professional/ethics content (IE 492, PHIL 334 Engineering Ethics, COM 313 Technical Writing). The one balance limitation is elective breadth: only two Technical Elective slots exist, leaving little room for students to specialize.
dimensions.suggestion: Expand from two to three or four technical-elective credits (or add a designated concentration track) so students can build depth in an area such as bioprocessing, energy or materials.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The catalog page is complete and highly legible: eight clearly labeled terms with per-term credit subtotals, a full course title for every item, and inclusion of professional-formation content (PHIL 334 Engineering Ethics, COM 313 Technical Writing) that many plans omit. Only a couple of GER rows carry null credit values, a very minor blemish on an otherwise clean layout.
dimensions.suggestion: Assign explicit credit values to the two GER placeholder rows (History/Humanities and the HSS Senior Seminar) so all eight terms sum visibly to 120.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum is strongly current for core process engineering: CHE 312 Chemical Process Safety, CHE 365 Chemical Engineering Computing, CHE 489 Process Dynamics and Control and CHE 472 Plant Design map directly onto practicing-engineer competencies, and MTEN 201 adds materials grounding. The main currency gaps are the absence of sustainability/decarbonization content, data-driven / machine-learning process methods, and any bioprocess-engineering exposure despite the department's biochemistry strength.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a sustainability/process-intensification or bioprocess elective and modernize CHE 365 and CHE 489 to include data-driven modeling and digital-twin/process-analytics techniques.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 33 sampled courses have present, specific descriptions with none thin: e.g., CHE 201 detailing material/energy balances and unit operations, CHE 312 covering plant-accident impact and individual/societal risk, CHE 365 on numerical methods for process simulation, and CHE 472 framing the capstone around engineering standards and public-health/safety constraints. Only excerpt length is truncated.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are clearly and consistently expressed, including co-requisites (CHE 365 'co-requisite: CHE 360') and multi-course design gates (CHE 472 lists six prerequisites), and grade conditions are stated on the science chain. The deduction is for seven dangling references (CHE 101, CHEM 125A, ENGR 211, GER 200, MATH 225, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A) that do not resolve in the catalog.

missingTopics.topic: Sustainability, decarbonization and green process design
missingTopics.rationale: CHE 472 Plant Design and CHE 360 Separations are the natural place to embed carbon capture, electrification and green-solvent selection, yet the plan has no sustainability course and only two technical electives.

missingTopics.topic: Data-driven process modeling and machine learning
missingTopics.rationale: CHE 365 Chemical Engineering Computing and CHE 489 Process Dynamics and Control already teach simulation and control, but neither covers data-driven/ML surrogate models now standard in process optimization.
The department offers biochemistry (CHEM 473 appears in the broader pool) yet the ChemE plan has no fermentation/bioreactor or downstream-bioprocessing course despite growing biomanufacturing demand.
With CHE 349 Kinetics and CHE 342 Thermodynamics II in place, an electrochemistry/battery elective would connect existing fundamentals to the energy-transition job market.
The plan emphasizes bulk process/plant design (CHE 472) but not consumer-product/formulated-product design, an area of growing ChemE employment in specialty chemicals and pharma.

emergingTrends.trend: Decarbonization, carbon capture and the hydrogen economy
emergingTrends.rationale: CHE 360 separations and CHE 472 design provide the ideal vehicles to teach CO2 capture and hydrogen/electrofuel process design, aligning the program with the energy transition.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital twins and data-driven process operations
emergingTrends.rationale: CHE 489 control and CHE 365 computing can be modernized toward real-time process analytics and digital-twin operation, a fast-growing plant-engineering practice.
Building on the department's organic/biochemistry strength, bioreactor and cell-culture process content positions graduates for the expanding biopharma sector.
Life-cycle thinking and process intensification woven into CHE 472 would reflect the shift toward circular-economy chemical production.
Coupling CHE 349 kinetics with electrochemistry supports the surge in battery, fuel-cell and electrochemical-manufacturing roles.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes / educational objectives (currently absent), mapped to the CHE 472 capstone and CHE 312 process-safety competencies.
Add a sustainability/decarbonization or bioprocess elective to close the program's biggest currency gap and reflect where ChemE hiring is moving.
Modernize CHE 365 (Computing) and CHE 489 (Control) to include data-driven/ML process modeling and digital-twin techniques.
Expand technical-elective capacity beyond the current two slots (or add a named concentration) so students can specialize in energy, materials or bioprocessing.
Resolve the seven dangling references (notably CHE 101, ENGR 211, MATH 225) so the deep depth-10 core chain is fully connected in the catalog.

b-s-in-chemistry-367.name: B.S. in Chemistry

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 8-term plan covers all five classical subdisciplines in logical order: general chemistry (CHEM 125/126) -> organic (CHEM 243/244 + 244A lab) and analytical (CHEM 222/221) -> physical chemistry (CHEM 231/235 + 339 lab) and quantum chemistry (CHEM 336) -> inorganic (CHEM 412), materials (CHEM 340), instrumental analysis (CHEM 480), and biochemistry (CHEM 473), capped by CHEM 491 research. Physical chemistry is correctly gated on the full math sequence through MATH 222 Differential Equations and PHYS 111/121, so the progression builds without gaps (deepestPrereqChain=6).
dimensions.suggestion: Group the senior technical electives into named sub-tracks (e.g., materials, analytical, biochemistry) so the strong core leads into a visible specialization.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is well-shaped: deepestPrereqChain=6 is appropriate, CHEM 231 Physical Chemistry is correctly gated on PHYS 111 plus MATH 211/213/309, and the 8 dangling references (CHEM 125A, CHEM 126A, CHEM 210, GER 200, GER 300, MATH 225, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A) are co-requisite labs, the 1-credit CHEM 210 Frontiers seminar, a 1-credit stats course, and GER placeholders rather than genuine breaks.
dimensions.suggestion: Add catalog stubs for the co-requisite labs (CHEM 125A/126A, PHYS 111A/121A) and the 1-credit CHEM 210 and MATH 225 so no referenced code dangles.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives appear in the data; only the term plan and course descriptions are present. For a B.S. that appears ACS-aligned, publishing outcomes is expected.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit learning outcomes (e.g., mastery of the five subdisciplines, laboratory/instrumental competence, and communication of chemical results), ideally mapped to ACS certification criteria.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (statedTotalCredits=120, summedTermCredits=120, creditReconcileDelta=0). The balance is strong: a dense chemistry core with full math/physics support, and a generous senior technical-elective allowance (five slots) that gives real flexibility while keeping the gen-ed footprint intact.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish suggested elective bundles for the five senior technical-elective slots so the ample elective space maps to coherent specializations.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are labeled by year and semester and credits total to 120. The one clear blemish is a stray '368 B.S. in Environmental Science' row (null credits) that has bled into Third Year, 1st Semester as a page-break/parse artifact, plus the usual null-code 'or' alternative rows.
dimensions.suggestion: Remove the stray '368 B.S. in Environmental Science' row from the Third Year term and collapse the 'or CHEM 121' alternatives into single lines.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The required core is current and complete: CHEM 340 Chemistry of Materials explicitly covers semiconductors, electronic materials, and nanoscale materials; CHEM 412 Inorganic covers coordination/organometallic chemistry and catalysis; CHEM 480 Instrumental Analysis covers modern spectroscopy/chromatography/MS; and BNFO 135 provides programming. Gaps relative to contemporary practice are the absence of a computational-chemistry/cheminformatics course (CHEM 336 Quantum is theory-only) and of green/sustainable chemistry and polymer content in the required core.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a computational-chemistry/cheminformatics course and a green-chemistry offering so the program reflects current data-driven and sustainability-focused practice.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are substantive and specific across the core (CHEM 340 Chemistry of Materials, CHEM 412 Inorganic Chemistry, CHEM 480 Instrumental Analysis, CHEM 231/235 Physical Chemistry), with only a truncated CHEM 336 Quantum Chemistry entry ('...282 Jordan' artifact) standing out.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly with grade conditions and explicit OR-alternatives and multi-course gates (e.g., CHEM 231 requires 'CHEM 122 or CHEM 126, PHYS 111 and MATH 211 or MATH 213 or MATH 309'); the chemistry chain uses current codes with minimal legacy numbering, so it is the cleanest of this department's programs.

missingTopics.rationale: CHEM 336 Quantum Chemistry supplies theory, but no applied course lets students run electronic-structure or molecular-modeling calculations that are now standard research tools.
The synthesis-heavy core (CHEM 243/244) has no green-chemistry course covering atom economy, benign solvents, and sustainable process design.
CHEM 340 Chemistry of Materials emphasizes crystalline/electronic materials but polymers, a major industrial employment area, are underrepresented.
CHEM 480 Instrumental Analysis introduces instrumentation, but a dedicated advanced NMR/MS structure-elucidation course would deepen a key professional skill.

emergingTrends.trend: AI/ML in chemistry and materials discovery
emergingTrends.rationale: With BNFO 135 required and CHEM 340 Chemistry of Materials in the core, a machine-learning-for-chemistry module would position graduates for data-driven discovery.
CHEM 340 Chemistry of Materials and CHEM 235 Physical Chemistry can anchor battery/energy-storage content central to the current chemical industry.
Sustainability now shapes chemical manufacturing; a green-chemistry course would modernize the synthesis-focused organic core.
Building on CHEM 340 and CHEM 336, high-throughput/computational materials methods are a fast-growing direction the program is well placed to enter.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program learning outcomes, ideally mapped to ACS certification criteria; none currently appear.
Add a computational-chemistry/cheminformatics course and a green-chemistry offering to close the main currency gaps.
Remove the stray '368 B.S. in Environmental Science' row that bled into the Third Year term and fix the truncated CHEM 336 description.
Organize the five senior technical-elective slots into named specialization bundles (materials, analytical, biochemistry).
Add catalog stubs for the co-requisite labs, CHEM 210, and MATH 225 so all referenced codes resolve.

b-s-in-civil-engineering-587.name: B.S. in Civil Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The eight parsed semesters (requirementGroups 8) give strong coverage of all major civil subdisciplines in a sensible order: a math/science/computing/CAD foundation (MATH 111/112, CHEM 125/126, PHYS 111, CS 101, CE 101 CAD), the engineering-mechanics spine (MECH 235 Statics, MECH 239 Strength of Materials, MECH 236 Dynamics, CE 332 Structural Analysis), then subdiscipline courses fanning out into structures (CE 333 Reinforced Concrete, CE 432 Steel), geotechnical (CE 341 Soil Mechanics, CE 443 Foundation Design), water (CE 320 Fluid Mechanics, CE 321 Water Resources), transportation (CE 350), materials (CE 210, CE 360 Sustainable Materials) and environmental (ENE 262), closing with CE 494/495 Design I/II. Coherence is very good; it is held just below exemplary because the fourth-year first semester embeds an inline GER/COM selection list that muddies the otherwise clean term structure.
dimensions.suggestion: Pull the embedded '300-level GER: select one of the following (COM 312/316/319/337/339)' block out of the Year 4 term grid into a separate 'GER options' callout so the capstone term reads cleanly.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph has reasonable depth for a broad civil program (deepestPrereqChain 8) and follows the expected mechanics-to-design flow (Statics into Strength of Materials into Structural Analysis into Concrete/Steel design; Fluid Mechanics into Water Resources). missingCourseCount is 10, but the missingCoursesSample (CE 101, CE 200A, CE 320A, CE 341A, CE 431, CHEM 125A, GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A) is overwhelmingly co-scheduled labs and GER placeholders rather than substantive broken links, so the working graph is sound.
dimensions.suggestion: Reconcile the co-listed CE lab sections (CE 200A, CE 320A, CE 341A, CE 431) and the science labs (CHEM 125A, PHYS 111A/121A) with the catalog so the ten dangling references drop toward zero.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the captured data; the page provides the plan of study and course descriptions only. For an ABET civil program this is a meaningful omission because accreditation and licensure expectations are framed in terms of explicit student outcomes not shown here.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 4-6 program outcomes covering design across civil subdisciplines, professional/ethical practice, and the CE 494/495 capstone competencies, and note the path toward FE/PE licensure.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (summedTermCredits 120, statedTotalCredits 120, creditReconcileDelta 0) and is well balanced: a broad required core spanning every civil subdiscipline, math/basic science, humanities/GER and HSS, plus three genuine elective slots (CE Elective, CE Designated Elective, Science Elective) and IE 492 Engineering Management. Breadth and depth are both accommodated within the 120-credit envelope.
dimensions.suggestion: Add per-line credit values to the null-credit GER/HSS rows so each term's contribution to the 120 total is explicit.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page is largely clear and complete, with eight labeled terms, per-term subtotals, and full course titles. Readability is reduced by the Year 4 first-semester term containing an inline multi-option COM/GER selection list (COM 339/312/319/337/316 all shown with null credits), which makes that term look overloaded and ambiguous, and by several null-coded GER placeholder rows.
dimensions.suggestion: Render selection-based requirements ('choose one of...') as a single labeled line with a credit value rather than listing every option inside the term grid.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum is solidly current across the traditional civil subdisciplines and includes a modern note in CE 360 Sustainable Civil Engineering Materials and an environmental-engineering touchpoint (ENE 262). The main currency gaps are digital-delivery tools (BIM/GIS beyond the basic CE 101 CAD), infrastructure resilience/climate adaptation, and smart-infrastructure sensing, all of which are increasingly expected of new civil graduates.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or embed a BIM/GIS digital-delivery component and an infrastructure-resilience/climate-adaptation module (e.g., extending CE 321 Water Resources) to modernize the design sequence.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 32 sampled courses carry present, specific descriptions with none thin, and titles clearly convey scope across the subdisciplines (CE 332 Structural Analysis, CE 341 Soil Mechanics, CE 320 Fluid Mechanics, CE 360 Sustainable Civil Engineering Materials, CE 494/495 Design). Only the excerpt text is truncated in the capture.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites follow the expected mechanics-and-design ordering with grade conditions on the core chain and clear course-to-course dependencies. The deduction reflects the ten dangling references, which although mostly co-listed labs (CE 200A, CE 320A, CE 341A, CE 431) and GER placeholders, still leave several catalog links unresolved.

missingTopics.topic: Building Information Modeling (BIM) and digital project delivery
missingTopics.rationale: CE 101 provides only basic CAD; the program has no BIM/parametric-modeling course despite BIM being a baseline expectation across structural, construction and infrastructure practice.
CE 200 Surveying is traditional; adding GIS would connect surveying, water-resources (CE 321) and transportation (CE 350) planning to the geospatial workflows employers now assume.
CE 321 Water Resources and the structural sequence provide the technical base, but no course addresses flood/coastal resilience, extreme-load design or climate-adaptation planning.

missingTopics.topic: Structural health monitoring and smart sensing
missingTopics.rationale: With CE 332/333/432 covering analysis and design, there is no exposure to instrumentation, sensor networks or data-based condition assessment of infrastructure.
CE 360 introduces sustainable materials, but the program lacks a systematic LCA/embodied-carbon method course to quantify sustainability across designs.

emergingTrends.rationale: Extending CE 321 Water Resources and the structural design courses toward resilience and extreme-event design matches the dominant near-term direction of civil practice and funding.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital twins, BIM and AI in construction management
emergingTrends.rationale: Coupling IE 492 and the CE 494/495 capstone with BIM and data-driven construction analytics reflects rapid digitalization of project delivery.
CE 360 is a strong anchor to expand into low-carbon concrete, recycled aggregates and embodied-carbon design as decarbonization reaches the built environment.
Sensor-instrumented bridges and pavements are becoming standard; a monitoring/IoT component would modernize the structural and transportation tracks.

emergingTrends.trend: Green stormwater infrastructure and nature-based solutions
emergingTrends.rationale: Linking CE 321 and ENE 262, green-infrastructure design responds to the shift in water management toward nature-based, distributed solutions.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes / educational objectives (currently absent), aligned to the CE 494/495 capstone and to FE/PE licensure expectations.
Add digital-delivery competency (BIM and GIS) beyond the basic CE 101 CAD to meet a now-standard employer expectation.
Introduce infrastructure-resilience / climate-adaptation content, most naturally by extending CE 321 Water Resources and the structural design sequence.
Clean up the Year 4 term grid by moving the inline COM/GER 'choose one' selection block into a separate labeled options callout with a credit value.
Reconcile the ten dangling references (co-listed CE and science labs plus GER placeholders) so the prerequisite graph is fully connected.

b-s-in-computer-engineering-601.name: B.S. in Computer Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 8-term plan builds a coherent hardware-forward spine: math/science (MATH 111/112/213/222/326, PHYS 111/122, CHEM 125) feeds circuits (ECE 231->232), digital design and microprocessors (ECE 251->252), electronics (ECE 271), and computer organization/architecture (ECE 353), while the CS thread (CS 115->116->280, CS 332 OS) runs in parallel and prerequisite depths rise smoothly from 0 to 8 into the ECE 414/416/495 capstone. Coherence is only softened by three undifferentiated 'COE Track Elective' slots plus a 'COE Track Laboratory Elective' and two 'Technical Elective' slots concentrated in the senior year.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the named senior tracks the track-electives feed (e.g., a computer-architecture path ECE 451/452/459 vs. a communications/networks path) so the upper-level plan reads as declared depth rather than open choice.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is deep and internally consistent, with a deepestPrereqChain of 8 reflecting the long circuits->electronics->architecture ladder and explicit grade conditions (e.g., CS 116 'CS 115 with a grade C or better'). Of the 11 missing references (missingCoursesSample: CS 251, ECE 341, ECE 373, ECE 394, ECE 414, ECE 492, ECE 493, GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A), the GER items and PHYS 111A/121A labs are catalog placeholders, but CS 251 and ECE 341/373/394/414/492/493 are genuine dangling course/track/lab references that should resolve to real entries.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the seven genuine dangling references (CS 251 and ECE 341/373/394/414/492/493), which appear in track/lab/project slots, so the metrics show only benign GER/lab placeholders.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or ABET-style student outcomes appear anywhere in the provided data for this ABET-track engineering B.S.; only the plan of study and course descriptions are present, which is a real catalog finding for an accredited program.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit program educational objectives and student outcomes (hardware/software design, experimentation, engineering ethics, communication) on the catalog page, which also supports ABET accreditation reporting.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: summedTermCredits 120 equals statedTotalCredits 120 with creditReconcileDelta 0.0, and terms are evenly loaded at 14-16 credits. The split transparently balances a math/science foundation, the ECE/CS core, an engineering-ethics and business/economics requirement (PHIL 334; MGMT 390/IE 492/ECON), GER, and the track/technical electives.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a one-line credit-summary table (math & science vs. ECE/CS core vs. GER/ethics vs. track & technical electives) so the balanced distribution is visible without reading all eight terms.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled and required ECE/CS courses are legible, but the plan carries parsing artifacts: a null-code 'FYS SEM' seminar shown at 0 credits, a fragmentary 'or MATH 337 or Linear Algebra' line, a 'Select one of the following:' block (MGMT 390/IE 492/ECON 201/265/266) whose options show null credits, and an 'or ECE 417' alternative rendered as a separate null row.
dimensions.suggestion: Render alternative-course requirements ('MATH 340 or MATH 337', 'ECE 416 or ECE 417') and the business/economics 'select one' block as single labeled entries with credits so the plan is self-contained.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
