dimensions.justification: The required core reflects classical computer-engineering practice well (digital design ECE 251, microprocessors ECE 252 with lab ECE 395, computer organization/architecture ECE 353, operating systems CS 332, circuits and electronics), and the elective tracks reach current topics (advanced computer architecture ECE 451/452). But the required sequence has notable modern gaps: no HDL/FPGA reconfigurable-logic course, no dedicated embedded-systems/RTOS course beyond bare-metal assembly, no machine-learning or hardware-for-AI content, no required computer-networks course (ECE 368 is signal transmission, not networking), and no hardware/embedded security.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a required HDL/FPGA design course (Verilog/VHDL) and an embedded-systems/RTOS course, and offer an ML-accelerator or edge-AI track elective, to close the largest currency gaps in an otherwise strong hardware core.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are present and technically specific (ECE 231 on circuit theory and system analysis, ECE 353 on register-transfer logic and CPU hardware design, ECE 251 on combinational/sequential logic, CS 332 on process management and concurrency). Only minor artifacts detract: ECE 101's excerpt opens with a stray ', 1 contact hour (0;0;1)' fragment and a few upper-level entries are truncated mid-sentence.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are explicit with consistent grade conditions and sensible compound logic (e.g., ECE 294 'ECE 231, ECE 251, ENGL 101', ECE 342 '(ECE 231 and ECE 291) or (ECE 231 and ECE 294)', ECE 453 'ECE 251 or CS 251 or equivalent, and MATH 333 or ECE 321 or equivalent'). Clarity is reduced only by references to courses missing from the catalog (CS 251, ECE 341/373/394/414) that leave a few chains pointing at undefined nodes.

missingTopics.topic: Hardware description languages and FPGA design
missingTopics.rationale: ECE 251 Digital Design and ECE 353 architecture teach gate- and register-level design but no Verilog/VHDL or reconfigurable-logic flow; add an HDL/FPGA course so students design and synthesize on programmable hardware as industry expects.

missingTopics.topic: Embedded systems and real-time operating systems
missingTopics.rationale: ECE 252 Microprocessors and ECE 395 lab cover assembly and bare-metal programming, but there is no dedicated embedded-systems/RTOS course covering interrupts, scheduling, and device drivers that define modern embedded practice.

missingTopics.topic: Machine learning and hardware for AI
missingTopics.rationale: The plan has no ML content at all; given the strong architecture base (ECE 353/451/452), a machine-learning or ML-accelerator elective would let CmpE graduates target the fast-growing hardware-for-AI segment.
ECE 368 covers signal transmission between digital systems, not networking protocols; a required computer-networks course would fill an obvious gap for engineers building connected devices.

With computer architecture (ECE 353), a design lab (ECE 495), and microprocessor work required, there is no coverage of secure boot, side channels, or trusted execution, leaving a security gap in the hardware track.

emergingTrends.rationale: Running ML on microcontrollers and custom accelerators is a defining CmpE direction; the ECE 353/495 architecture-and-design sequence is the natural place to require applied edge-AI work.
Industry and research are standardizing on RISC-V; modernizing ECE 353/ECE 451 to build and extend a RISC-V core would keep the architecture track current.
Modern systems combine programmable logic and accelerators on one SoC; extending the advanced-architecture track (ECE 451/452) toward heterogeneous design targets this shift.
Connected embedded devices dominate the CmpE job market; pairing microprocessors (ECE 252) with a networks course would position graduates for cyber-physical/IoT design.
Supply-chain trust, side-channel defense, and secure enclaves are now first-class hardware concerns that the current architecture-and-design core does not address.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program educational objectives and ABET student outcomes, which are absent from the page and required for accreditation reporting.
Add required HDL/FPGA design and embedded-systems/RTOS courses to close the two biggest currency gaps in the hardware core.
Resolve the seven genuine dangling references (CS 251, ECE 341/373/394/414/492/493) so the prerequisite and track/lab slots point at real catalog entries.
Declare the senior COE track and technical-elective slots as named concentrations (e.g., computer architecture vs. communications) for intentional upper-level depth.
Clean plan-of-study artifacts (null-code FYS seminar, 'or MATH 337' and 'or ECE 417' fragments, the null-credit business/economics 'select one' block) so the plan is self-contained.

b-s-in-computer-science-228.name: B.S. in Computer Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 8-term plan is the fuller flagship path: the CS spine (CS 100 -> 113 -> 114 -> 241 -> 280/288 -> 332/350/356 -> 435/490 -> 491) is complemented by theory II (CS 341), data science (CS 301), cybersecurity (CS 351), and linear algebra (MATH 337), with prerequisite depths rising smoothly from 0 to 6. Coherence is only slightly weakened by four undifferentiated 'CS Elective 300 or above' slots concentrated in the senior year.
dimensions.suggestion: Group the four senior-year CS electives into 1-2 declared concentrations (e.g., AI/data, security, systems) so the upper-level plan reads as intentional depth rather than open choice.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy with a deepest chain of 6 and good breadth (CS 341 depth 5, CS 351 depth 5, CS 301 depth 6), and the required sequence is internally consistent with no genuine CS breaks. All 6 dangling references (missingCourseCount 6: GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) are GER placeholders, physics labs, and one-credit seminars.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the physics-lab (PHYS 111A/121A) and YWCC 207/307 catalog entries and the GER placeholders so the metrics show zero dangling references.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or ABET-style educational objectives appear in the provided data despite this being the college's flagship accredited-track B.S.; only the plan of study and course descriptions are present. This absence is a real catalog finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit program learning outcomes / student outcomes (design, analysis, communication, professional responsibility), which also supports program accreditation reporting.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: summedTermCredits 120 equals statedTotalCredits 120 with creditReconcileDelta 0.0, and terms are evenly loaded at 14-16 credits. The distribution transparently balances a deep CS core, a math/science block (MATH 111/112/333/337, PHYS 111/121 with labs), GER, and upper-level CS electives.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an explicit credit-summary table (major core vs. math/science vs. GER vs. CS electives) to make the well-balanced split visible without reading all eight terms.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled and the required courses are legible, but the plan contains parsing artifacts: a stray cross-program line ('230 B.S. in Computer Science and B.S. in Applied Physics') appears in Year 4, the COM 312 requirement is split across an 'or COM 313 or Technical Writing' fragment, and a null-code FYS seminar row shows 0 credits.
dimensions.suggestion: Render alternative-course requirements ('COM 312 or COM 313') as single entries and remove the cross-program artifact line so the B.S. plan is self-contained.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The required core reflects current practice well: it mandates data science (CS 301), cybersecurity (CS 351), databases (CS 331), networks (CS 356), systems (CS 350), advanced algorithms (CS 435), software engineering (CS 490), and a team Senior Project (CS 491). The main currency gaps are the lack of a required dedicated AI/ML course beyond CS 301 and no cloud/distributed-systems requirement, plus CS 288's dated PHP/MySQL framing.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a required or default intro-AI/ML course and a cloud/distributed-systems elective, and refresh CS 288's stack toward current cloud-native tooling.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are present and specific with genuine technical content (CS 341 on automata/computability/complexity, CS 351 on cybersecurity fundamentals, CS 301 on data-science models and hands-on skills); only the MATH entries (111/112/337) are terse topic lists.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are explicit with consistent grade conditions and sensible compound logic (e.g., CS 341 '(CS 241 or MATH 226) and CS 280 with a grade C or better', CS 351 'CS 241 and CS 356'), and the required chain is internally consistent. Only minor phrasing inconsistency and dangling GER references detract.

missingTopics.topic: Dedicated artificial intelligence / machine learning course
missingTopics.rationale: CS 301 introduces data-science models but the B.S. lacks a required AI/ML course covering supervised/unsupervised learning and neural networks in depth; add a required CS 4xx Machine Learning to match peer programs.

CS 332 (OS) and CS 356 (networks) stop at the single node and LAN; there is no coverage of containers, orchestration, or distributed consensus that define modern backends, so a distributed-systems/cloud course is warranted.
Concurrency is only touched inside CS 332; with multicore and GPU hardware ubiquitous, a dedicated parallel-programming course would strengthen the systems track.

missingTopics.topic: Software engineering breadth (testing, DevOps, version control)
missingTopics.rationale: CS 490 is the single SWE course and CS 288 centers PHP/MySQL, so CI/CD, automated testing, and API design are underweighted relative to industry hiring expectations.
The plan has no interaction-design content, a gap for graduates building user-facing products; a HCI elective would round out the largely back-end and theory core.

emergingTrends.rationale: Building, evaluating, and deploying LLM-based systems is now a core industry skill; the CS 491 Senior Project and CS 490 are ideal vehicles to require applied generative-AI work.
CS 301 gives a data-science foundation that could extend into production ML pipelines, one of the fastest-growing CS job categories.
Industry deployment has moved to containerized, serverless, and edge patterns that the current systems sequence does not address, motivating a cloud requirement.
With CS 351 already required, the B.S. is well positioned to lead on secure-by-default development and on defending ML systems against adversarial attacks.
With a strong theory core (CS 241/CS 341) and math base, a quantum-computing elective would future-proof the program's theoretical distinctiveness.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program learning outcomes / student outcomes, which are absent and are also needed for accreditation reporting.
Add a required dedicated AI/ML course and a cloud/distributed-systems requirement to close the two biggest currency gaps in an otherwise strong core.
Organize the four senior-year 'CS Elective 300 or above' slots into declared concentrations (AI/data, security, systems) for intentional depth.
Modernize CS 288's PHP/MySQL emphasis toward current cloud-native and data tooling.
Clean plan-of-study artifacts (the stray '230 ...' cross-program line and split 'or COM 313' rows) and resolve the 6 dangling references (PHYS 111A/121A, YWCC 207/307, GER 200/300).

b-s-in-computer-science-and-b-s-in-appli.name: B.S. in Computer Science and B.S. in Applied Physics

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The five-year plan builds logically along three well-ordered spines: CS (CS 100 -> 113 -> 114 -> 241/280 -> 341/350/435 -> 490/491), physics (PHYS 111 -> 121 -> 234 -> 335 -> 430/432 -> 442/485), and math (MATH 111 -> 112 -> 211 -> 222/335), with a deepest prereq chain of 7 confirming genuine term-over-term progression. Integration points such as CS 301 Data Science, PHYS 485 Computer Modeling, and OPSE 310 Virtual Instrumentation sensibly tie the two disciplines together, though the first year is heavily front-loaded at 18 credits.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a curriculum map showing how the physics track feeds the computing courses (e.g., PHYS 485 modeling drawing on CS 301/CS 435) so the dual-degree rationale is explicit rather than implied by co-scheduling.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sensible with a deepest chain of 7, reasonable for a rigorous 5-year dual degree, and grade conditions are clean throughout. The 7 dangling references (missingCourseCount 7: CHEM 125A, CHEM 126A, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, PHYS 231A, GER 200, GER 300) are all lab co-requisites or GER category placeholders rather than broken CS/physics chains.
dimensions.suggestion: Register the lab co-requisites (CHEM 125A/126A, PHYS 111A/121A/231A) and the GER 200/300 category codes so these dangling references resolve and missingCourseCount drops toward zero.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only a plan of study and course list are present. This is a real catalog finding, and it matters more here because a dual degree needs an explicit statement of the integrated computing-plus-physics competency it produces.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 program learning outcomes covering computing foundations, applied-physics modeling, and integrated computational problem-solving directly on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (creditReconcileDelta 0.0; summedTermCredits 134 = statedTotalCredits 134), and the distribution spans a heavy STEM core (CS, physics, math, chemistry) with writing, ethics (IS 350), and GER woven in. Load is uneven, however: the first year is 18 credits while the two final terms drop to 12, and 134 credits is light for a true BS/BS pairing, worth confirming.
dimensions.suggestion: Level the term loads (redistribute from the 18-credit first year) and annotate the gen-ed vs. major-core vs. elective credit split so the balance is transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled with per-term credits shown, but the page carries PDF-extraction artifacts: 'or MATH 328 or Mathematical Methods...' and 'or R750 404 or Quantum Mechanics' alternatives appear as separate credit-less rows, and a stray '232 B.S. in Computer Science and B.S. in Mathematical Sciences' line bleeds in from an adjacent program.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the plan-of-study parse so 'or ...' alternatives fold into their parent course and the stray '232 ...' cross-program fragment is removed.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The course set is strong for scientific-computing careers: CS 301 (data science), CS 351 (cybersecurity), CS 356 (networks), CS 435 (algorithms), CS 490 (software engineering) on the computing side, and PHYS 442 Quantum Mechanics, PHYS 485 Computer Modeling of Applied Physics Problems, and OPSE 310 Virtual Instrumentation on the physics side. The main gaps are a dedicated ML/AI course and any explicit high-performance/parallel computing for large-scale simulation.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a machine-learning-for-science elective and an HPC/GPU computing option so graduates can apply modern compute to the physical-modeling work PHYS 485 introduces.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are present, specific, and informative, naming concrete topics (CS 341 on automata/computability/complexity, CS 435 on balanced trees/hashing/graph algorithms, MATH 335 on Gauss/Green/Stokes theorems, PHYS 485 on computer modeling). None read as thin placeholders; only excerpts are truncated.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated unambiguously with consistent grade conditions ('with a grade C or better') and sensible alternatives (e.g., CS 280 'CS 114 or CS 116 or IT 114'), and intro courses correctly show none. Clarity is only modestly reduced by references to lab co-reqs and GER categories that are not themselves catalogued.

missingTopics.rationale: Computational physics depends on numerical analysis, yet MATH 328 (Mathematical Methods) is only an 'or' option and no dedicated numerical-methods course is required; make a numerical-methods course a required bridge feeding PHYS 485.
PHYS 485 Computer Modeling and OPSE 310 imply large simulations but the plan has no GPU/parallel/HPC course; add one so students can run realistic physics workloads.

missingTopics.topic: Machine learning for the physical sciences
missingTopics.rationale: CS 301 introduces data science but there is no ML course applied to scientific data or surrogate modeling, now central to computational physics; add a scientific-ML elective.
Neither the CS nor physics track includes visualization or large scientific-dataset analysis, a core skill for experimental and simulation work; add a data-visualization module or course.
With PHYS 442 Quantum Mechanics plus a strong CS theory core (CS 241/CS 341), the program is unusually positioned to offer quantum computing but currently does not; add a quantum-computing elective.

emergingTrends.rationale: The rare combination of required quantum mechanics (PHYS 442) and CS theory makes this dual degree ideally placed to lead on quantum algorithms as the field industrializes.
Physics-informed neural networks and ML surrogates are transforming simulation; CS 301 plus PHYS 485 are natural anchors for a scientific-ML pathway.
Modern applied-physics modeling runs on accelerators and HPC clusters, so an HPC skill set would directly extend PHYS 485's computer-modeling work.
Coupling OPSE 310 instrumentation with simulation points toward digital-twin engineering, a growing area in aerospace, energy, and manufacturing.
Automated, ML-guided experimentation is reshaping physics labs; the program's instrumentation and computing base could position graduates for this niche.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program learning outcomes for the integrated CS-plus-physics competency (currently none are present).
Require a numerical-methods/scientific-computing course and add an HPC/parallel-computing option to properly support PHYS 485 computer modeling.
Add a scientific-machine-learning elective and, leveraging PHYS 442, a quantum-computing elective to occupy the program's distinctive niche.
Level the uneven term loads (18-credit first year vs. 12-credit final terms) and annotate the gen-ed/major-core/elective credit split.
Clean the plan-of-study artifacts (fold 'or MATH 328' / 'or R750 404' alternatives into their parent rows, remove the stray '232 ...' line) and register the 7 dangling lab/GER references.

b-s-in-computer-science-and-b-s-in-mathe.name: B.S. in Computer Science and B.S. in Mathematical Sciences, Applied Mathematics

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: Within the visible terms the CS spine (CS 114 -> 280 -> 288 -> 350 -> 435/341) and the math spine (MATH 211 -> 222/335 -> capstone) progress sensibly, and a deepest prereq chain of 7 shows real layering. Two problems hold it to adequate: the parsed plan starts at 'Second Year - 1st Semester', so the entire first-year foundation (intro CS, Calc I/II, intro physics) is missing; and the shown plan is conspicuously physics-heavy (PHYS 234/335/430/432/442/485, OPSE 310) for a degree titled CS + Applied Mathematics, so the applied-math half is underrepresented in the visible plan of study.
dimensions.suggestion: Add the missing first-year term and explicitly label which courses satisfy the CS degree versus the Applied Mathematics degree so the dual-degree progression is legible end to end.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is deep and healthy: a deepest chain of 7 with a clean applied-math ladder (MATH 340 -> 391/331 -> 440/450 -> 451 at depth 7) and well-formed boolean/grade conditions throughout. The 8 dangling references (missingCourseCount 8: CHEM 126A, GER 200, GER 300, MATH 300, MATH 400, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, PHYS 231A) are all lab co-sections or GER/elective-tier placeholders rather than genuine prerequisite breaks.
dimensions.suggestion: Map the 8 dangling codes to real catalog entries or explicitly mark GER 200/300 and MATH 300/400 as category placeholders so the graph resolves cleanly.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: The provided data contains no program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives, only a plan of study and course list. This absence is a real catalog finding and is especially costly for a dual degree, where the value proposition is the integrated computing-plus-applied-mathematics competency that goes unstated.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit program learning outcomes distinguishing the computing and applied-mathematics competencies the combined degree is designed to produce.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan does not reconcile: creditReconcileDelta is -33.0 (summedTermCredits 101 vs statedTotalCredits 134), driven by the parsed plan omitting the entire first-year term rather than by a curriculum flaw. As presented the credit distribution across GER, CS core, math, and physics cannot be verified, so this is primarily a data-consistency caveat that nonetheless undermines transparency.
dimensions.suggestion: Include the missing first-year term so summed credits reconcile to 134, then add a category-level breakdown (GER, CS core, applied-math, physics, electives).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are labeled with per-term credit totals, but the page is incomplete and noisy: the first-year term is absent entirely (only 7 requirement groups, starting at Year 2), 'or ...' alternatives (or MATH 328, or COM 313, or R750 404) render as detached credit-less rows, and there is no program-level overview. The combination of a missing term and detached fragments makes the plan hard to read as a whole.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore the first-year term, fold 'or ...' alternatives into their parent requirements, and add an introductory program description.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The coursework is contemporary and marketable: CS 301 (data science), CS 351 (cybersecurity), CS 356 (networks), CS 435 (algorithms), CS 331 (databases), CS 490/491, plus a strong computational-math core (MATH 340 Applied Numerical Methods, MATH 391 Numerical Linear Algebra, MATH 453 High-Performance Numerical Computing, MATH 448 Stochastic Simulation). The chief gaps are a dedicated machine-learning course and no explicit optimization or cloud/DevOps exposure.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a machine-learning elective and a continuous-optimization course to convert the strong numerical/statistics base into the quantitative-ML skill set employers now expect.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are consistently present, specific, and substantive (CS 301 on data-science models, CS 341 on automata/computability, MATH 453 on state-of-the-art large-scale numerical algorithms, MATH 448 on stochastic simulation). A minor blemish is bleed-through in MATH 222's excerpt (which runs into unrelated 'MATH 225. Survey of Probability...' text).

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are generally clear with explicit grade conditions and sensible AND/OR logic (e.g., CS 341 '(CS 241 or MATH 226) and CS 280', MATH 450 'MATH 331 and MATH 337 and MATH 340'). Clarity is reduced by the 8 program-level dangling references and by 'or' alternatives appearing as detached null-code items, weakening end-to-end consistency.

missingTopics.rationale: The program has an unusually strong stats and linear-algebra base (MATH 333, MATH 337, MATH 391) plus CS 301 data science, yet no dedicated ML course; adding statistical/machine learning would be the single highest-leverage addition for an applied-math+CS graduate.

Optimization is foundational to applied mathematics, operations research, and ML training, but no optimization course appears despite the deep numerical suite (MATH 340/391/453); add a convex/numerical optimization course.

CS + applied math is a classic feeder to quantitative finance, and with MATH 448 Stochastic Simulation already present a mathematical-finance elective would open a high-demand career path currently unaddressed.

missingTopics.topic: Modern data engineering and big-data tooling
missingTopics.rationale: CS 301 is the only data course; graduates working with the large-scale numerical problems MATH 453 targets need distributed-data and pipeline skills, so a data-engineering elective is warranted.
Beyond CS 241/CS 435, there is no dedicated discrete-optimization or graph-theory course, a gap for applied-math students heading into networks, logistics, and OR.

emergingTrends.trend: Scientific machine learning and AI-augmented solvers
emergingTrends.rationale: ML surrogates and neural solvers are reshaping numerical computing; the program's numerical suite (MATH 340/391/440/453) is an ideal launchpad for a scientific-ML pathway.
MATH 453 already introduces HPC numerical algorithms; formalizing GPU/parallel computing would match where large-scale applied-math workloads now run.
With CS theory (CS 241/CS 341) plus deep applied math, the program is well placed to add quantum-algorithm content as the area matures.
Stochastic simulation (MATH 448) plus CS gives a strong quant foundation; ML-driven trading and risk modeling are a natural, marketable direction.
Combining the program's PDE/numerical courses with statistics points toward UQ, increasingly central to simulation-based science and engineering.

topSuggestions: Reconcile the credit plan by restoring the missing first-year term so summed credits meet the stated 134 (currently 101, delta -33), then publish a category-level credit breakdown.
Publish explicit program learning outcomes distinguishing the computing and applied-mathematics competencies of the combined degree.
Add a machine-learning elective and a continuous-optimization course to turn the strong numerical/statistics base into a modern quantitative-ML skill set.
Clarify the dual-degree structure by labeling which courses satisfy CS versus Applied Mathematics, since the visible plan reads as physics-heavy relative to the program title.

Fix presentation and resolve the 8 dangling references: fold 'or ...' alternatives into their parent rows, add a program overview, and map or flag CHEM 126A, PHYS 111A/121A/231A, GER 200/300, and MATH 300/400.

b-s-in-data-science-268.name: B.S. in Data Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 8-term plan is a model of ordered progression: a programming spine (CS 100 -> CS 113 -> CS 114 -> CS 241/CS 280 -> CS 288) runs in parallel with a math/stats track (MATH 111 -> 112 -> 244 Probability / 337 Linear Algebra -> 341 Statistical Methods II -> 478 Stat Methods in Data Science), and both converge into the data core (CS 331 Databases, DS 340, CS 370 AI, CS 375 ML, CS 482 Data Mining, CS 444 Big Data Systems, CS 450 Data Visualization) and a two-part capstone (CS 492/DS 493). Prerequisite depths climb smoothly from 0 to 7 with no abrupt jumps.
dimensions.suggestion: Convert the generic 'Data Science Elective 1/2/3' placeholders into named concentration tracks (e.g., ML systems, business analytics, bio/health data) so the upper-level path is as prescribed as the core.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is well-shaped: deepestPrereqChain=7 is appropriate for a quantitative four-year degree, and the 6 dangling references (missingCourseCount=6: GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) are GER placeholders, physics labs, and one-credit seminars rather than genuine data-science prerequisite breaks. Structural health is strong but not perfectly clean.
dimensions.suggestion: Add catalog entries or code mappings for YWCC 207/307 and the PHYS 111A/121A labs and resolve the GER 200/300 placeholders so no referenced code dangles.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only a term-by-term plan and per-course descriptions are present. For a flagship B.S. this absence is a real catalog finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit program learning outcomes (e.g., what a graduate can model, engineer, analyze, visualize, and communicate) on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan does not reconcile: statedTotalCredits is null and creditReconcileDelta is therefore null, while summedTermCredits is only 105.0, well short of a typical ~120-credit B.S. The eighth term (Fourth Year, 2nd Semester) is truncated with null credits and just two items (a senior-seminar GER and one free elective), so both the stated total and the final-term accounting are incomplete.
dimensions.suggestion: State the program's total credit requirement and complete the eighth-term listing so the summed credits reconcile to a stated total (targeting the ~120-121 typical of a B.S.).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled by year and semester and the required courses are legible, but presentation is undercut by the truncated final term (null credits, only two rows), the null stated total, and repeated generic 'Data Science Elective' placeholders that leave the upper-level path visually open.
dimensions.suggestion: Complete the eighth-term rows, add a stated total, and replace bare elective placeholders with example course lists so the plan is fully legible end to end.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The required core is strong and current for data science: CS 375 Machine Learning, CS 482 Data Mining, CS 444 Big Data Systems, CS 450 Data Visualization, CS 370 AI, CS 331 Databases, and a deep statistics track (MATH 244/341/478). The main currency gaps are the absence of a dedicated deep-learning course beyond intro ML, no MLOps/data-engineering pipeline course, and no explicit generative-AI/LLM content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a deep-learning course and an MLOps/data-engineering course so graduates can both build modern models and deploy them in production.

descriptionQuality.justification: Course descriptions are present and substantive across the program's spine (CS 375 Machine Learning, CS 444 Big Data Systems, CS 482 Data Mining, MATH 478 Statistical Methods in Data Science), with no thin or placeholder excerpts observed among sampled courses.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with clear course logic and grade conditions, and the only dangling references (missingCourseCount=6) are non-core GER placeholders, physics labs, and one-credit seminars rather than data-science course breaks, so the core prerequisite chain is unambiguous.

missingTopics.rationale: CS 375 is 'Introduction to Machine Learning' and CS 370 is intro AI, but there is no dedicated deep-learning course (CNNs, RNNs, transformers), which is now foundational to contemporary data science.

CS 444 Big Data Systems is infrastructure-adjacent, but no course covers data pipelines, feature stores, model deployment, or monitoring, the single biggest skills gap for industry data-science hiring.
The stats spine (MATH 244/341/478) is strong on probability and regression but has no A/B testing or causal inference, which are central to product and industry analytics.
CS 482 Data Mining and CS 370 AI touch adjacent ground, but there is no text/NLP or computer-vision course even though most modern data is unstructured.

missingTopics.topic: Responsible AI: fairness, privacy, and interpretability
missingTopics.rationale: IS 350 Computers, Society and Ethics is a general ethics course; given the ML and data-mining core, the program should add dedicated algorithmic-fairness, privacy (e.g., differential privacy), and model-interpretability content.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI and large language models
emergingTrends.rationale: The AI/ML spine (CS 370/375/482) predates the foundation-model era; a course or module on LLMs, prompting, retrieval-augmented generation, and fine-tuning is essential positioning for graduates.
Industry has shifted from model-building to reliable ML systems; CS 444 and the CS 492/DS 493 capstone could anchor a production-ML track.
CS 444 Big Data Systems should modernize toward streaming (Kafka), lakehouse architectures, and cloud data platforms that dominate current data engineering.
Regulation such as the EU AI Act and practices like model cards are arriving; graduates need fairness, privacy, and governance literacy beyond the single IS 350 ethics course.

The elective pool already includes BME 210 and BNFO 135 (bio/health signals and bioinformatics); deepening domain tracks (health, business analytics) via the elective placeholders would sharpen employability.

topSuggestions: State a total credit requirement and complete the truncated eighth term so the plan reconciles (current summed credits are only 105 against a null stated total).
Add a deep-learning course and an MLOps/data-engineering course to close the two clearest gaps between the curriculum and industry data-science practice.
Introduce generative-AI/LLM content and a responsible-AI (fairness/privacy/interpretability) offering to modernize the AI-ML core beyond IS 350's general ethics.
Convert the 'Data Science Elective 1/2/3' placeholders into named concentration tracks with example course lists.
Publish program-level learning outcomes and resolve the 6 dangling GER/lab/seminar references so the catalog page is complete.

b-s-in-electrical-engineering-608.name: B.S. in Electrical Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The course universe (39 sampled courses) describes a complete, well-ordered EE curriculum, from circuits (ECE 231->232) and electronics (ECE 271->371/375) through EM fields (ECE 361), signals and random processes (ECE 321/333), into control (ECE 431/432/439), power (ECE 442/443/449), communications (ECE 421/422/425/481/489), and computer systems (ECE 353/451/452), with prerequisite depths reaching 8. However, only 4 requirement groups parsed and they capture the co-op tail (ENGR 410 Co-op, 'Fifth Year' terms) rather than the full plan, and those terms carry cross-program Computer Engineering labels (ECE 495 'Computer Engineering Design Lab', 'COE Track Elective'), so term-over-term progression cannot be verified from the published plan.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the full 5-year plan of study (all terms, not just the co-op tail) and replace the CmpE-labeled capstone/track rows with the EE senior-design and EE track electives so progression is legible.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is rich and internally consistent, with explicit grade/compound conditions (e.g., ECE 371 'ECE 232, ECE 271, and ECE 294 or ECE 291', ECE 431 'ECE 333, or ECE 232 and MATH 337') and a deepestPrereqChain of 8 appropriate to a 5-year EE program. The 5 missing references (missingCoursesSample: ECE 414, GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A) are mostly GER/lab placeholders plus ECE 414 (Project I), the one genuine dangling course.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve ECE 414 (Electrical and Computer Engineering Project I) in the catalog so the senior-project chain feeding ECE 416/417 has no dangling reference.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or ABET student outcomes appear in the provided data for this flagship accredited EE degree; only fragmentary plan terms and course descriptions are present.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit EE program educational objectives and student outcomes (analysis, design, experimentation, professional and ethical responsibility) on the catalog page to support both students and ABET reporting.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The credit accounting does not reconcile as published: statedTotalCredits is 145 but summedTermCredits is only 42, giving a creditReconcileDelta of -103.0. This is primarily a data/parse caveat, the combined 5-year co-op plan of study did not parse and only the co-op tail was captured, rather than proof of imbalance, but as it stands the page cannot demonstrate that its 145 credits are distributed sensibly across general education, math/science, EE core, and electives.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the complete term-by-term plan so the credits sum to and reconcile with the stated 145-credit total, and add a general-education vs. math/science vs. EE-core vs. electives summary.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The published plan is fragmentary and mislabeled: only four groups appear, several are co-op rows (ENGR 410, 'CO-OP II'), the 'Fifth Year' terms carry cross-program Computer Engineering titles ('Computer Engineering Design Lab', 'COE Track Elective') inside an EE degree, and alternative rows such as 'or ECE 417' render as separate null entries, so a reader cannot follow the intended EE sequence.
dimensions.suggestion: Regenerate the plan of study for EE specifically (remove the CmpE capstone/track artifacts, label all terms, and merge 'ECE 416 or ECE 417' into one entry) so the flagship EE page is complete and self-contained.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The EE course set is broad and current: renewable energy (ECE 443), power systems (ECE 442/449), modern communications and software-defined radio (ECE 425 wireless, ECE 481 digital comm, ECE 489 SDR/Matlab-Simulink), state-space and modern control (ECE 431/432), advanced computer architecture (ECE 451/452), and EM/photonics (ECE 461/462/469). The main currency gaps are the absence of any required machine-learning/AI content, no dedicated power-electronics/converter course for electrification, and device coverage (ECE 375) that stops short of VLSI/IC design.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a machine-learning-for-signals elective and a power-electronics course (for EV/grid electrification), and extend the devices sequence (ECE 375) toward VLSI/IC design to match semiconductor-sector demand.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are present and specific across the EE spectrum (ECE 361 on electrostatics and Coulomb's/Gauss's laws, ECE 431 on feedback control and Laplace/state-space methods, ECE 489 on Matlab/Simulink and Software-Defined Radio labs, ECE 443 on wind/solar/biomass sources). Only minor artifacts appear, such as ECE 429's excerpt beginning 'Pre or' and some entries truncated mid-sentence.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with explicit compound logic and grade conditions (e.g., ECE 375 '(ECE 271 and ECE 291) or (ECE 271 and ECE 294)', ECE 495 '(ECE 294 and ECE 353) or (ECE 394 and ECE 353)', ECE 425 'ECE 481 or ECE 421'). Clarity is dented only by the dangling ECE 414 reference in the ECE 416/417 chain and the 'Pre or' fragment in ECE 429.

missingTopics.rationale: The program has a deep signals/communications/control set (ECE 421/431/481) but no ML; a machine-learning-for-signals elective would modernize how graduates approach detection, estimation, and control.
Power systems (ECE 442/449) and renewable energy (ECE 443) are present, but there is no dedicated power-electronics/converter course, the core skill behind EV drivetrains and grid inverters.
Device physics is covered (ECE 375/371) but stops short of transistor-level IC/VLSI design; a VLSI course would align the program with CHIPS-era semiconductor hiring.
ECE 252 microprocessors and ECE 353 computer systems are present, yet no embedded-systems/IoT course ties sensing, low-power design, and connectivity together for modern EE products.

missingTopics.topic: Cybersecurity for communication and control systems
missingTopics.rationale: With strong comms (ECE 422/425/481) and control (ECE 431/432), there is no coverage of securing networked control or wireless links, a growing requirement in critical infrastructure.

emergingTrends.rationale: ECE 443 (renewables) and ECE 442/449 (power systems) already position the program well; a smart-grid/inverter emphasis would make this a signature strength.
ECE 425 and ECE 489 already teach wireless and SDR; refreshing them toward mmWave, massive-MIMO, and 6G keeps the communications track at the frontier.
Reshoring of chip fabrication is driving demand; extending device courses (ECE 375) into VLSI/analog-IC design captures this trend.
Data-driven control and learned signal processing are reshaping the field; ECE 421/431 are ideal hosts for a machine-learning module.
Vehicle and grid electrification depend on power electronics that the current power sequence does not cover, motivating a converter/drive course.

topSuggestions: Publish the complete 5-year plan of study so credits reconcile to the stated 145 (currently only 42 summed, delta -103) and terms are legible.
Remove the cross-program Computer Engineering artifacts (the CmpE 'Design Lab' and 'COE Track' rows) from the EE plan and replace them with EE senior design and EE track electives.
Publish explicit EE program educational objectives and ABET student outcomes, which are absent from the page.
Add a machine-learning-for-signals elective and a power-electronics course, and extend devices toward VLSI, to close the main currency gaps.
Resolve the dangling ECE 414 (Project I) reference so the senior-project chain into ECE 416/417 is complete.

b-s-in-environmental-science-368.name: B.S. in Environmental Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The presented 8-term plan is not an environmental-science sequence at all: every term is populated with the Chemistry degree's courses (CHEM 336 Quantum Chemistry, CHEM 340 Chemistry of Materials, CHEM 412 Inorganic, CHEM 231/235 Physical Chemistry, CHEM 473 Biochemistry) and contains not a single EVSC course, while the genuinely environmental offerings appear only in the course pool (EVSC 125 Fundamentals of Environmental Sciences, EVSC 325 Energy and Environment, EVSC 340 Environmental Health and Safety, EVSC 375 Environmental Biology, CHEM 360 Environmental Chemistry of Air Pollution and Climate Change). The four-year path a student would read does not match the discipline, which is a serious coherence problem.
dimensions.suggestion: Replace the mis-attributed chemistry plan with the real environmental-science plan of study, sequencing EVSC 125 -> EVSC 375 Environmental Biology -> EVSC 325/340 and CHEM 360 alongside the chemistry support courses.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The environmental courses that exist have sensible, shallow prerequisites (EVSC 325 Energy and Environment requires CHEM 125 and PHYS 111; EVSC 340 requires CHEM 126; CHEM 360 requires CHEM 126/122), and deepestPrereqChain=6 is reasonable. But the program has the highest dangling count of this set (missingCourseCount=12: BIOL 202, BIOL 206, CHEM 125A, CHEM 126A, CHEM 210, GER 200, GER 300, MATH 225, PHYS 102A, PHYS 103A, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A), and the mismatch between the chemistry-based plan and the EVSC course pool makes the true prerequisite backbone impossible to verify.
dimensions.suggestion: Once the correct EVSC plan is in place, verify the prerequisite chain through the environmental courses and resolve the 12 dangling lab/GER/stats references.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives appear in the data; only a (mis-attributed) term plan and course descriptions are present.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit environmental-science learning outcomes (field/lab competency, environmental data analysis, and understanding of ecological and pollution systems).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles numerically (statedTotalCredits=120, summedTermCredits=120, creditReconcileDelta=0), but the reconciliation is misleading: it balances because the displayed plan is the Chemistry degree's, so the credit distribution shown does not reflect an environmental-science major and gives no transparency into how EVSC core, chemistry support, and electives actually divide.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebuild the credit distribution around the real EVSC requirements so the 120-credit total reflects environmental-science coursework rather than the chemistry plan.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Because the plan-of-study is populated with the Chemistry sequence (down to CHEM 336 Quantum Chemistry and CHEM 340 Chemistry of Materials, neither an environmental-science requirement) and shows no EVSC courses, a reader cannot see the actual environmental-science four-year path. The environmental identity of the program is invisible in its own plan, which is the page's dominant defect.
dimensions.suggestion: Correct the plan-of-study attribution so the environmental-science courses that already exist in the catalog are the ones displayed term by term.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The environmental courses that do exist are relevant and current: CHEM 360 Environmental Chemistry of Air Pollution and Climate Change directly addresses climate, EVSC 325 Energy and Environment covers energy systems, EVSC 340 Environmental Health and Safety covers regulatory/health science, and CS 100/CS 113 provide programming useful for environmental data. But because none of this is in the required plan, and there is no visible GIS/remote-sensing, hydrology, or environmental-modeling course, the program's currency is undercut by both content gaps and the attribution problem.
dimensions.suggestion: Add GIS/remote-sensing and an environmental-modeling/data-analysis course (leveraging the CS 100/113 base) and ensure CHEM 360 and the EVSC courses are required, not just listed.

descriptionQuality.justification: The environmental and chemistry descriptions that are present are substantive and specific (EVSC 125 Fundamentals of Environmental Sciences, EVSC 325 Energy and Environment, EVSC 340 Environmental Health and Safety, CHEM 360 Environmental Chemistry of Air Pollution and Climate Change), with no thin placeholders among the environmental offerings.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: The EVSC course prerequisites are stated clearly with grade conditions (e.g., EVSC 325 requires CHEM 125 and PHYS 111 with C or better), but the overall picture is clouded by the highest dangling-reference count in the set (12) and by the plan/pool mismatch, so the effective prerequisite path for the major cannot be traced.

missingTopics.rationale: No geographic-information-systems or remote-sensing course appears for environmental-science students, despite being a core spatial-analysis skill in the field (a GIS course, BIOL 376, exists elsewhere in the catalog but not here).
Water quality is touched by EVSC 125, but there is no dedicated hydrology/water-resources course, a central environmental-science subdiscipline.
BIOL 205 and EVSC 375 Environmental Biology give an introduction, but the program lacks an upper-level ecosystem/ecology course to anchor the biological side.
EPS 202 Society, Technology, and the Environment provides context, but no dedicated environmental-policy/law course prepares students for the regulatory dimension of the profession.

emergingTrends.rationale: CHEM 360 Environmental Chemistry of Air Pollution and Climate Change is the natural anchor to expand into quantitative climate modeling.

emergingTrends.trend: Environmental data science and remote sensing
emergingTrends.rationale: CS 100/CS 113 are already in the course pool; connecting them to environmental sensor and satellite data would modernize the analytical training.
EVSC 325 Energy and Environment positions the program to grow renewable-energy and sustainability content that dominates current environmental careers.
Pairing EPS 202 with a dedicated environmental-justice/policy course reflects a fast-growing emphasis in the field.

topSuggestions: Fix the plan-of-study attribution: the displayed 8-term plan is the Chemistry degree's sequence with zero EVSC courses, so replace it with the actual environmental-science plan.
Ensure the existing environmental courses (EVSC 125/325/340/375, CHEM 360 Environmental Chemistry) are required and shown term by term, not merely listed in the pool.
Add GIS/remote-sensing and an environmental-modeling/data-analysis course (building on the CS 100/113 base) plus hydrology and upper-level ecology.
Publish explicit environmental-science learning outcomes.
Resolve the 12 dangling lab/GER/stats references once the correct plan is in place.

b-s-in-forensic-science-371.name: B.S. in Forensic Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 8-term plan is well-ordered around three parallel tracks that converge into forensic capstones: a chemistry spine (CHEM 125/126 -> 243/244 Organic -> 221/222 Analytical -> 473/475 Biochemistry), a biology spine (BIOL 200 -> 201/202 -> 205/206 -> 340 Physiology / 352 Genetics), and a forensic spine (FRSC 201 Intro -> 307 Crime Scene -> 359 Physical Methods -> 479 Forensic Biology / 480 Forensic Microscopy), with MATH 111/112/333 supplying quantitative support. Per-course depths climb smoothly from 0 to 6 and FRSC 479 (depth 6) correctly sits behind BIOL 352 Genetics, so the sequencing is genuinely dependency-driven rather than nominal.
dimensions.suggestion: Convert the two '300/400-level Forensics Elective' placeholders in Fourth Year into a named menu (e.g., FRSC 475 Forensic Chemistry, EVSC 416 Toxicology, IT 330 Computer Forensic) so the senior year is as prescribed as the underlying tracks.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain=6 is appropriate for a lab-science B.S., and the graph is dependency-honest: FRSC 307 requires FRSC 201, FRSC 359 requires 201/307, and FRSC 479 requires BIOL 352. The 8 missing references (missingCoursesSample: BIOL 202, BIOL 206, CHEM 125A, CHEM 126A, GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A) are co-requisite lab sections, physics labs, and GER placeholders rather than genuine forensic prerequisite breaks, so the core chain is intact.
dimensions.suggestion: Add catalog entries (or explicit co-requisite mappings) for the CHEM/BIOL/PHYS lab sections and resolve the GER 200/300 placeholders so no referenced code dangles.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only the term-by-term plan and per-course descriptions are present. For a laboratory B.S. that feeds accredited crime-lab careers, the absence of stated competencies (chain-of-custody, evidence integrity, analytical validation, expert reporting) is a real catalog finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit program learning outcomes framed around forensic competencies (evidence handling, analytical method validation, interpretation, and courtroom communication), ideally mapped to FEPAC accreditation criteria.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles cleanly: statedTotalCredits=120, summedTermCredits=120, and creditReconcileDelta=0.0, with a transparent split across gen-ed (ENGL, MATH, PHYS, GERs), a heavy science core (CHEM/BIOL), and the FRSC major sequence. The only softness is that several rows carry null credits (Computing GER, History/Humanities GERs, the 'Select one of the following' co-op/research/seminar block) even though the term totals still balance.
dimensions.suggestion: Assign explicit credit values to the null-credit GER and 'select one' rows so every line item is accounted for, not just the term subtotals.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled by year and semester and the science sequence is legible, but the page has rough edges: the 'R120 356 Molecular Biology' row carries a null code (a Rutgers-legacy cross-listing), the Fourth-Year 'Select one of the following:' block lists FRSC 490/491/495 with null credits, and generic 'Forensics Elective I/II' placeholders leave the senior path visually open.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean up the legacy 'R120' rows into resolved course codes and render the 'select one' capstone options as a labeled choice group with credits shown.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The required core is strong on classical wet-lab forensics (FRSC 307 Crime Scene, 359 Physical/trace Methods, 479 Forensic Biology covering serology and DNA, 480 Forensic Microscopy) backed by real analytical chemistry (CHEM 221/222). The currency gaps are in the required plan itself: forensic chemistry/drug analysis (FRSC 475), forensic toxicology (EVSC 416), digital/multimedia forensics, and statistical interpretation of evidence are electives-or-absent rather than required, even though these dominate modern casework and post-PCAST scrutiny.
dimensions.suggestion: Require at least one of forensic chemistry/toxicology and one statistics-of-evidence module so every graduate leaves with drug-analysis exposure and defensible interpretation skills.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present and substantive across the FRSC spine (307 Crime Scene, 359 Physical Methods, 479 Forensic Biology, 480 Forensic Microscopy) and the CHEM/BIOL core; the main gap is BIOL 352 Genetics, whose descriptionExcerpt is empty despite gating FRSC 479.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with explicit grade conditions (typically 'C or better') and clear course logic, and the dangling references (missingCourseCount=8) are lab co-requisites, physics labs, and GER placeholders rather than forensic-course breaks; the recurring 'R120' Rutgers-legacy alternatives are the one readability wrinkle.

missingTopics.rationale: The plan has MATH 333 Probability & Statistics but no course on likelihood ratios, error rates, or interpretation of pattern/DNA evidence, which the 2009 NAS and 2016 PCAST reports made foundational to defensible testimony.

The required core covers biology (FRSC 479) and microscopy (FRSC 480) but leaves drug chemistry/toxicology (FRSC 475, EVSC 416) as unrequired electives, despite toxicology and seized-drug analysis being among the highest-volume crime-lab disciplines.
No digital-evidence course (e.g., IT 330 Computer Forensic) sits in the required plan even though phones, cloud data, and multimedia are now central to nearly every investigation.

missingTopics.topic: Quality assurance, accreditation, and expert testimony
missingTopics.rationale: FRSC 495 Senior Seminar touches professional practice, but there is no dedicated treatment of ISO 17025/FEPAC quality systems, proficiency testing, or courtroom testimony that accredited labs require.

missingTopics.topic: Crime-scene reconstruction and pattern evidence breadth
missingTopics.rationale: FRSC 307/359 introduce trace and impression evidence, but bloodstain-pattern analysis and shooting/scene reconstruction are not separately developed.

emergingTrends.trend: Forensic genetic genealogy and massively parallel sequencing
emergingTrends.rationale: FRSC 479 Forensic Biology teaches serology and STR DNA; investigative genetic genealogy and next-generation sequencing are now transforming cold-case and identification work and should modernize its DNA content.
Booking-station Rapid DNA and handheld spectrometers are moving analysis out of the lab; FRSC 307/359 should expose students to point-of-collection instrumentation.

emergingTrends.trend: Validation and error-rate reform of pattern evidence
emergingTrends.rationale: Post-PCAST scrutiny of fingerprint, firearm, and impression evidence demands empirical error rates; FRSC 359/480 should incorporate validation studies and uncertainty reporting.
Probabilistic genotyping software and machine-learning triage are entering casework; graduates need to understand, validate, and testify about algorithmic tools.

emergingTrends.trend: Toxicology of novel psychoactive substances and the opioid crisis
emergingTrends.rationale: Fentanyl analogs and designer drugs are reshaping seized-drug and toxicology workloads; a forensic chemistry/toxicology requirement would keep the program current.

topSuggestions: Require at least one forensic chemistry/toxicology course and add a statistics-of-evidence module so every graduate has drug-analysis exposure and defensible-interpretation skills.
Publish explicit, FEPAC-aligned program learning outcomes (evidence handling, method validation, interpretation, courtroom communication).
Add or require digital/multimedia forensics (e.g., IT 330 Computer Forensic) to reflect the ubiquity of digital evidence.
Name the two senior Forensics Elective menus and modernize FRSC 479's DNA content toward next-generation sequencing and forensic genetic genealogy.
Resolve the 8 dangling lab/GER references and assign credits to the null-credit GER and 'select one' capstone rows so the page is fully complete.

b-s-in-human-computer-interaction-247.name: B.S. in Human-Computer Interaction

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The plan builds a genuinely coherent HCI identity: a UX research spine (IS 247 Designing the User Experience -> IS 375 Discovering User Needs -> IS 448 Usability & Measuring UX), a psychology/methods track (PSY 210 plus cross-listed R830 Cognitive Processes, Statistical Methods, and Experimental Methods), a human-factors requirement (IE 355 or AD 201), a design intro (AD 150), and a technical base (CS 100, IS 117, IS 218, IS 331) converging on an IS 491/IT 491 capstone. Progression is well-ordered. The weakness is that the technical/build side stops at web development (IS 218) with no interaction-implementation course, so the degree leans research-heavy relative to making.
dimensions.suggestion: Insert a prototyping/interaction-design studio between IS 247 and IS 448 so students who learn to research and evaluate interfaces also learn to build interactive prototypes, not only web pages.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sensible: deepestPrereqChain is 7 (IS 448 Usability & Measuring UX, an appropriate terminal for the UX research sequence) with foundational courses at shallow depths and no evidence of over-constraining. missingCourseCount is 3 and all are peripheral -- GER 300 is a general-education placeholder and YWCC 207/YWCC 307 are one-credit professional-development seminars listed in the plan but absent from the course catalog.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the three dangling references by cataloguing YWCC 207/YWCC 307 and re-tagging GER 300 as a requirement category rather than a course code.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes, objectives, or overview are present in the data. For an interdisciplinary HCI degree spanning computing, psychology, and design, the absence of an explicit competency statement is especially costly because a student cannot see how the three strands are meant to add up.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 learning outcomes covering user research, interaction/interface design, usability evaluation, cognitive/psychological foundations, and ethical design, plus a short overview of the computing/psychology/design blend.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles (statedTotalCredits 120, summedTermCredits 120, creditReconcileDelta 0.0) and term loads are steady at 14-16 credits. However, transparency is weakened by heavy reliance on unspecified placeholders -- four 'HCI Specialization Electives' plus roughly eight 'General Elective' slots -- and by cross-institution R830 psychology courses whose 3-4 credit values sit oddly against the otherwise uniform 3-credit rows, making the true required content harder to read.
dimensions.suggestion: Enumerate the four HCI specialization electives (or link an approved list) and label the R830 psychology courses' credit values explicitly so the balance between required HCI content and open electives is visible.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are labeled and the core HCI courses render cleanly, but the cross-listed Rutgers-Newark psychology courses appear as null-code title rows with registrar abbreviations ('R830 301 Stat Meth Cog & Beh', 'R830 304 Cognitive Processes', 'R830 102 Prin Of Psychology'), several 'Select one of the following:' and 'or ...' options are unresolved lines, and GER rows are split with null credits.
dimensions.suggestion: Give the R830 cross-listed courses proper codes/titles (or map them to NJIT equivalents), and collapse the 'Select one of the following' and 'or ...' groupings into single readable choice lines.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum reflects current UX practice strongly: dedicated user-research (IS 375), usability and UX measurement (IS 448), human factors (IE 355), and a solid cognitive/experimental-methods foundation give graduates the research toolkit UX teams hire for. The visible gaps are modern-tooling and craft rather than concept: there is no explicit accessibility course, no prototyping/design-systems studio (Figma), and no front-end implementation depth, so the degree is stronger on evaluating experiences than on designing and shipping them.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an accessibility/inclusive-design course and a prototyping studio using current tools (Figma, design systems) to complement the strong research core with production-ready design skills.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 24 sampled courses carry present, informative descriptions with no placeholders; the HCI core is especially rich (IS 247 on interface design/evaluation, IS 375 framed around anticipating user needs, IS 448 on UX research and usability, IE 355 human-machine systems). A handful of GER/math rows are terser, but no description reads as thin or empty.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with clear grade gates and sensible logic (IS 218: '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'; COM 312/313 with explicit ENGL 102 + GER conditions), and intro courses correctly show none. Clarity is dinged only by the cross-listed R830 psychology courses, whose prerequisites are not surfaced in NJIT terms, and by minor phrasing variance.

missingTopics.rationale: No course addresses accessibility, yet it is now a legal and professional baseline for UX; it should be a required complement to IS 247/IS 448 rather than absent.
The program teaches research and evaluation (IS 375/IS 448) but no dedicated hands-on interaction-design/prototyping course, leaving graduates strong on 'why' but light on 'how to build'.
Technical build stops at IS 218 web development; HCI graduates increasingly need to prototype interactive, stateful interfaces, not static pages.

missingTopics.topic: Quantitative UX / experimentation and A/B testing at scale
missingTopics.rationale: IS 448 covers usability measurement, but modern product UX relies on large-sample experimentation and behavioral analytics that the current lab-oriented R830 stats track does not reach.
A core HCI competency for structuring complex systems is not explicitly represented among the IS/design courses.

emergingTrends.trend: Designing for generative AI and human-AI interaction
emergingTrends.rationale: Prompt UX, conversational flows, and trust/uncertainty in AI features are the fastest-growing UX area; the program's strong research core (IS 375/IS 448) is ideally placed to add an AI-interaction elective.
Voice and multimodal UX are mainstream in consumer and enterprise products, extending the interface-design scope of IS 247 beyond screens.
Spatial interfaces are a major near-future direction for HCI; a specialization elective here would future-proof the design track.
Building on IS 350 ethics, ethical/persuasive-design scrutiny is now central to UX hiring and regulation.
UX research is professionalizing into ops and analytics-driven practice, a natural extension of IS 375/IS 448 into tooling and repositories.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit HCI learning outcomes and a program overview that makes the computing/psychology/design blend legible.
Add a required accessibility/inclusive-design course -- the single most conspicuous gap for a modern UX degree.
Add an interaction-design/prototyping studio (Figma, design systems) plus front-end prototyping to balance the research-heavy core.
Introduce a 'designing for AI / conversational interaction' elective that leverages the strong existing UX-research sequence.
Enumerate the HCI specialization electives and clean the cross-listed R830 psychology rows so required content is visible.

b-s-in-industrial-design-202.name: B.S. in Industrial Design

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The course list reveals a coherent, well-built industrial-design spine: AD foundations (AD 111/112/150, AD 161->162) feed the ID studio ladder ID 263 -> 264 -> 363 -> 364 -> 464 (deepestPrereqChain 5), supported by ID 216->217 (modeling/prototyping then manufacturing), ID 340->341 (materials then sustainable materials), ID 310 research, ID 312 mechanics/electronics, and ID 410 professional practice. However, only 1 requirement group parsed (a single '2nd Semester'), so the term-over-term progression cannot be read from the plan itself and must be inferred from the course graph.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the full four-year plan of study; the underlying course sequence is strong but only one term is currently visible, hiding the progression.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy and the cleanest of this set: missingCourseCount is only 2 (GER 300 and the PHYS 102A lab), and the ID studio and support chains are sensibly gated (ID 264 <- AD 150 and ID 263; ID 363 <- ID 216, ID 217 and ID 264; ID 341 <- ID 340). The deepest chain of 5 is reasonable for a studio-based B.S.
dimensions.suggestion: Register the PHYS 102A lab co-requisite and the GER 300 category code to bring missingCourseCount to zero.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the data; only a single parsed term and the course list are present. An industrial-design B.S. should state outcomes around user-centered design, prototyping/manufacturing competence, and professional practice.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 program learning outcomes covering design research, ideation, modeling/prototyping, materials and manufacturing, and professional practice.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: creditReconcileDelta is -106 (summedTermCredits 14 vs. stated 120), but this is almost entirely a parse caveat: only one requirement group (a 14-credit '2nd Semester') was captured, so seven-or-so terms are simply absent from the sum rather than genuinely unbalanced. With a single term visible, the gen-ed/major/elective split cannot be assessed.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore the full plan of study so the summed credits reconcile to the stated 120 and the distribution across general education, the studio core, and electives becomes transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: As parsed, the page shows only one term (labeled simply '2nd Semester') containing DD 464, DD 449 (or DD 442), a design elective, and a credit-less senior-seminar GER row. There is no program description, no year labels for the other terms, and no view of the foundational studios, so the page does not convey the shape of the degree despite the strong underlying course catalog.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the complete, year-labeled plan of study with a program overview; the current single-term view badly understates a well-structured program.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The course set is current and professionally oriented: ID 341 Sustainable Materials and Processes, ID 310 Ethnographic and Marketing Research (user research), ID 312 Mechanics and Electronics (a foothold for smart products), ID 340 Materials and Processes, ID 370 New Product Testing, and ID 410 Professional Practice and Ethics together cover the modern ID workflow well. Gaps are narrower here: no explicitly named CAD/CAID software course and no dedicated service/systems-design or interaction-design-for-products offering.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an explicit CAD/CAID and design-for-manufacturing course and a service/systems-design elective to round out an already strong, current curriculum.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are consistently present, specific, and informative (ID 341 critiques traditional materials and processes for sustainability; ID 310 details ethnographic user-research methods; ID 312 addresses electronics-driven products; ID 216 covers drafting and fabrication communication). Registration restrictions are stated, and none read as thin placeholders; only excerpts are truncated.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly and consistently, with a sensible studio chain (ID 264 <- AD 150 and ID 263; ID 363 <- ID 216, ID 217 and ID 264; ID 341 <- ID 340 or ARCH 295) and correct empty prerequisites on foundation courses. With only 2 dangling references (GER 300, PHYS 102A), internal consistency is high; a minor quirk is DD 464 carrying the title 'Digital Design Studio III'.

missingTopics.rationale: ID 216/217 cover modeling and manufacturing but no course names industry CAID tools (Rhino, SolidWorks, Fusion 360, Alias); explicit software fluency is a baseline hiring expectation.
ID 340 Materials and Processes provides the base, but a dedicated DFM/DFA course would connect design decisions to production cost and feasibility.
The studios are product-object centered; contemporary ID increasingly designs product-service systems, a competency absent from the plan.
ID 312 Mechanics and Electronics opens the door to smart products, but no course covers the UI/interaction layer that connected products require.
AD 201 Human Factors and ID 310 research provide a base, but explicit inclusive/accessible-design methods are increasingly expected in professional practice.

emergingTrends.rationale: ID 341 Sustainable Materials is a strong anchor; extending it toward circular-economy and lifecycle-assessment methods would put the program at the field's leading edge.
ID 312 Mechanics and Electronics is a foothold; adding embedded/IoT and interaction content would align the program with the connected-product market.
Generative design and AI ideation tools are entering ID practice; the studio and modeling courses are natural places to introduce them.
ID 216/217 prototyping should evolve toward advanced additive/digital-fabrication methods now standard in product development.

emergingTrends.trend: Design for health, aging, and accessibility
emergingTrends.rationale: ID 310's user research and AD 201 human factors position the program to lead on inclusive design for aging and healthcare, a growing market.

topSuggestions: Publish the complete, year-labeled plan of study; only a single '2nd Semester' term is currently parsed, hiding an otherwise strong four-year sequence and driving the -106 credit delta.
Publish explicit program learning outcomes (none are currently present).
Add an explicit CAD/CAID and design-for-manufacturing course so software and production fluency are named requirements rather than assumed.
Build on ID 341 and ID 312 with circular-design and smart-product/IoT content to occupy the sustainability and connected-product frontier.
Register the 2 dangling references (GER 300, PHYS 102A) to bring the course graph to full consistency.

b-s-in-industrial-engineering-625.name: B.S. in Industrial Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 8-term plan builds a coherent IE progression: a math/science/engineering-science foundation (MATH 111/112/211/222, PHYS 111/121, CHEM 121/122, MECH 320 statics, MECH 236 dynamics, ME 339 mechanical design, ECE 405 EE principles) feeds the IE core, statistics (IE 331) into the operations-research sequence (IE 439 deterministic -> IE 440 stochastic), simulation (IE 445), quality (IE 461), supply chain (IE 459), and facilities/material handling (IE 466), culminating in the IE 443/444 senior project, with prerequisite depths rising to 6. Progression is logical and well ordered.
dimensions.suggestion: Group the three 'IE Technical Elective' slots into named tracks (e.g., analytics/OR, quality/manufacturing, human factors/ergonomics) so the senior electives read as intentional specialization.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sensible with a moderate deepestPrereqChain of 6 appropriate to IE, and the 3 missing references (missingCoursesSample: GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A) are all GER/lab placeholders rather than genuine dangling courses. However, many IE courses gate on class standing ('Restriction: junior standing') with an empty course prerequisite (12 of 37 sampled courses have no course prereq), and a few prereqs are inconsistent with the plan, IE 445 lists 'CS 101' though the plan requires CS 115, and ME 339/MECH 236 reference MECH 234 though the plan carries MECH 320.
dimensions.suggestion: Convert the standing-based restrictions to explicit course prerequisites where a genuine dependency exists, and fix the CS 101-vs-CS 115 and MECH 234-vs-MECH 320 mismatches so prerequisites match the plan's actual courses.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or ABET student outcomes appear in the provided data for this accredited IE degree; only the plan of study and course descriptions are present.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit IE program educational objectives and student outcomes (systems modeling, statistical analysis, design of operations, professional responsibility) to support students and ABET reporting.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: summedTermCredits 120 equals statedTotalCredits 120 with creditReconcileDelta 0.0, and terms are evenly loaded at 14-17 credits. The distribution transparently balances math/science, engineering-science breadth (statics, dynamics, mechanical design, EE principles), the IE core, general-education/humanities, and three IE technical electives.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a credit-summary table (math & science vs. engineering-science vs. IE core vs. GER vs. technical electives) so the balanced split is visible at a glance.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are labeled and IE courses are legible, but the plan carries artifacts: a stray cross-program 'None 626 B.S. in Mechanical Engineering' line in Year 3, a 'Select one of the following:' humanities block (HUM 211/212, HIST 213) whose options show null credits, and footnote markers left inline (MATH 211 '*', CHEM 122 '**') without visible footnotes.
dimensions.suggestion: Remove the cross-program '626 B.S. in Mechanical Engineering' artifact line, render the humanities 'select one' options as labeled entries with credits, and surface or drop the '*'/'**' footnotes.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The core reflects classical industrial-engineering practice well, deterministic and stochastic operations research (IE 439/440), simulation (IE 445), statistical quality (IE 461), supply chain and production planning (IE 459), facilities and material handling (IE 466), human factors (IE 355), and engineering economy (IE 334/335). Currency gaps are notable, however: the only programming is CS 115 in C++ (not a data-analytics language), there is no dedicated data-analytics/machine-learning-for-operations course, no explicit Lean/Six Sigma course (IE 461 is classical SQC), and the plan is manufacturing-centric with little service/healthcare-systems content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a data-analytics course in Python/R for operations and a Lean/Six Sigma course, and modernize IE 445 (simulation) and IE 441 (information & knowledge engineering) toward digital-twin and analytics tooling.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are present and specific (IE 439 on deterministic OR with linear/nonlinear/integer programming, IE 440 on Markov chains and queueing, IE 461 on quality methods and defect prevention, IE 445 on simulation modeling of industrial systems). Only a recurring 'Restriction: ... standing' preface and some truncated entries slightly reduce information density.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Where course prerequisites are given they are explicit with grade conditions (e.g., IE 339 'IE 203, IE 224', IE 461 'IE 331', CHEM 122 'CHEM 121 with a grade C or better'), but clarity is uneven: 12 of 37 sampled courses gate only on class standing with no course prerequisite, and several prereqs cite courses the plan does not use (IE 445 requires 'CS 101' while the plan takes CS 115; ME 339 and MECH 236 cite MECH 234 while the plan carries MECH 320), leaving internally inconsistent references.

missingTopics.rationale: IE 331 (statistics) and CS 115 (C++) do not cover modern analytics tooling; a Python/R data-analytics course would let IE graduates work with the data pipelines that now drive operations decisions.
IE 461 covers classical statistical quality control but there is no explicit Lean/Six Sigma course, the dominant process-improvement methodology in industry certifications and hiring.

missingTopics.topic: Machine learning and prescriptive analytics for operations
missingTopics.rationale: The OR sequence (IE 439/440) is classical optimization; adding ML/prescriptive-analytics would modernize how students forecast demand and optimize under uncertainty.

The plan is manufacturing-centric (IE 224 production, IE 466 material handling, IE 459 supply chain); a service-systems or healthcare-systems course would broaden IE beyond the factory floor where much IE hiring now occurs.
