There is no sustainability content; a green-manufacturing or life-cycle-analysis course would connect facilities and supply-chain work (IE 459/466) to environmental objectives.

emergingTrends.rationale: Smart manufacturing with IoT sensing and digital twins is reshaping the field; IE 224 (production process) and IE 445 (simulation) are ideal to modernize with digital-twin and cyber-physical content.

emergingTrends.trend: Data science and ML in operations
emergingTrends.rationale: Operations decisions are increasingly data-driven; extending IE 440 (stochastic models) and IE 441 (information & knowledge engineering) toward machine learning would keep the analytics core current.
Post-pandemic disruption has made resilience central; IE 459 (supply chain and production planning) is the natural host for risk-analytics and network-resilience methods.
Robots and AI are changing work design; IE 355 (human factors) is positioned to address human-automation interaction and cobots in modern operations.
Environmental and ESG pressures are reshaping operations; sustainability metrics could be woven into IE 459/466 to make green systems a program strength.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit IE program educational objectives and ABET student outcomes, which are absent from the page.
Add a data-analytics (Python/R) course and a Lean/Six Sigma course to close the two biggest currency gaps in an otherwise strong classical IE core.

Fix the internally inconsistent prerequisites (IE 445's CS 101 vs. the plan's CS 115; ME 339/MECH 236's MECH 234 vs. the plan's MECH 320) and convert standing-only gates to explicit course prerequisites.
Modernize IE 445 (simulation) and IE 441 (information & knowledge engineering) toward digital-twin, IoT, and analytics tooling for Industry 4.0.
Remove the cross-program '626 B.S. in Mechanical Engineering' artifact line and render the humanities 'select one' block and '*'/'**' footnotes cleanly.

b-s-in-information-technology-254.name: B.S. in Information Technology

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: This is the most technically coherent of the informatics degrees: a programming arc (CS 100 -> CS 113 -> IT 114 Advanced Programming), a networking/systems arc (IT 120 -> IT 340 System Administration and IT 420 Computer Systems & Networks -> IT 490 Systems Integration), a data arc (IS 331 database), and design/apps (IT 201, IT 202) all converge on an IT 491 capstone. Term loads are steady at 13-16 credits and difficulty ramps sensibly. The only softness is the large block of eight generic 'Specialization Course' slots whose sequencing/content is not shown, so the concentration's internal progression is invisible.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the named specialization tracks with their ordered course lists so the eight 'Specialization Course' slots read as a coherent concentration rather than interchangeable placeholders.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is well-formed and slightly shallower than its IS siblings: deepestPrereqChain is 6 (IT 490 Systems Integration, gated on CS 113/115, IS 331/CS 331, and IT 340 -- a sensible terminal), with clear intermediate chains (IT 340/IT 420 on IT 120 or CS 356). missingCourseCount is 4 and all are peripheral -- GER 200/GER 300 placeholders and the one-credit YWCC 207/YWCC 307 seminars.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the four dangling references (catalog YWCC 207/YWCC 307; re-tag GER 200/GER 300 as requirement categories) so the otherwise clean IT prerequisite graph has no loose ends.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes, objectives, or overview are present in the data. Given IT's ABET-style emphasis on applied competencies, the absence of a stated outcomes list is a meaningful catalog gap.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 learning outcomes covering programming, networking/system administration, database, systems integration, and professional practice, plus a short program overview.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: Credit accounting reconciles cleanly (statedTotalCredits 120, summedTermCredits 120, creditReconcileDelta 0.0), and the required IT core is substantial and well-defined (IT 101/114/120/201/202/340/420/490 plus CS 100/113, IS 331/350). The transparency caveat is the large open block -- eight 'Specialization Courses' plus roughly seven 'General Electives' -- which is a lot of undefined credit even though the core anchors the degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Show a credit-distribution summary and label the specialization block's credit total so the split between the defined IT core and the ~24 credits of specialization/free electives is explicit.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Cleaner than the IS B.A. -- fewer footnote artifacts -- but the same class of defects remains: GER rows are split with null credits ('Science Literacy GER', 'History and Humanities GER 300+ level'), the COM alternative renders as a null-code 'or COM 312 or Oral Presentations' line, eight identical 'Specialization Course' placeholders carry no content, and YWCC titles are abbreviated ('Computing & Effective Com').
dimensions.suggestion: Collapse the GER and 'or ...' rows into single readable lines and either name or link the eight specialization slots so the plan is not dominated by identical placeholders.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The core covers durable IT skills -- programming (IT 114), networking (IT 120/IT 420), system administration (IT 340), internet applications (IT 202), systems integration (IT 490), and database (IS 331) -- but it reads as traditional on-premises IT. The single most conspicuous gap is the absence of any cybersecurity course despite a networking-and-sysadmin backbone that is the ideal foundation for it; there is also no cloud/DevOps, virtualization/containers, or infrastructure-automation content, all now central to IT roles.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a required cybersecurity course (network/systems security) and a cloud-computing/DevOps course, so the networking + sysadmin base leads into the security and cloud skills employers now demand.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 19 sampled courses carry present, specific descriptions with no missing/placeholder entries (e.g., IT 340 system administration, IT 420 computer systems and networks, IT 490 systems integration, IS 331 database design). Content is informative and course-appropriate; only a few registrar titles are terse.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with explicit grade gates and clean alternatives (IT 114: 'CS 113 or CS 115'; IT 202: 'CS 100 or CS 113 or CS 115 or a course in a high-level programming language as approved by department'; IT 490: 'CS 113 or CS 115, IS 331 or CS 331, and IT 340'), and intro courses correctly show none. The consistent, well-formed chains make this the clearest prerequisite set among the informatics degrees; minor phrasing variance keeps it from a 5.

missingTopics.rationale: A program built on networking (IT 120/IT 420) and system administration (IT 340) with no security course has a glaring gap; security is now inseparable from every IT operations role and should be a required course.

missingTopics.topic: Cloud computing and virtualization (AWS/Azure, containers, Kubernetes)
missingTopics.rationale: IT 340/IT 420/IT 490 are on-premises-centric; modern IT infrastructure is cloud and container based, so a cloud/virtualization course is essential currency.
IT 114 teaches application programming, but operational automation (infrastructure-as-code, pipelines) that defines contemporary IT work is absent.
Systems integration (IT 490) would benefit from ITSM practices and modern monitoring/observability that IT operations roles rely on daily.
Enterprise IT increasingly manages mobile and IoT endpoints, a natural extension of the networking/sysadmin core not currently represented.

emergingTrends.rationale: The sysadmin/systems-integration roles this program trains for are shifting to cloud-native DevOps/SRE; IT 340/IT 490 should evolve toward automated, cloud-based operations.
Security is now central to every IT function; the program's networking + sysadmin base is the ideal launchpad for a zero-trust-oriented security track.
LLM-assisted troubleshooting and automated incident response are reshaping IT operations, a forward-looking addition to IT 490 systems integration.
Declarative, versioned infrastructure (Terraform/Ansible) is now standard practice and a direct modernization of the IT 340 system-administration content.
Distributed edge/IoT infrastructure extends the networking curriculum (IT 120/IT 420) into a fast-growing operational domain.

topSuggestions: Add a required cybersecurity course -- the largest gap given the networking and system-administration backbone.
Add cloud-computing/DevOps with virtualization/containers and infrastructure-as-code to modernize the on-prem-centric core.
Publish program-level learning outcomes and an overview.
Name and sequence the eight 'Specialization Course' slots into published tracks so the concentration's progression is visible.
Clean presentation: collapse split GER and 'or ...' rows and replace identical specialization placeholders with real course listings.

b-s-in-science-technology-society-and-b-.name: B.S. in Science, Technology & Society and B.S. in Business and Information Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The information-systems spine is coherent and genuinely vertical (CS 100->113->114, IS 117->218->219->421, IS 331->IS 491 senior project) with a deepestPrereqChain of 7, and business courses (ACCT 115/117, ECON 201, FIN 218/315, HRM 301, ENTR 210, IE 492) interleave sensibly. However, only one plan-of-study term parsed (requirementGroups=1, the '2nd Semester' block), so term-over-term ordering cannot be verified, and for a dual degree whose first half is Science, Technology & Society the STS/policy/social-studies-of-technology content is almost invisible in the course set, represented only by IS 350 Computers, Society and Ethics.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore the full eight-term plan-of-study grid and explicitly slot the STS half (history/philosophy of science, technology policy, science-and-society courses) so the dual degree reads as an integrated program rather than a BIS track with a missing companion.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is well-formed and readable, with grade-conditioned prerequisites (e.g., 'CS 113 with a grade C or better', 'IS 219 and (IS 331 or CS 331)') and per-course depths from 0 to 7. missingCourseCount is only 3, and all three (YWCC 107, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) are one-credit college seminars that dangle without breaking any core IS chain; the depth-7 path into IS 465/IS 448 is on the deep side but genuine.
dimensions.suggestion: Catalog the three YWCC 107/207/307 seminars so they resolve, and audit the depth-7 chain into IS 465 (statistics GUR -> ... ) to confirm it does not over-constrain scheduling in an already dual-major load.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: The program data contains no program-level learning outcomes, educational objectives, or narrative overview of any kind; only the course list and a single parsed term are present. For a dual degree that fuses a humanities/social-science field (STS) with an applied IS field, the absence of outcomes is especially costly because the integrative value proposition is left entirely implicit.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-7 program-level learning outcomes that explicitly bridge the two halves (e.g., analyze the societal and ethical impact of information systems, design and build business web/mobile applications, evaluate technology policy with data).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan does not reconcile: summedTermCredits is only 12.0 against a statedTotalCredits of 120.0, giving a creditReconcileDelta of -108.0 because just one 12-credit term parsed. Beyond the parsing gap, 120 credits is itself suspect for a genuine dual B.S./B.S. degree, which typically requires substantially more, so the reader cannot see how general-education, STS, business, and IS credits are apportioned.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebuild the full credit grid and confirm the true total for the dual degree, then add a distribution summary (general education / STS core / business core / IS core / electives) so the 120 (or corrected) figure is transparent and reconciles term by term.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Only one term rendered (the '2nd Semester' group), several rows carry null course codes, and alternatives appear as bare text such as 'or IT 491 or IT Capstone Project'; there is no program description, no outcomes, and no STS-side structure. The single parsed block therefore reads as a fragment rather than a catalog-quality plan.
dimensions.suggestion: Fix the plan-of-study parsing so all terms render with labeled headings and per-term credit totals, collapse 'or' alternatives into clear either/or pairs, and add a short program overview describing the STS+BIS pairing.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The BIS half is current and employable (IS 218 web apps, IS 322 mobile, IS 331 databases, IS 333 social network analysis, IS 390 requirements analysis, IS 455 IS management, IT 310 e-commerce, IS 247/375 UX), but for a program explicitly branded Science, Technology & Society the currency of the STS half is thin: only IS 350 touches ethics, and there is no data governance, technology policy, privacy law, or applied data-science/AI content despite this being exactly where STS+IS careers now concentrate.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or list electives in technology policy / data governance and applied data science or AI so the STS half carries modern currency (algorithmic accountability, privacy regulation) rather than resting on a single ethics course.

descriptionQuality.justification: The sampled courses carry real, specific descriptions rather than placeholders (e.g., ACCT 115 develops 'fundamentals of financial accounting'; IS 333 teaches programs that 'grab information from social networking systems ... and analyze this to reveal useful but hidden information'). Excerpts are truncated at ~220 characters, and a few are genuinely thin (IS 489 INFO Undergrad Thesis Research at ~81 characters), but coverage is otherwise strong and informative.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated in clear, human-readable form with explicit grade conditions and boolean structure (e.g., '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'), and the parser resolved a clean depth graph. The only blemish is the 3 dangling YWCC 107/207/307 seminar references, which are peripheral one-credit courses rather than gatekeepers.

missingTopics.topic: Technology and science policy / STS methods
missingTopics.rationale: For a degree whose first half is Science, Technology & Society, the course set shows no policy analysis, history/philosophy of science, or qualitative social-science methods course; IS 350 alone cannot carry the STS half, so a dedicated STS core (e.g., a Technology, Policy and Society course) is needed.

missingTopics.topic: Applied data analytics and machine learning
missingTopics.rationale: The IS track stops at IS 333 social network analysis and IS 392 web mining; there is no applied ML, predictive modeling, or Python/R data-science course, which is now standard for information-systems graduates and directly relevant to an STS student studying data-driven society.

No cybersecurity, privacy, or data-governance course appears despite IS 218/322/331 building data-collecting web, mobile, and database systems; STS+BIS graduates working at the technology-society boundary need GDPR/CCPA-style privacy and security engineering literacy.

Only MATH 105 (elementary probability and statistics) is visible; an STS+IS program that analyzes technology in society should require inferential statistics and research design (e.g., MATH 333) so students can produce, not just consume, empirical claims.

The web sequence (IS 117/218/219/421) is traditional server-side web development with no cloud, DevOps, or API/microservice content, leaving a gap between the program's build courses and how business information systems are actually deployed today.

emergingTrends.rationale: This is the fastest-growing STS-meets-technology niche and the natural destination for STS+BIS graduates; the program should extend IS 350 into a dedicated responsible-AI / algorithmic-accountability course to own this space.

Privacy law (GDPR, CCPA, state AI acts) is reshaping how information systems are built and operated; a program that combines societal analysis with hands-on IS development is ideally positioned to teach privacy-by-design if it adds a privacy/compliance elective.

LLM-assisted development and AI-embedded business software are now baseline industry practice; IS 218/322/421 build courses should incorporate AI-assisted and AI-feature development so graduates ship current systems.

emergingTrends.trend: Platform and social-media analytics for policy
emergingTrends.rationale: IS 333 social network analysis is a strong foundation the program can grow into misinformation, platform governance, and computational social science, uniting the STS and IS halves around a marketable, high-demand skill set.

With IS 247/375/448 already present, the program can position for value-sensitive and ethical design, an area where STS framing plus UX practice is a distinctive combination employers increasingly seek.

topSuggestions: Rebuild the full plan-of-study grid; only one 12-credit term parsed (creditReconcileDelta -108 against a stated 120), so students currently cannot see the degree structure at all.
Make the Science, Technology & Society half explicit by slotting STS/policy/history-of-science courses, since the visible curriculum is almost entirely BIS with only IS 350 representing STS.
Publish program-level learning outcomes that bridge societal analysis and applied IS, none of which currently appear in the data.
Add modern data and policy currency: an applied data-science/ML course and a technology-policy or data-privacy course, closing the biggest STS-side and IS-side gaps at once.
Catalog the YWCC 107/207/307 seminars and verify the true dual-degree credit total so the page reconciles and dangling references disappear.

b-s-in-web-information-systems-258.name: B.S. in Web & Information Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The web-development spine is genuinely strong and well-sequenced (IS 117 -> IS 218 -> IS 219 Advanced Website Development -> IS 421 Advanced Web Applications), reinforced by a CS base (CS 100 -> CS 113 -> CS 114/IT 114), a UX thread (IS 247/IS 375/IS 448), database (IS 331), and a rich set of web-relevant courses (IS 322 Mobile Apps, IS 373 Content Management Systems, IS 392 Web Mining & IR, IS 333 Social Network Analysis). Coherence is materially hurt, however, by the plan being truncated: only 7 requirement groups parsed and the Fourth Year 2nd Semester -- the culminating term and any capstone (IS 491) -- is entirely absent, so the progression does not visibly finish.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore the missing Fourth Year 2nd Semester, including the capstone, so the strong web-development sequence reads as a completed four-year progression rather than stopping mid-senior-year.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sensible and notably rich (75 referenced courses): deepestPrereqChain is 7 (IS 333 Social Network Analysis, gated on computing and statistical GERs), with the web sequence forming clean intermediate chains (IS 322: 'IS 218, IS 219, or IT 202'; IS 421 advanced web on the IS 218/219 base). missingCourseCount is 4 and all are peripheral -- GER 200/GER 300 placeholders and the one-credit YWCC 207/YWCC 307 seminars.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the four dangling references (catalog YWCC 207/YWCC 307; re-tag GER 200/GER 300 as requirement categories) so the deep, well-formed web/IS graph has no loose ends.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes, objectives, or overview are present in the data, so a prospective student cannot see what distinguishes 'Web & Information Systems' from the general IS degree beyond the course titles themselves.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish learning outcomes emphasizing full-stack web development, content/data-driven web systems, UX, and web analytics, plus a short overview positioning the degree against the general B.S./B.A. in IS.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: Credit accounting cannot be reconciled: statedTotalCredits is null, creditReconcileDelta is null, and summedTermCredits is only 108 -- roughly a full term (~12 credits) short of a typical 120-credit B.S. This directly reflects the missing Fourth Year 2nd Semester, so both the program total and the final-term credits are unaccounted for. This is a genuine credit-transparency defect and a data-consistency caveat, not merely a rounding issue.
dimensions.suggestion: Parse and display a single stated total credit count and restore the missing final term so the summed term credits reconcile to it (currently null total and only 108 summed credits).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The most serious presentation defect is the truncated plan -- only seven terms render and the final semester is missing entirely -- which alone undermines completeness. On top of that, GER rows are split with null credits, alternatives render as null-code 'or COM 313 or Technical Writing' lines, footnote numbers bleed into titles ('General Elective 3 2'), and several titles are abbreviated ('IS 219 Adv Website Development', 'Computing & Effective Com'), so the page reads as incomplete and noisy.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore the missing final term first, then merge split GER and 'or ...' rows, strip footnote numbers from titles, and expand truncated course titles.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: This is the most web-current of the informatics degrees: a genuine full web progression (IS 117/218/219/421) plus mobile applications (IS 322), content management systems (IS 373), web mining and information retrieval (IS 392), and social network analysis (IS 333) map well onto contemporary web-engineering and web-data roles. The gaps are modern-stack specifics rather than concept: no explicit modern JavaScript-framework/API course, no web/application security, and no cloud/serverless deployment, so graduates may be strong on breadth but light on the exact production stack.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a modern full-stack course (JavaScript framework + REST/GraphQL APIs) and a web-application-security course, and update IS 219/IS 421 to reflect current framework and deployment practice.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 38 sampled courses carry present, specific descriptions with no missing/placeholder entries across a broad set (IS web courses, CS foundations, DD design, COM communication, IS 333 social-network analysis, IS 392 web mining). The breadth of well-described electives is a strength; only a few registrar titles are terse.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with explicit grade gates and coherent alternatives across the web sequence (IS 218/IS 219: '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'; IS 322: 'IS 218, IS 219, or IT 202'; CS 114: 'CS 113 with a grade C or better'), and intro courses correctly show none. Minor issues -- GER-set placeholders inside some prerequisites (IS 333/IS 350) and phrasing variance -- keep it from a 5.

missingTopics.topic: Web and application security (OWASP, authentication, secure coding)
missingTopics.rationale: A web-development-heavy program (IS 218/219/421, IS 373) with no security course is a notable gap; secure web coding is a baseline expectation for any web engineer.

missingTopics.topic: Modern JavaScript frameworks and full-stack APIs (React/Node, REST/GraphQL)
missingTopics.rationale: The web sequence should explicitly teach current front-end frameworks and API design rather than leaving IS 218/219/421 to generic 'web application' framing.

missingTopics.topic: Cloud/serverless deployment and web DevOps (CI/CD, containers, CDN)
missingTopics.rationale: Building web apps without teaching how to deploy and operate them (IS 421 stops at 'advanced web applications') leaves a real production gap.
Despite a UX thread (IS 247/IS 375/IS 448), no course covers accessibility, which is a legal and professional requirement for modern web work.

missingTopics.topic: Web data engineering (NoSQL, caching, APIs at scale)
missingTopics.rationale: IS 331 covers relational databases only; web systems increasingly rely on NoSQL, caching, and API-driven data layers not represented here.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI in web systems (LLM features, RAG, AI-assisted content)
emergingTrends.rationale: AI-powered search, content, and personalization build directly on IS 373 (CMS) and IS 392 (web mining/IR), positioning graduates for the fastest-growing web capability.

emergingTrends.trend: API-first / headless CMS and JAMstack architectures
emergingTrends.rationale: IS 373 Content Management Systems should modernize toward headless/API-first delivery, the dominant contemporary web-content pattern.
IS 322 mobile applications is well placed to cover PWAs and cross-platform frameworks as web and mobile converge.

emergingTrends.trend: Social and graph analytics with recommendation systems
emergingTrends.rationale: IS 333 (social network analysis) and IS 392 (web mining) give the program a strong base to teach recommendation and graph-analytics at scale, a core web-data direction.

emergingTrends.trend: Privacy-first and decentralized web (Web3, consent-driven data)
emergingTrends.rationale: With IT 310 e-commerce and IS 333 present, the program can address privacy-by-design and emerging decentralized-web patterns reshaping web data practice.

topSuggestions: Restore the missing Fourth Year 2nd Semester (including the capstone) and fix credit accounting: statedTotalCredits and creditReconcileDelta are null and summed credits are only 108.
Add a web/application-security course -- the clearest gap in a web-development-focused degree.
Add a modern full-stack course (JavaScript framework + REST/GraphQL APIs) and update IS 219/IS 421 to current framework and deployment practice.
Publish program-level learning outcomes and an overview that positions Web & IS against the general IS degrees.
Add accessibility and cloud/serverless deployment content, then clean the split GER/'or ...' rows and footnote-laden titles.

bachelor-of-architecture-164.name: Bachelor of Architecture

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: This flagship first-professional (five-year, 150-credit) plan has an exemplary progression: the studio spine (ARCH 195 -> 196 -> 295 -> 296 -> 395 -> 396 -> 463/495 -> 561/595) advances in lockstep with parallel technical sequences (Construction I/II ARCH 223/224, Structures I/II ARCH 303/304, Environmental Control Systems I/II ARCH 309/314) and History I/II (ARCH 210/211), so representation, tectonics, and theory mature together term over term. Prerequisite depths rise smoothly from 0 to 7 with no abrupt jumps.
dimensions.suggestion: Explicitly designate the fourth/fifth-year Options and Advanced studios as an 'integrated/comprehensive' studio and state which technical courses (structures, ECS) are synthesized there, so the culminating integration is legible on the page.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is deep and sensible (deepestPrereqChain 7) and tracks the studio spine cleanly through ARCH 561 Synthesis Seminar (depth 7). Only 3 dangling references exist (missingCoursesSample: GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 102A), and all three are general-education placeholders and a physics-lab co-requisite rather than genuine architecture breaks.
dimensions.suggestion: Register the PHYS 102A General Physics I Lab entry and resolve the GER 200/300 placeholders so the metrics show zero dangling references.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only the plan of study and course description excerpts are present. For a NAAB-accredited first-professional degree this absence is a real catalog finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit program learning outcomes mapped to the NAAB Student Criteria (design synthesis, technical integration, professional practice, history/theory), which also supports accreditation reporting.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: summedTermCredits 150 equals statedTotalCredits 150 with creditReconcileDelta 0.0, and terms are evenly loaded at 14-16 credits across ten semesters. The split is transparent, balancing a deep studio/technical major core, a gen-ed block (ENGL 101/102, MATH 105/107, PHYS 102), and distributed ARCH electives labeled by category (Technology, History/Theory).
dimensions.suggestion: Add a one-line credit-summary table (studio vs. technical core vs. history/theory vs. gen-ed vs. electives) so the balanced distribution is visible without reading all ten terms.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled with per-term credits and most courses carry substantive descriptions. Legibility is reduced by parsing artifacts: placeholder rows such as 'or ARCH 566 or Advanced Architectural Design Studio' with null credits, repeated 'ARCH Elective iii' labels, and description excerpts where catalog boilerplate ('ARCH 2**. Architecture Elective. 3 credits, 3 contact hours (3;0;0)') has leaked into ARCH 196/296/396.
dimensions.suggestion: Render alternative-course rows (ARCH 464 'or ARCH 566') as single entries and strip the leaked contact-hour boilerplate from the continuation-studio descriptions.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The required core reflects durable professional practice well (Construction I/II, Structures I/II, ECS I/II passive+active, Professional Practice I/II ARCH 472/475, and a Synthesis Seminar). However digital and computational fluency is thin in the required spine: the only computing requirement is the dated CS 104 'Computer Programming and Graphics Problems,' while BIM (ARCH 337), digital fabrication (ARCH 301/335), and building-performance simulation sit only in electives, and sustainability lives largely inside ECS.
dimensions.suggestion: Make BIM (ARCH 337) and a building-performance/energy-modeling course required rather than elective, and modernize or replace CS 104 with a computational-design course.

descriptionQuality.justification: Core descriptions are specific and informative (ARCH 210/211 History, ARCH 223/224 Construction, ARCH 303/304 Structures, ARCH 309/314 ECS all give genuine topic content). Quality is dragged down by the continuation studios (ARCH 196 'A continuation of ARCH 195. ARCH 2**...', ARCH 296, ARCH 395 'This course is a continuation of ARCH 296.') that are thin or carry leaked catalog boilerplate.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Most prerequisites are explicit and consistent, but several compound raw strings mix 'and'/'or' without parentheses so precedence is ambiguous (e.g., ARCH 464 'ARCH 396 or ARCH 364 and ARCH 304 or ARCH 329 and ARCH 314 or ARCH 327 and ARCH 324' and ARCH 211 'ARCH 210 or ARCH 251 and ARCH 252'). Dangling GER/PHYS-lab references are minor.

missingTopics.topic: Building codes, life-safety, and accessibility/universal design
missingTopics.rationale: Professional Practice I/II (ARCH 472/475) cover project delivery and firm structure but no course explicitly teaches code compliance, egress/life-safety, or ADA/universal design, which are licensure (ARE) and practice essentials.

ECS I/II (ARCH 309/314) teach passive and active systems conceptually, but the plan has no course on quantitative energy/daylight modeling with contemporary simulation tools, now standard in high-performance design.

BIM (ARCH 337) and digital fabrication/tectonics (ARCH 301/335) exist only as electives while the sole required computing course is the dated CS 104; parametric modeling (Rhino/Grasshopper) is absent from the required spine.
Construction I/II (ARCH 223/224) survey wood/steel/masonry/concrete assemblies without addressing embodied carbon or whole-life LCA, now central to responsible materials selection.

missingTopics.topic: Construction cost, estimating, and project delivery methods
missingTopics.rationale: The professional-practice sequence omits construction economics, estimating, and modern delivery (design-build, IPD), leaving a business-of-building gap for graduates entering practice.

emergingTrends.rationale: Text-to-image and ML-driven design exploration are reshaping early-stage studio work; ARCH 110/156 Tools and Techniques and the studio sequence are the natural place to introduce responsible generative-AI workflows, replacing the aging CS 104 emphasis.

emergingTrends.trend: Decarbonization and net-zero / whole-life carbon design
emergingTrends.rationale: Codes and clients increasingly demand net-zero and embodied-carbon accounting; the ECS sequence (ARCH 309/314) and Construction courses should evolve toward carbon-quantified design.

emergingTrends.trend: BIM-based integrated delivery and digital twins
emergingTrends.rationale: Industry practice now assumes Revit-level BIM fluency and lifecycle digital twins; elevating ARCH 337 from elective to requirement positions graduates for contemporary coordinated project delivery.
Robotic assembly and CNC/3D-printed construction are moving from research to practice; ARCH 301/335 digital-fabrication content could seed a required maker/robotics component.

emergingTrends.trend: Mass timber and low-carbon structural systems
emergingTrends.rationale: Cross-laminated timber and hybrid systems are transforming mid-rise construction; Structures I/II (ARCH 303/304) and Construction I/II should add mass-timber design and detailing.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program learning outcomes mapped to the NAAB Student Criteria; they are absent from the catalog data and required for accreditation.
Require BIM (ARCH 337) and a building-performance/energy-simulation course, and modernize or replace the dated CS 104 with computational-design content.
Add a codes/life-safety/accessibility course and construction-cost/project-delivery content to close the biggest licensure and business-of-building gaps.
Integrate embodied-carbon/LCA and mass-timber design into Construction I/II (ARCH 223/224) and Structures I/II (ARCH 303/304).
Clean plan-of-study artifacts (the 'or ARCH 566' null-credit row, repeated 'ARCH Elective iii' labels, leaked contact-hour boilerplate) and resolve the 3 dangling references (GER 200/300, PHYS 102A).

bachelor-of-architecture-and-m-b-a-in-ma.name: Bachelor of Architecture and M.B.A. in Management of Technology

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The architecture spine is coherent (the rich sampled pool spans studios depth 0-7 plus Structures, ECS, and a strong technology-elective set: ARCH 335 Digital Tectonics, 337 BIM, 361 Adaptive Paradigms, 423 Advanced Construction, 429 Advanced Structures, 461 Resilient Structural Design). But the displayed plan mixes full architecture years with an unstated graduate M.B.A. block and truncates at 'Fourth Year 2nd Semester' (only 2 items, null credits), so the combined progression is not fully legible.
dimensions.suggestion: Present the complete integrated timeline including the graduate M.B.A.-MOT terms, and show where the technology-management electives (ARCH 335/337) bridge into the business curriculum.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The architecture prerequisite graph is sound (deepestPrereqChain 6), but this program carries 19 dangling references (missingCoursesSample includes ACCT 615, FIN 516/600/618/624, HRM 601, MGMT 640/645/680/692, ARCH 650/651/652). These are legitimately graduate business/architecture courses outside the undergraduate catalog, so the count is a combined-degree data caveat rather than a true structural defect, but it still hurts on-page navigability.
dimensions.suggestion: Cross-link the graduate M.B.A. and ARCH 6xx courses to the graduate catalog so the 19 references resolve instead of dangling, and resolve the GER 200/300 placeholders.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data; only the plan and course descriptions are present. For a design-plus-technology-management dual degree this is a real gap, since the differentiator is precisely the fusion of architectural and technology-management competencies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish combined learning outcomes covering NAAB design competencies and Management-of-Technology outcomes (innovation, entrepreneurship, tech strategy).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The metrics show summedTermCredits 108 against statedTotalCredits 36, a creditReconcileDelta of +72.0. The stated 36 evidently represents only the graduate M.B.A.-MOT overlay, so this large delta is a combined-degree data-consistency caveat rather than a defect, but as presented the page does not reconcile or show the true combined total.
dimensions.suggestion: State the full combined credit total and its split (B.Arch core vs. M.B.A.-MOT overlay) so the 36-credit figure is contextualized and the plan reconciles.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are labeled and most rows legible, but the final term is truncated to two courses with null credits, placeholder rows recur ('ARCH Elective iii'), and description excerpts leak contact-hour boilerplate (ARCH 196/296). The graduate M.B.A. portion is not depicted at all.
dimensions.suggestion: Complete the truncated fourth-year term, depict the graduate M.B.A.-MOT terms, and clean the placeholder and boilerplate rows.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: A B.Arch combined with an M.B.A. in Management of Technology is strongly relevant to design-technology entrepreneurship, firm innovation, and proptech leadership, and the elective pool already leans that way (AD 325 Entrepreneurship for Designers, ARCH 335 Digital Tectonics, 337 BIM, 361 Adaptive Paradigms). The main currency gap is that these technology bridges are elective, and the required computing course (CS 104) is dated.
dimensions.suggestion: Require a BIM/computational course and foreground AD 325 Entrepreneurship and ARCH 335/337 as explicit design-technology bridges into the M.B.A. block.

descriptionQuality.justification: The architecture and technology-elective descriptions are often rich and specific (ARCH 361 Adaptive Paradigms on kinetic/responsive architecture, ARCH 423 Advanced Construction, ARCH 429 Advanced Structures, ARCH 335 Digital Tectonics), but continuation studios (ARCH 196/296/396) are thin/boilerplate, AD 325 Entrepreneurship has an empty description, and no M.B.A. course descriptions are shown.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Clarity is materially reduced by 19 dangling references to graduate business/architecture courses (ACCT/FIN/HRM/MGMT, ARCH 650-652) that are not in the undergraduate catalog, plus unparenthesized compound architecture prereqs (ARCH 464, ARCH 483 'ARCH 224, ARCH 304, ARCH 314 or (ARCH 323, ARCH 304, ARCH 327)'). The dangling count is largely a combined-degree caveat, but on the page the prerequisites read as unresolved.

missingTopics.topic: Technology entrepreneurship and venture creation for design
missingTopics.rationale: AD 325 Entrepreneurship for Designers exists (and even lacks a description) but is not clearly required; a design-plus-MOT degree should make venture creation and tech-startup practice a core, well-documented component.
The plan lacks a course connecting building technology to the technology-management theme (construction platforms, proptech, digital delivery), the obvious intersection of this dual degree.
ARCH 335 Digital Tectonics and 337 BIM sit in electives while the only required computing is the dated CS 104; a technology-management architect should have required computational fluency.

missingTopics.topic: Innovation strategy and IP for the built environment
missingTopics.rationale: No course addresses managing innovation, intellectual property, or technology strategy specific to design/construction, a core Management-of-Technology competency.

missingTopics.topic: Product and service design / design thinking at scale
missingTopics.rationale: The technology-management pairing would benefit from design-thinking and product-management content linking architectural design methods to broader technology products.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI and computational design tooling
emergingTrends.rationale: AI-driven design and estimating are core to technology-managed practice; ARCH 335 Digital Tectonics and the studio sequence should incorporate generative workflows, replacing the aging CS 104 emphasis.

emergingTrends.trend: Proptech, digital twins, and smart buildings
emergingTrends.rationale: ARCH 361 Adaptive Paradigms and BIM (ARCH 337) point toward smart, sensor-driven buildings and digital twins that the MOT curriculum can frame as managed technology platforms.
AD 325 Entrepreneurship plus the technology electives position graduates to launch or lead construction-tech ventures, a fast-growing sector.
ARCH 423 Advanced Construction and digital-fabrication content set up a required maker/robotics component aligned with technology-management practice.
Management-of-Technology skills applied to project and portfolio analytics are increasingly decisive in design-firm competitiveness.

topSuggestions: State the true combined credit total and B.Arch/M.B.A. split so the +72 reconciliation gap (36 stated vs. 108 summed) is contextualized and the plan reads coherently.
Publish combined learning outcomes fusing NAAB design competencies with Management-of-Technology outcomes.
Require a BIM/computational-design course and make AD 325 Entrepreneurship and ARCH 335/337 explicit bridges into the M.B.A.-MOT block; modernize the dated CS 104.
Cross-link the 19 dangling graduate references (ACCT/FIN/HRM/MGMT, ARCH 650-652) to the graduate catalog and resolve GER 200/300.
Complete the truncated fourth-year term and clean thin/boilerplate descriptions (including the empty AD 325 entry).

bachelor-of-architecture-and-m-s-in-civi.name: Bachelor of Architecture and M.S. in Civil Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: Only two requirement groups are shown and they depict a graduate capstone year that is internally coherent (ARCH 601/602 Urban Design Studio, ARCH 636 History and Theory of Urban Planning and Design, ARCH 677 Geographic Information Systems, ARCH 684 Topics of Sustainable Urbanism, ARCH 651 Public and Private Development). However, the displayed graduate courses are urban-design titled rather than civil-engineering, and the undergraduate architecture foundation is not shown, so the combined-degree progression is both incomplete and possibly mislabeled.
dimensions.suggestion: Show the full B.Arch-to-M.S.-Civil-Engineering path and confirm that the displayed graduate courses match the civil-engineering track (they currently read as urban design).

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The underlying architecture prerequisite pool is deep and sensible (deepestPrereqChain 6, with strong structural electives ARCH 316 Structural BIM, 429 Advanced Structures, 461 Resilient Structural Design in the sample). The 9 dangling references (missingCoursesSample: ARCH 601/602/636/651/677/684 plus GER 200/300, PHYS 102A) are graduate courses and gen-ed placeholders outside the undergraduate catalog, so the count is a combined-degree data caveat rather than a defect.
dimensions.suggestion: Cross-link the six graduate ARCH 6xx courses to the graduate catalog and resolve GER 200/300 and PHYS 102A so the references stop dangling.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data; only the (partial) plan and course descriptions are present. For an architect-engineer dual credential the absence is significant because the value is the integration of design and structural/civil competencies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish combined learning outcomes spanning NAAB architectural competencies and M.S. Civil Engineering competencies (structural analysis, infrastructure, resilience).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The two displayed graduate terms reconcile internally (summedTermCredits 30 = statedTotalCredits 30, creditReconcileDelta 0.0), which is a positive, but 30 credits clearly represents only the graduate overlay and not the full combined program, so the page does not convey the true combined total or split.
dimensions.suggestion: State the full combined credit total (undergraduate B.Arch plus the 30-credit graduate overlay) and the split between the two degrees.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Only two terms are shown, both graduate, with the entire undergraduate plan absent, and the displayed courses (urban design) appear to mismatch the program title (Civil Engineering). Combined with an 'Urban Design Elective' placeholder row, the page is too partial and internally inconsistent to serve as a reliable catalog entry.
dimensions.suggestion: Correct or verify the graduate course listing against the M.S. Civil Engineering curriculum and present the complete term-by-term combined plan.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: A B.Arch combined with an M.S. in Civil Engineering is strongly relevant for architect-engineers working at the design-structure interface, and the underlying architecture pool already carries current structural/engineering content (ARCH 316 Structural Computer Applications BIM, 429 Advanced Structures, 461 Resilient Structural Design and Construction), while the shown graduate courses add GIS (ARCH 677) and sustainable urbanism (ARCH 684).
dimensions.suggestion: Require the structural bridge electives (ARCH 429 Advanced Structures, 461 Resilient Structural Design) in the combined path so every student enters the M.S. with a strong structural foundation.

descriptionQuality.justification: The architecture sample includes rich, specific descriptions (ARCH 316 Structural BIM, ARCH 361 Adaptive Paradigms, ARCH 337 Building Information Modeling, ARCH 335 Digital Tectonics), but the displayed graduate courses (ARCH 601/602/636/677/684/651) have no description excerpts on the page, and continuation studios (ARCH 196/296/396) remain thin/boilerplate.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Undergraduate architecture prerequisites are mostly explicit though some compound strings are unparenthesized (ARCH 464, ARCH 211). The 9 dangling references are graduate/gen-ed placeholders (a combined-degree caveat), and the shown graduate courses list no prerequisites at all, leaving the graduate-year dependencies unstated.

missingTopics.topic: Advanced structural analysis and design integration
missingTopics.rationale: ARCH 303/304 Structures I/II are static-analysis surveys and ARCH 429 Advanced Structures is elective; a civil-engineering combined degree should require deeper structural analysis (steel/concrete design, matrix/FE methods) in the undergraduate bridge.
ARCH 461 Resilient Structural Design addresses hazards as an elective, but performance-based and seismic design should be a required competency for an architect-engineer track.
Structures II (ARCH 304) touches foundations briefly; the plan lacks dedicated soils/foundation content essential to civil-engineering practice.
Construction I/II (ARCH 223/224) survey assemblies but not the materials-science depth (concrete mix design, steel behavior, durability) expected before graduate civil study.
ARCH 316 Structural Computer Applications BIM exists as an elective; making structural-BIM interoperability required would connect the architectural and civil sides of the dual degree.

emergingTrends.rationale: ARCH 461 Resilient Structural Design positions the program to lead on hazard-driven, performance-based engineering that defines modern structural practice.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital twins and BIM-based structural coordination
emergingTrends.rationale: ARCH 316 and ARCH 337 BIM set up structural digital twins and model-based coordination increasingly required on complex projects.

emergingTrends.trend: Mass timber and low-carbon structural systems
emergingTrends.rationale: Structures I/II and Construction I/II could add cross-laminated-timber and low-carbon structural design, a major shift in mid-rise construction engineering.
The graduate courses ARCH 684 Sustainable Urbanism and ARCH 677 GIS point toward resilient infrastructure and data-driven civil planning at the city scale.
Generative and optimization-driven structural design (topology optimization, parametric structures) is emerging; ARCH 429 Advanced Structures and BIM electives could incorporate it.

topSuggestions: Verify and correct the graduate course listing: the two shown terms read as urban-design courses (ARCH 601/602/636/677/684/651) yet the degree is M.S. Civil Engineering.
Present the complete combined plan and state the full credit total/split; only a 30-credit graduate overlay is currently shown.
Publish combined learning outcomes spanning NAAB architectural and civil-engineering competencies.
Require the structural bridge electives (ARCH 429 Advanced Structures, ARCH 461 Resilient Structural Design, ARCH 316 Structural BIM) and add geotechnical/materials depth in the undergraduate path.
Cross-link the 6 graduate ARCH 6xx references and resolve GER 200/300 and PHYS 102A so references stop dangling.

bachelor-of-architecture-and-m-s-in-mana.name: Bachelor of Architecture and M.S. in Management

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The underlying architecture sequence is coherent (the sampled course pool shows the full studio spine rising from depth 0 to 7 with Structures, ECS, and History in parallel), but the displayed plan of study is fragmentary: it begins mid-stream at a '2nd Semester' group, shows only 5 of the requirement groups, and never depicts where the M.S. in Management courses interleave with the B.Arch, so the combined-degree flow cannot be read as presented.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the complete integrated five-plus-one plan showing every term and the exact points where graduate management courses (management, finance, HRM) join the architecture spine.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The architecture prerequisite graph is deep and sensible (deepestPrereqChain 7, tracking the studio spine to ARCH 561 at depth 7), and only 3 references are dangling (missingCoursesSample: GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 102A), all gen-ed placeholders and a physics lab rather than architecture breaks. The graduate management courses are simply not referenced in this parse.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the GER 200/300 and PHYS 102A entries and cross-link the graduate management prerequisites so the combined path's dependencies are visible.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data; only the (partial) plan and course descriptions are present. For a dual professional-plus-management credential the absence is notable because the value proposition is precisely the integration of design and management competencies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish combined-degree learning outcomes articulating the design competencies (NAAB) and the management competencies (M.S.) and how they reinforce each other in practice.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The metrics show a large reconciliation gap: summedTermCredits 74 against statedTotalCredits 120 gives creditReconcileDelta -46.0. For a combined degree with a partial plan this is a data-consistency caveat rather than an outright defect, but as presented nearly half the credits are unaccounted for, so the reader cannot see how 120 is composed.
dimensions.suggestion: Show the full term-by-term plan so the summed credits reconcile to the stated total, and add a summary of the B.Arch vs. M.S. Management credit split.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms carry credits and the individual rows are legible, but the plan is visibly truncated (it opens at '2nd Semester' with the entire first year missing) and relies on repeated placeholder rows ('ARCH Elective', 'Free Elective', GER lines with null credits). The result reads as a fragment of the intended combined plan.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore the first-year and graduate-year terms and label each elective slot with its category and credit rule so the page is self-contained.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Pairing the professional B.Arch with an M.S. in Management is highly relevant to contemporary practice, where firm leadership, project management, and business operations are decisive; the architecture core already includes Professional Practice I/II (ARCH 472/475) that dovetails naturally with management study. Currency gaps mirror the base B.Arch (dated CS 104, BIM/performance simulation elective-only).
dimensions.suggestion: Surface the practice-management linkage explicitly by sequencing ARCH 472/475 immediately before the graduate management block and adding a construction-economics/real-estate bridge course.

descriptionQuality.justification: Architecture core descriptions are specific and informative (ARCH 303/304 Structures, ARCH 309/314 ECS, ARCH 316 Structural Computer Applications BIM, ARCH 301 Digital Modeling and Fabrication all give real content), but the continuation studios (ARCH 196/296/396) are thin or carry leaked contact-hour boilerplate, and no management course descriptions are shown.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Architecture prerequisites are mostly explicit but several compound strings are unparenthesized and ambiguous (ARCH 464, ARCH 211). Dangling references are limited to 3 gen-ed/lab placeholders, so clarity is only modestly reduced; the missing management-side prerequisites are absent rather than wrong.

missingTopics.topic: Construction economics, cost, and real-estate development
missingTopics.rationale: Professional Practice I/II (ARCH 472/475) cover delivery and firm structure but not estimating, pro-formas, or development finance, which is exactly the bridge this management combined degree should own.
Beyond ARCH 472/475 there is no dedicated course on running design projects (scheduling, staffing, fee structures), the practical core of pairing architecture with an M.S. in Management.

missingTopics.topic: Required BIM and digital project delivery
missingTopics.rationale: ARCH 337 BIM and ARCH 316 Structural BIM exist only as electives; BIM-based coordination is central to managing modern projects and should be required in a management-focused track.

missingTopics.topic: Sustainability and performance metrics for decision-making
missingTopics.rationale: ECS I/II (ARCH 309/314) teach systems but not the cost/carbon trade-off analysis a manager-architect needs; a performance-and-economics course would connect design to business decisions.
The plan lacks explicit coverage of contracts, insurance, and risk management, essential management competencies for architecture practice leadership.

emergingTrends.rationale: The M.S. Management pairing is ideally positioned to cover digital-project platforms and construction-tech adoption, extending ARCH 337 BIM and ARCH 301 fabrication toward technology management.

emergingTrends.trend: AI in design practice and business operations
emergingTrends.rationale: Generative design and AI-driven estimating/scheduling are reshaping firm operations; the combined degree should teach responsible AI adoption in both studio (ARCH 110/156) and management contexts.
IPD and coordinated BIM workflows are now standard on large projects; elevating ARCH 337 to required aligns the management track with how projects are actually delivered.

emergingTrends.trend: Decarbonization economics and ESG in the built environment
emergingTrends.rationale: Carbon accounting and ESG reporting are becoming business imperatives; linking ECS content (ARCH 309/314) with management coursework prepares graduates for sustainability-driven decisions.
Analytics for project performance, staffing, and portfolio decisions is a growth area the management degree can supply to design practice.

topSuggestions: Publish the complete integrated B.Arch + M.S. Management plan so all terms are shown and summedTermCredits reconcile to the stated 120 (currently a -46 gap).
Publish combined-degree learning outcomes tying NAAB design competencies to management competencies.
Add construction-economics/real-estate and project/firm-management courses that are the natural bridge of this dual degree.
Require BIM (ARCH 337) and sequence Professional Practice I/II (ARCH 472/475) directly into the graduate management block.
Resolve the 3 dangling references (GER 200/300, PHYS 102A) and restore the missing first-year and graduate terms so the plan is self-contained.

bachelor-of-architecture-and-m-u-d-176.name: Bachelor of Architecture and M.U.D

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The full professional B.Arch spine is present and well-ordered (ten requirement groups, studios ARCH 195 -> 595 with Structures, ECS, and History in parallel), and the sampled electives strongly support the Urban Design theme: ARCH 324 Landscape and Urbanism (required), plus ARCH 571 Sustainable City, 572 Mapping Urbanism, 574 Case Studies in Community and Urban Design, 576 Architecture of Utopia, and 557/559 housing seminars. The urban emphasis reads as an intentional overlay on a coherent base.
dimensions.suggestion: Formalize the urban electives into a declared, sequenced M.U.D track (ARCH 324 -> 571/572 -> 574) so the path into the graduate urban-design year is explicit.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The architecture prerequisite graph is deep and sensible (deepestPrereqChain 7 to ARCH 561), but the program carries 21 dangling references (missingCoursesSample includes ACCT 615, FIN 516/600/618, HRM 601, MATH 661, MGMT 620/625/630/635, ARCH 650/651/652). These are graduate-level courses outside the undergraduate catalog, so the high count is a combined-degree data caveat, but the sample notably lists management courses that look out of place for an urban-design degree and warrant verification.
dimensions.suggestion: Cross-link the graduate M.U.D courses (ARCH 6xx) to the graduate catalog, verify the management-titled references belong to this plan, and resolve GER 200/300.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data; only the plan and course descriptions are present. For a design-plus-urban-design credential this is a real gap, since the payoff is the integration of building-scale and city-scale competencies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish combined learning outcomes spanning NAAB building-design competencies and M.U.D urban-design competencies (urban analysis, public space, housing, sustainable urbanism).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The metrics show summedTermCredits 150 against statedTotalCredits 48, a creditReconcileDelta of +102.0. The stated 48 evidently captures only the graduate M.U.D overlay, so the large delta is a combined-degree data-consistency caveat rather than a defect, but the page does not reconcile or show the full combined total.
dimensions.suggestion: State the full combined total (~198) and its B.Arch-vs-M.U.D split so the 48-credit overlay is contextualized.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The ten-term B.Arch plan is complete and clearly labeled with per-term credits, and the urban electives carry substantive descriptions. Weaknesses are the recurring placeholder rows ('ARCH Elective iii', 'or ARCH 566' with null credits) and the fact that the graduate M.U.D year is not shown on the page.
dimensions.suggestion: Depict the graduate M.U.D terms and label each elective slot with its category and credit rule so the combined plan is self-contained.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: A B.Arch combined with an M.U.D is highly relevant to contemporary urban practice, and the elective pool is current: ARCH 571 Sustainable City, 572 Mapping Urbanism, and 574 Case Studies in Community and Urban Design address sustainability, urban data/representation, and real-world urban problems. The chief gap is that data/GIS-based urban analytics is not visibly required in the undergraduate portion.
dimensions.suggestion: Require a GIS/urban-analytics course (the sibling M.S. plan lists ARCH 677 Geographic Information Systems) in the combined path so every M.U.D-bound student gains spatial-data skills.

descriptionQuality.justification: The urban and history/theory electives have rich, specific descriptions (ARCH 572 Mapping Urbanism on city representation and information, ARCH 574 Case Studies, ARCH 557/559 housing, ARCH 535 History of Architectural Ideas, ARCH 571 Sustainable City), but the continuation studios (ARCH 196/296/396) are thin/boilerplate and ARCH 583 'ST:' is a placeholder stub.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Clarity is reduced by 21 dangling references to graduate courses (ARCH 6xx and management-titled entries) outside the undergraduate catalog, plus unparenthesized compound architecture prereqs (ARCH 464, ARCH 211). The dangling count is largely a combined-degree caveat, but the presence of seemingly mismatched management references makes the prerequisite picture harder to trust.

missingTopics.rationale: ARCH 572 Mapping Urbanism introduces urban representation but the undergraduate plan does not require GIS/spatial analysis (the M.S. sibling lists ARCH 677 GIS); data-driven urban analysis is now foundational to urban design.
The urban electives cover housing, landscape, and community design but not mobility networks or infrastructure systems, a core dimension of shaping cities.
ARCH 557/559 address housing socially and historically, but the plan lacks the economic/policy mechanics (financing, zoning incentives) an urban designer needs to act on affordability.

missingTopics.topic: Climate adaptation and resilient urban systems
missingTopics.rationale: ARCH 571 Sustainable City addresses sustainability broadly, but explicit climate-adaptation and flood/heat-resilience planning at the district scale is not represented.

missingTopics.topic: Community engagement and participatory design methods
missingTopics.rationale: ARCH 574 uses a case-study method, but structured participatory/co-design and equity-centered engagement methods are not a required competency.

emergingTrends.rationale: ARCH 572 Mapping Urbanism and the need for GIS point toward analytics-driven urban design (mobility data, urban simulation) that the combined degree should require and modernize.
ARCH 571 Sustainable City is a natural anchor for expanding into district-scale climate adaptation, green infrastructure, and flood/heat resilience.

emergingTrends.trend: Equity, environmental justice, and inclusive urbanism
emergingTrends.rationale: ARCH 557/559 housing seminars provide a base to foreground environmental justice and equitable development, now central to urban-design discourse.
Contemporary urbanism emphasizes proximity and transit-oriented development; ARCH 324 and the urban electives could evolve toward these frameworks.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital twins for cities and scenario planning
emergingTrends.rationale: City-scale digital twins and scenario modeling are emerging planning tools that extend the mapping/representation focus of ARCH 572 into predictive urban design.

topSuggestions: State the full combined B.Arch + M.U.D credit total and split so the +102 reconciliation gap (48 stated vs. 150 summed) is contextualized and the plan reconciles.
Publish combined learning outcomes spanning building-scale (NAAB) and city-scale (M.U.D) competencies.
Require a GIS/urban-analytics course (e.g., ARCH 677) and formalize the urban electives (ARCH 324 -> 571/572 -> 574) into a declared M.U.D track.
Add transportation/mobility, housing-economics, and climate-adaptation content to close the biggest urban-design coverage gaps.
Cross-link and verify the 21 dangling graduate references (ARCH 6xx and the management-titled entries), resolve GER 200/300, and clean thin/placeholder descriptions (ARCH 583 'ST:').

biological-sciences-minor-350.name: Biological Sciences Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: As a minor, no full plan of study is expected, and the underlying prerequisite ordering is sound (foundations BIOL 200 -> BIOL 201/202 and BIOL 205/206 gate the upper electives). But the page exposes essentially the entire biology major elective pool (63 referenced courses, from BIOL 280 Ecology to BIOL 436 Advanced Neuroscience Modeling) with no stated 'core plus N electives' structure, so what a minor must actually complete, and in what order, is not conveyed.
dimensions.suggestion: State the minor's required core (e.g., the BIOL 200/201/205 foundations) plus a defined number of upper-level electives so the progression is explicit rather than an open catalog.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is rich and well-formed (deepestPrereqChain=7), and the 9 dangling references (BIOL 202, BIOL 206, CHEM 125A, CHEM 126A, GER 200, GER 300, HUM 099, PHYS 102A, PHYS 103A) are co-requisite labs and GER/developmental placeholders, not real breaks. One structural implication worth noting for a minor: nearly every upper elective requires the full two-semester foundations (BIOL 201/202 and BIOL 205/206), so the minor is heavily front-gated.
dimensions.suggestion: Because most electives require the entire foundations sequence, publish the gateway courses prominently so minors budget for the prerequisite load before upper electives open up.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No learning outcomes or objectives for the minor appear in the data; only the course pool and prerequisite graph are present.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish concise minor-level outcomes stating the biological competencies a completer gains.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: There is no credit accounting: statedTotalCredits, summedTermCredits, and creditReconcileDelta are all null with requirementGroups=0. Null reconciliation is an expected data-consistency caveat for a minor, but the page still gives no total credit count or number of required courses, so a student cannot see the minor's size.
dimensions.suggestion: State the minor's total credit requirement and how many courses are required versus elective.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The entry presents a large, unstructured elective list with no required-course framing, no credit total, and no ordering, so despite good individual descriptions it reads as the major's catalog rather than a defined minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Reformat as a compact minor requirement block (core + elective menu + credit total) instead of the full major course pool.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The available electives are broad and current, including modern computational and molecular offerings: BIOL 432 Introduction to Computational Neuroscience, BIOL 436 Advanced Neuroscience Modeling, BIOL 470 Dynamic Principles in Systems Biology, and BIOL 453 Applied Genetics & Genomics, alongside disease ecology (BIOL 468). A minor built from these would expose students to contemporary biology; the limitation is that none of this is guaranteed by a defined requirement set.
dimensions.suggestion: Curate a recommended modern track (e.g., BIOL 470 Systems Biology + BIOL 453 Applied Genetics & Genomics) so minors can deliberately pick current, high-value courses.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are substantive across the pool (BIOL 280 Ecology, BIOL 432 Introduction to Computational Neuroscience, BIOL 470 Dynamic Principles in Systems Biology, BIOL 453 Applied Genetics & Genomics), with only isolated gaps such as the empty excerpt for BIOL 352 Genetics.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites carry explicit grade conditions but rely heavily on legacy dual-numbering ('BIOL 201 and BIOL 202 or R120 201 and R120 202'), and one record (BIOL 383) states its prerequisite inside the description with a null prerequisite field, which is confusing for a minor whose students may be outside the department.

missingTopics.rationale: No statistics course is guaranteed in the minor's pool, leaving completers without the quantitative literacy modern biology assumes.

missingTopics.topic: Molecular biology and genomics as a recommended path
missingTopics.rationale: BIOL 453 Applied Genetics & Genomics exists but is buried in an undifferentiated list; a minor should surface molecular/genomic content deliberately.
General microbiology is absent from the visible electives despite its centrality to health and biotechnology interests that draw students to a biology minor.
Computational modeling appears (BIOL 432/470) but no entry-level bioinformatics/programming course is offered within the minor's pool for non-majors.

emergingTrends.rationale: BIOL 432 Introduction to Computational Neuroscience and BIOL 470 Dynamic Principles in Systems Biology already position the minor to offer a computational-biology emphasis.
BIOL 453 Applied Genetics & Genomics can anchor a genome-editing/sequencing recommendation for minors from technical majors.
The deep neuroscience cluster (BIOL 432/436) reflects the field's shift toward quantitative, model-based neural analysis and is a strong draw for computing/engineering minors.
BIOL 468 Disease Ecology & Evolution offers a timely, cross-disciplinary elective linking environment and health.

topSuggestions: Define the minor's structure explicitly: required core courses, number of electives, and a total credit requirement (currently none of these are stated).
Publish minor-level learning outcomes.
Curate a recommended modern track from the existing pool (e.g., BIOL 470 Systems Biology + BIOL 453 Applied Genetics & Genomics) rather than presenting the full major catalog.
Surface the foundations gateway (BIOL 200/201/205) so minors understand the prerequisite load before upper electives open.
Clean the legacy dual-numbering and the malformed BIOL 383 prerequisite record for readability by non-majors.

biomedical-engineering-minor-esc-student.name: Biomedical Engineering – Minor, ESC Students

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No requirement groups are parsed (requirementGroups 0), so the minor has no structured plan of study, no declared required core and no elective rules in the data; the 29 referenced courses read as a pool. The pool itself is internally coherent as BME content (BME 111 Physiology, BME 210 Signal Processing, BME 301/302/303/304 fundamentals, BME 382/383 modeling/measurement) atop CHEM and MATH support, but it also includes BME 491 Research and Independent Study and the full BME 495/496 capstone sequence, which are atypical for a minor and suggest the major's course set was scraped wholesale rather than a curated minor pathway.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit minor structure for ESC students: a required BME foundation (e.g., BME 111 + BME 301 + one fundamentals course) plus a short curated elective list, and drop the research/capstone courses (BME 491/495/496) that belong to the major.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: Within the pool the prerequisite chains are well formed and deep (deepestPrereqChain 7) with clear grade conditions (e.g., BME 210 'MATH 112 or MATH 238 with a C or better'), but a depth-7 chain is heavy for a minor and reflects inherited major prerequisites that an ESC (Engineering Science) student outside the department may struggle to satisfy. missingCourseCount is 5, with a missingCoursesSample (BIOL 206, GER 200, GER 300, HUM 099, HUM 100) that is entirely general-education/placeholder rather than broken BME links.
dimensions.suggestion: Verify that the minor's chosen courses are reachable by ESC students without hidden major-only chains, and state any assumed prerequisites (e.g., which physiology/math background is presumed) explicitly on the page.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data. For a minor this is especially consequential, because the purpose of a minor is to certify a defined competency to students from another major (here, Engineering Science), and there is no statement of what BME competency the minor guarantees.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 learning outcomes describing what the BME minor certifies to ESC students (e.g., ability to model a physiological system, analyze biomedical signals, understand medical-device fundamentals).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: There is no credit information: statedTotalCredits is null, summedTermCredits is null and creditReconcileDelta is null. Per the rubric this null profile is a data-consistency caveat for a thin-data minor rather than proof of imbalance, but as presented a prospective student cannot see how many credits the minor requires or how they distribute across required and elective work.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total minor credit requirement (typically 15-18 credits) and list exactly which courses count and how many are required versus elective.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The captured entry is incomplete as a catalog page: no plan of study (requirementGroups 0), no credit total, no program description, and a course pool that blends genuine minor content with major-only research/capstone courses. A reader cannot determine what the minor actually consists of or how to complete it.
dimensions.suggestion: Render a clean minor page with a one-paragraph description, an explicit required/elective course list scoped to ESC students, and a stated total-credit figure.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The referenced pool contains current, employable BME content (BME 210 biological signal processing, BME 382 physiological modeling, BME 383 measurement lab, BME 301 electrical fundamentals), so a well-chosen minor could add real value to an ESC student's profile. The score is held at adequate because, absent a defined structure, there is no guarantee a minor student reaches these applied courses, and the pool shows the same ML/tissue-engineering currency gaps as the parent BME degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Curate a recommended modern mini-track (e.g., biosignals-and-devices) so ESC minors deliberately pick up in-demand skills rather than an unstructured subset.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 24 sampled courses have present, specific descriptions with none thin (e.g., BME 111 Physiology, BME 382 Engineering Models of Physiological Systems, CHEM 473 Biochemistry), and the text is substantive; only excerpt length is truncated in the capture.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites on the referenced courses are stated clearly with grade conditions and OR-alternatives, but the chains are deep (up to 7) and inherited from the major, and with no requirement structure they cannot be tied to an actual minor pathway; five general-education references (BIOL 206, GER 200/300, HUM 099/100) also remain dangling.

missingTopics.topic: A defined foundational core scoped to ESC students
missingTopics.rationale: The pool mixes minor-appropriate courses with major-only BME 491/495/496; the most important missing element is a stated, ESC-reachable required core (e.g., BME 111 + BME 301 + one fundamentals course).
BME 210 and BME 382 give the signals/modeling base, but as in the parent degree there is no data-analysis/ML course, the skill ESC students would most want to pair with their engineering background.

missingTopics.topic: Medical-device design and regulation for non-majors
missingTopics.rationale: The parent program's BME 472 FDA Regulation is absent from this pool; a minor-accessible devices/regulation course would give ESC students a distinctive, marketable competency.
BME 304 Materials Fundamentals appears, but a minor-scoped biomaterials elective would round out a recognized BME employment area for engineering-science students.

missingTopics.topic: Removing major-only research/capstone from the minor pool
missingTopics.rationale: BME 491/495/496 are full major requirements that inflate the pool's depth and mislead prospective minors; scoping them out is as important as adding topics.

emergingTrends.rationale: Pairing an ESC student's engineering skills with an accessible ML-for-biosignals option (building on BME 210) would position the minor for the fastest-growing BME hiring area.

emergingTrends.trend: Wearable sensors and continuous physiological monitoring
emergingTrends.rationale: BME 210 and BME 383 point toward wearable/point-of-care sensing, an attractive applied direction for engineering-science minors.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital health and connected medical devices
emergingTrends.rationale: A devices-and-connectivity elective would let ESC minors engage the software-and-regulation side of modern medical technology.
A BME minor is an ideal vehicle for ESC students to combine core engineering with biology; a defined bio-x track makes that combination legible.
BME 382 is a natural anchor to expose minors to patient-specific simulation, aligning with the digital-twin-of-the-patient trend.

topSuggestions: Publish the actual minor requirements and total credits, which are entirely absent (requirementGroups 0; statedTotalCredits, summed and delta all null).
Define a coherent, ESC-reachable structure (required BME foundation plus a curated elective list) and remove major-only courses (BME 491 research, BME 495/496 capstone) from the minor pool.
Publish 3-5 learning outcomes stating what the BME minor certifies to Engineering Science students.
